# THE BOOK OF PSALMS Vol. II


PREFACE

[The third collection of the Psalms begins with Psalm 73 and ends with Psalm 89. Bullinger calls this the "Levitical" book because it deals mainly with the "sanctuary," but I do not think this is too appropriate. The "sanctuary" originated in ]{.class_4938}[Exodus, ]{.class_4937}[not Leviticus, and there is more material on it in ]{.class_4938}[three ]{.class_4937}[chapters of Exodus than there is in ]{.class_4938}[fifteen ]{.class_4937}[chapters of Leviticus. Still, Bullinger insists that it is "emphatically the book of the sanctuary." To prove this, he sets up an outline where he uses the word "sanctuary" in thirteen lines. He could just as well have used the word "temple," and, as far as that goes, he could have outlined the book from another standpoint altogether.]{.class_4938}

The theme of the second book, which we just studied, was "the enemies of Israel," and that explains the dozens of passages in it on the Tribulation, the Antichrist, and the destruction of Israel's enemies at the Advent. You could connect it to Exodus if you recognized the plagues on Pharaoh as a type of those in Revelation 16, but most of Exodus (chaps. 16--40) deals with the Jews in the wilderness, facing no enemies but themselves. Amalek pops up one time (Exod. 17:8) and is gone. Psalms 73--89 do not differ much in content from the second book; it is one continual "harping" on the Tribulation, the Advent, and the Millennial reign. One whole Psalm describes Exodus to Joshua as a "parable," and one whole Psalm is a confirmation of the Davidic covenant of 2 Samuel 7.

[Psalms 84, 85, 87, and 88 are ]{.class_4938}["for the sons of Korah." ]{.class_4939}[The original ]{.class_4938}["Korah" ]{.class_4939}[was the leader of a rebellion in Numbers 16. His]{.class_4938}[ "sons,"]{.class_4939}[ however---at least some of them---did NOT go down into the pit when it opened (see Num. 26:11). This would mean that, at that time, these children were under the age of responsibility, as stated in Deuteronomy 1:39. The Korahites show up in Chronicles as ]{.class_4938}["keepers of the gates of the tabernacle" ]{.class_4939}[(1 Chron. 9:19) and ]{.class_4938}["keepers of the entry." ]{.class_4939}[Psalms 42, 44--49 were written for these ]{.class_4938}["sons of Korah."]{.class_4939}

[Psalm 73 is ascribed to Asaph, the ]{.class_4938}["son of Berachiah" ]{.class_4939}[(1 Chron. 6:39). He is a "singer" in 1 Chronicles 15:19 who handles cymbals. When David writes something, he often "delivers" it to Asaph (1 Chron. 16:7), and the reason for this is that Asaph does not just bang cymbals together; he is said to be able to ]{.class_4938}["prophesy with harps, with psalteries, and with cymbals," ]{.class_4939}[as well (1 Chron. 25:1). Psalm 50 is ]{.class_4938}["a Psalm of Asaph," ]{.class_4939}[and now his name appears on the first of eleven Psalms ascribed to him (Ps. 73--83). Since Asaph can ]{.class_4938}["prophesy,"]{.class_4939}[ he is called a]{.class_4938}[ "seer"]{.class_4939}[ in 2 Chronicles 29:30. There will be no doubt left about Asaph's ability to ]{.class_4938}[prophesy ]{.class_4937}[by the time we have plowed through Psalms 74, 75, and 76.]{.class_4938}

PSALM 73

73:1 "Truly God is good to Israel, even to such as are of a clean heart.

2 But as for me, my feet were almost gone; my steps had well nigh slipped.

3 For I was envious at the foolish, when I saw the prosperity of the wicked.

4 For there are no bands in their death: but their strength is firm.

5 They are not in trouble as other men; neither are they plagued like other men.

6 Therefore pride compasseth them about as a chain; violence covereth them as a garment.

7 Their eyes stand out with fatness: they have more than heart could wish.

8 They are corrupt, and speak wickedly concerning oppression: they speak loftily.

9 They set their mouth against the heavens, and their tongue walketh through the earth.

10 Therefore his people return hither: and waters of a full cup are wrung out to them.

11 And they say, How doth God know? and is there knowledge in the most High?"

[The theme of this Psalm, plainly, is the worldly prosperity of the wicked with which the saints must deal in this life; hence, the Psalm is loaded with ]{.class_4938}[devotional ]{.class_4937}[material. ]{.class_4938}[Doctrinally, ]{.class_4937}[the ]{.class_4938}["ungodly" ]{.class_4939}[of verse 12 (and the ]{.class_4938}["wicked," ]{.class_4939}[vs. 3) is a reference to the Antichrist's people in the Tribulation. This is apparent by their prophesied ]{.class_4938}["end"]{.class_4939}[ (vs. 17) which cannot be said of millions of ungodly people in the Church Age. But as we have said, the material is largely ]{.class_4938}[devotional, ]{.class_4937}[so Davidson, Briggs, Duhm, Ewald, Hupfeld, Baethgen, and Dummelow shouldn't have to tear it up so badly with "rathers" and "betters," and Motyer, Lange, Clarke, Ellicott, Kroll, Yates, Jamieson, Fausset, Brown, and other "common taters" shouldn't have to flip and flop, bluff and bluster, and blow and "side wind" to get through the Psalm.]{.class_4938}

"[Truly God is good to Israel\...." ]{.class_4939}[When? Between 1933 and 1945? ]{.class_4938}["Truly God is good to Israel, even to such as are of a clean heart" ]{.class_4939}[(see Ps. 24:4), but the man in the text had a clean heart, and God was "beatin' the stuffin' out of him" (vs. 14). You see, the Book is always deeper than it looks. Of course we can ]{.class_4938}[spiritualize. ]{.class_4937}[We can say that a true "Israelite" is one inside (Rom. 2:29), whose heart has been purified by faith (Acts 15:9). We note further that envy (vs. 3) came to Israel when they were provoked to jealousy ]{.class_4938}["by a foolish nation"]{.class_4939}[ (Rom. 10:19). ]{.class_4938}[Envy is uncleanness of heart. ]{.class_4937}[Asaph, in Christian vernacular, is about to "backslide" (or more properly, "get out of fellowship with God") for his feet and his "steps" are about to trip him up. His stumblingblock is envy---the root sin of Satan (Isa. 14:14); the sin that caused the murder of Jesus Christ (Mark 15:10).]{.class_4938}

[Envy is a major cause of backsliding. In the New Testament, it is ]{.class_4938}[the mark ]{.class_4937}[of the ]{.class_4938}["carnal" ]{.class_4939}[Christian (1 Cor. 3:3). The elder brother (whom Christ used to picture a law-abiding Pharisee; Luke 15:29) can't stand to see the publicans and harlots (typified by the younger) getting saved and celebrating. It eats his guts out. Envy caused the death of Abel, the murder of Naboth, and the attempted assassination of David (1 Sam. 18:10--11), as well as the damnation of the Jews who tried to stop the Gentiles from believing on Christ (Acts 13:45 and 1 Thess. 2:15--16). ]{.class_4938}["Who is able to stand before envy?" ]{.class_4939}[(Prov. 27:4). (Look at Rachel's case: Gen. 30:1.) Envy runs rampant among four classes of Americans: opera singers, movie stars, radio (or TV) announcers, and ]{.class_4938}[preachers. ]{.class_4937}[Any position that puts a man in the spotlight produces envy in sinners who want the spotlight. Examples are too numerous even to list. Many times the motive for building a church, building a youth camp, promoting a soulwinning campaign, or building a "record breaking Sunday School" is simply jealousy: someone else had won more souls, had a better attendance, had better facilities, etc.]{.class_4938}

[It is ENVY, pure envy, and nothing but envy, that supports the lifetime ministries of more than a thousand Bible-correcting scholars in America and England. T. Robertson, Hort, J. G. Machen, Trench, Schaff, Vincent, Thayer, Rendall, Ryle, Kutilek, Custer, Panosian, Wisdom, Bob Jones IV, Robert Sumner, Dell, Sherman, Martin, Farstad, Hodges, and Willmington. All mouth, no fruit. [Sometimes I have been envious of their ]{.class_4938}[corporations. ]{.class_4937}[I used to live in the middle of the PCC "campus"---they owned all the land around me on all four sides. 73:12 "Behold, these are the ungodly, who prosper in the world; they increase in riches.

13 Verily I have cleansed my heart in vain, and washed my hands in innocency.

14 For all the day long have I been plagued, and chastened every morning.

15 If I say, I will speak thus; behold, I should offend against the generation of thy children.

16 When I thought to know this, it was too painful for me;

17 Until I went into the sanctuary of God; then understood I their end.

18 Surely thou didst set them in slippery places: thou castedst them down into destruction.

19 How are they brought into desolation, as in a moment! they are utterly consumed with terrors.

20 As a dream when one awaketh; so, 0 Lord, when thou awakest, thou shalt despise their image."

Verse 12 is self explanatory; Job, James, and Matthew all deal with this in a Tribulation context where the "poor and needy" are constantly contrasted with the "rich and ungodly. "

"[I have cleansed\...washed\...been plagued\...chastened every morning."]{.class_4939}

J. Simpson, "The Rocket," et at.).]{.class_4938}

73:21 "Thus my heart was grieved, and I was pricked in my reins.

22 So foolish was I, and ignorant: I was as a beast before thee.

23 Nevertheless I am continually with thee: thou hast holden me by my right hand.

24 Thou shalt guide me with thy counsel, and afterward receive me to glory.

25 Whom have I in heaven but thee? and there is none upon earth that I desire beside thee.

26 My flesh and my heart faileth: but God is the strength of my heart, and my portion for ever.

27 For, lo, they that are far from thee shall perish: thou hast destroyed all them that go awhoring from thee.

28 But it is good for me to draw near to God: I have put my trust in the Lord GOD, that I may declare all thy works."

[A jealous man is ]{.class_4938}["foolish\...and ignorant" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 22). The trouble (as usual) is "heart trouble" (vs. 21). The thing that controls the man (]{.class_4938}["reins," ]{.class_4939}[see Ps. 7:9) is aggravated, so that he kicks ]{.class_4938}["against the pricks" ]{.class_4939}[(Acts 9:5). I think, "How is it that I cannot get a gospel tract in German, printed in full color, when it is only five inches by three inches, while on the newsstand, every month, there are five 90 page pornographic magazines (8x11 in.) in full color?" I think, "How is it that I cannot publish a book of 208 paintings on the book of Revelation---which has never been done on this earth---when Walden Books carry anywhere from ten to thirty books of paintings that deal with nothing but nature or humanism?" ]{.class_4938}["My heart was grieved." ]{.class_4939}[I see young men in my classes trying to "make ends meet"; some of them with two and three children to raise. One of them was a blind boy, and two students attended on crutches. Two of my students were soaked with hospital bills over \$12,000 when their salaries were ]{.class_4938}[less ]{.class_4937}[than five dollars an hour. I have had students who walked more than two miles to school---sometimes in the rain---and I have had students who literally ate nothing but peanut butter sandwiches for four months at a time; they couldn't afford anything else. ]{.class_4938}["Grieved\...so foolish was I, and ignorant." ]{.class_4939}[Hard to lay hold of, isn't it? I had one student whose wife died on him and left him with four children; to heat his small house, he had to cut an oil drum in half and burn charcoal in it. At one time, PCC tore down more than a dozen houses worth \$30,000 to \$70,000 apiece, and they simply hauled them off to make room for some pretty grass.]{.class_4938}

"[I was as a beast before thee." ]{.class_4939}[Let's see you explain it with any lexicon or dictionary or "Dead Sea Scrolls"!]{.class_4938}

[When we envy those who prosper in the world---especially those who compromise, lie, cheat, steal, or make a false profession to get wealthy---we tend to ]{.class_4938}[complain ]{.class_4937}[or ]{.class_4938}[worry. ]{.class_4937}[Both operations are from the Devil, according to 1 Corinthians 10:10. Wesley said he would just as soon get drunk as worry. Why?]{.class_4938}

"[I am continually with thee: thou hast holden me by my right hand. Thou shalt guide me with thy counsel and afterward receive me to glory." ]{.class_4939}[The pie IS in the sky "by and by" if you are going to do RIGHT (2 Tim. 3:12). If you are going to do ]{.class_4938}[wrong,]{.class_4937}[ you can get dessert NOW, and these days that is exactly what most Christian "celebrities" do. Their god is ]{.class_4938}[their ministry,]{.class_4937}[ so as all pragmatic humanists, they will "use" or "prefer" anything that will help their attendance, image, or enrollment: i.e., \$\$\$\$.]{.class_4938}

[Spurgeon can't stand the ]{.class_4938}[Holy Bible]{.class_4937}[ so he alters the word ]{.class_4938}["I"]{.class_4939}[ ]{.class_4937}[(vs. 23) to "he" (]{.class_4938}[Treasury,]{.class_4937}[ p. 265). The ]{.class_4938}[Living Bible ]{.class_4937}[alters it to "you." Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown alter it to "thee." Now you can take your pick, like any good humanistic pragmatist: "choose the alternative lifestyle that is meaningful to you in a pluralistic society." Take]{.class_4938}[ "I,"]{.class_4939}[ "he," "you," or "thee." How about "they," "we," "us," or "them"? Why stop when you have a good thing going?]{.class_4938}

[The Hebrew Masoretic text reads as the ]{.class_4938}[King James: ]{.class_4937}["I am continually\...."]{.class_4939}

Imagine! "Thou art continually with THEE," or "He art continually with THEE, " or "You art continually with YOU." There is nothing like a seminary education to make a perfect blockhead out of a nincompoop. I like the way Kyle Yates handles it: "he refused to parade his doubt lest he influenced others adversely\...God is his guide and strength\...the psalmist emphasizes the sense of God's nearness as he experiences it in his present circumstances." Ain't that a flip? How is that for "off the wall?" Try it this way: "The psalmist, realizing that God is God and can help folks out, decides to ask God to help him out so he won't have to help himself out." Nothing like a "deep Bible study," is there?

[The psalmist (if you want ]{.class_4938}[the truth ]{.class_4937}[of the matter) is still with God in ]{.class_4938}[spirit ]{.class_4937}[and ]{.class_4938}[mind, ]{.class_4937}[even when unable to understand the things described in the preceding verses. "I'm with YOU, Lord!" Watch Simon Peter expound the text: "I don't understand one cotton pickin' thing you said about eating blood, because that is a violation of the Law, and I don't even eat catfish because of Leviticus 10; but I'm with ya', Lord. I'm sticking with you (John 6:66--68). You can count on me! (Luke 22:33) ]{.class_4938}['I am continually with thee!']{.class_4939}["]{.class_4938}

"[Whom have I in heaven but thee?" ]{.class_4939}[Well, certainly not the ]{.class_4938}["Queen of Heaven" ]{.class_4937}[(Jer. 44:17--19, 25). When you have "flesh failure," as well as "heart failure" (vs. 26), there is nothing left but God. Verse 26 can apply to a New Testament Christian.

"[For, lo, they\...shall perish\...destroyed." ]{.class_4939}[Then ]{.class_4938}[not ]{.class_4937}[loving God is "spiritual whoremon--gering." Job said what was ]{.class_4938}["right" ]{.class_4939}[about God (Job 42:7), even though his buddies didn't agree (Job 32:3).]{.class_4938}

Gesenius, Hupfeld, Cheyne, and Bickell want verse 9 to say "from the heart their iniquity cometh forth," and verse 17 should have Asaph entering the "mysteries" instead of the sanctuary (DeWette, Ewald, Hitzig). PSALM 74

74:1 "O God, why hast thou cast us off for ever? why doth thine anger smoke against the sheep of thy pasture?

2 Remember thy congregation, which thou hast purchased of old; the rod of thine inheritance, which thou hast redeemed; this mount Zion, wherein thou hast dwelt.

3 Lift up thy feet unto the perpetual desolations; even all that the enemy hath done wickedly in the sanctuary.

4 Thine enemies roar in the midst of thy congregations; they set up their ensigns for signs.

5 A man was famous according as he had lifted up axes upon the thick trees.

6 But now they break down the carved work thereof at once with axes and hammers.

7 They have cast fire into thy sanctuary, they have defiled by casting down the dwelling place of thy name to the ground.

8 They said in their hearts, Let us destroy them together: they have burned up all the synagogues of God in the land."

[Nothing is difficult in the matter of placement for the Psalm; it is somewhere past ]{.class_4938}[1993]{.class_4937}[. Asaph is a ]{.class_4938}[prophet. ]{.class_4937}[Although ]{.class_4938}[historically ]{.class_4937}[you]{.class_4938}[ ]{.class_4937}[might place the Psalm on Nebuchadnezzar in 586 B.C., it would still come to nothing, because Asaph died at least three hundred years before Nebuchadnezzar was born. When the ]{.class_4938}[King James ]{.class_4937}[translators adopted the word ]{.class_4938}["synagogues" ]{.class_4939}[in verse 8, they blew every "good, godly, dedicated, recognized, qualified authority" into the Bermuda Triangle. V. Henderson, or Jerry Falwell would tolerate such a revelation; they removed it from the ]{.class_4938}[NKJV ]{.class_4937}[and left you with "Dead Duck Otherism"---a heresy taught at BJU.]{.class_4938}

"[O God, why hast thou cast us off for ever?" ]{.class_4939}[This is Lamentations 5:20--22, to the letter, which pictures what will take place very soon---if our calendar is correct. But ]{.class_4938}["God hath not cast away his people which he foreknew" ]{.class_4939}[(Rom. 11:2). The remnant (Joel 2:32) will be saved in its entirety (Rom. 11:26). Here, we find five similitudes of the church which do not apply ]{.class_4938}[doctrinally ]{.class_4937}[to the church, thereby leading every postmillennial and amillennial expositor on seven continents astray.]{.class_4938}

1\. Sheep (vs. 1).

2\. Congregation (vs. 2).

3\. A purchased possession (vs. 2).

4\. Inheritance (vs. 2).

5\. The redeemed (vs. 2).

[Doctrinally, ]{.class_4937}[the inheritance is that of Jeremiah 52:3, 52:13--15, and Deuteronomy 32:9, which places the passage right into Daniel's Seventieth Week where Kroll (Liberty University) can't find it (see below). Verse 3 should be compared to Isaiah 63:18.]{.class_4938}

[Asaph reaches out beyond Nebuchadnezzar and views the temple as rebuilt and burned (vs. 2) so that God no longer dwells in it, and the synagogues that took its place between 400 B.C. and A.D. 2002 are also burned to the ground. The "Champions for Christ," at Falwell's, can do nothing with the passage. The ravages of Nebuchadnezzar and Titus (before and after the birth of Christ) are only ]{.class_4938}[preludes to ]{.class_4937}[the opera. The opera will take place in the next few years, and there will not be one SYNAGOGUE---and I don't mean "meeting place"---left in the land of Israel.]{.class_4938}

"[The rod of thine inheritance." ]{.class_4939}[It began with a rod (see Exod. 4:2--4), and a shepherd's rod at that. The sheep are the sheep of Isaiah 63:11, and Jacob was the shepherd who used a rod to increase this flock (Gen. 30:37--42). ]{.class_4938}["Lift up thy feet." ]{.class_4939}[In the Bible, eyes, hands, and voices are "lifted" up. God is asked to lift up His feet, and He does (see Ps. 7:6 and 94:2). The idea is to take a step (or "kick"---see Ps. 60:8) in the right direction. The temple is desolated (vs. 3), so all of the scholars missed all the prophetic references; they are in Daniel 9:27, 11:31, and Matthew 24:15. The ]{.class_4938}[NIV ]{.class_4937}[(with its corrupt ancestors---the ]{.class_4938}[RV, ASV, NASV, RSV, ]{.class_4937}[and ]{.class_4938}[NRSV) ]{.class_4937}[took the word clean out (]{.class_4938}["desolations"]{.class_4939}[)]{.class_4938}[ ]{.class_4939}[so you would lose the revelation given in the chain references of 1611.]{.class_4938}

"[Thine enemies roar in the midst"]{.class_4939}[ of the ]{.class_4938}[congregation ]{.class_4937}[(singular, not plural), if you are stupid enough to correct the text like Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown. Having brought their readers down to their level of ignorance, they are aped by the translators of the ]{.class_4938}[NIV, ASV, RV, RSV, NASV, ]{.class_4937}[and the ]{.class_4938}[NRSV, ]{.class_4937}[who convert ]{.class_4938}["congregations" ]{.class_4939}[into the temple itself, or "the holy place." [Dead Duck Otherism. ]{.class_4937}[There is only ONE English Bible that reveals the truth; this means if you ever get the ]{.class_4938}[whole ]{.class_4937}[truth, you will be called a "heretic" by those who turn up their snotty noses at "King James Onlyism."]{.class_4938}

[The destruction of the temple---it takes place THREE times (Nebuchadnezzar, Titus, the Son of Perdition)---is described, although it had not been built when Asaph writes. The builders of the temple had hewn the trees in Lebanon (1 Kings 5:6--9), but now the hewers arrive who break down their work (Jer. 39:8). They set fire to the sanctuary (2 Kings 25:9) and level the temple (vs. 7). This did not happen literally until A.D. 70 (Matt. 24:2). Still, Kroll dumps it backwards five centuries. 74:9 "We see not our signs: there is no more any prophet: neither is there among us any that knoweth how long.

10 O God, how long shall the adversary reproach? shall the enemy blaspheme thy name for ever?

11 Why withdrawest thou thy hand, even thy right hand? pluck it out of thy bosom.

12 For God is my King of old, working salvation in the midst of the earth.

13 Thou didst divide the sea by thy strength: thou brakest the heads of the dragons in the waters.

14 Thou brakest the heads of leviathan in pieces, and gavest him to be meat to the people inhabiting the wilderness.

15 Thou didst cleave the fountain and the flood: thou driedst up mighty rivers.

16 The day is thine, the night also is thine: thou hast prepared the light and the sun.

17 Thou hast set all the borders of the earth: thou hast made summer and winter."

[The party is all over. Verse 9 locks the criminals in the slammer after safely "securing" their handcuffs and foot chains. When he says ]{.class_4938}["there is no more any prophet," ]{.class_4939}[he is talking for a pitiful handful of Jews in Sela, Petra (see Lam. 5:15--21) who are awaiting total annihilation, ]{.class_4938}[after ]{.class_4937}[the post-Tribulation rapture. ]{.class_4938}[ALL ]{.class_4937}[of the commentators---atheist, Fundamentalist, Catholic, Liberal, Conservative, Modernist, Neo Evangelical, Orthodox, and Jewish---miss ]{.class_4938}[all ]{.class_4937}[the references.]{.class_4938}

"[Why withdrawest thou thy hand\...?"]{.class_4939}[ Why have you quit dealing with us? ]{.class_4938}["Pluck it out of thy bosom." ]{.class_4939}[Then it must have been put there before it was "withdrawn." He is "sleeping"---although this is plainly ]{.class_4938}[figurative ]{.class_4937}[(see Ps. 121:4)---in the Church Age, as far as Israel is concerned. [We are approaching a catastrophe. Satan is about to appear, and you never knew a time in the history of the ]{.class_4938}[AV ]{.class_4937}[correctors where they didn't do everything in their power to cover up for him (see comments under Job 41 in that commentary) and get rid of anything that might reveal his character, work, methods, appearance, or ministry (see comments in Exod. 18 on Jethro's advice, in that commentary). The ]{.class_4938}["King of old" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 12) has to be a reference to the beginning of the theocracy when ]{.class_4938}["he was king in Jeshurun" ]{.class_4939}[(Deut. 33:5) and had ]{.class_4938}["armies"]{.class_4939}[ (Exod. 6:26, 7:4) to fight for a ]{.class_4938}["nation" ]{.class_4939}[(Gen. 18:18). 74:18 "Remember this, that the enemy hath reproached, O LORD, and that the foolish people have blasphemed thy name.

19 O deliver not the soul of thy turtledove unto the multitude of the wicked: forget not the congregation of thy poor for ever.

20 Have respect unto the covenant: for the dark places of the earth are full of the habitations of cruelty.

21 O let not the oppressed return ashamed: let the poor and needy praise thy name.

22 Arise, O God, plead thine own cause: remember how the foolish man reproacheth thee daily.

23 Forget not the voice of thine enemies: the tumult of those that rise up against thee increaseth continually."

[The enemy in the passage (called ]{.class_4938}["the foolish people"]{.class_4939}[) are the ones found in Daniel 11:30--32; Revelation 13:3--8; and Zechariah 14:1--3. God's people, at this time, have been reduced to ]{.class_4938}["the congregation of thy poor" ]{.class_4939}[(see Ps. 9:18, 10:2, and comments). They are the ]{.class_4938}["poor and needy" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 21) of nearly every Psalm in the Psalter. These words never refer to the Church Age one time, and the only time that they refer to the Ark is at the installation of the Son of David's "millennial kingdom" (2 Chron. 6:41); Solomon's reign is the clearest type of the Millennium in the entire Old Testament.]{.class_4938}

[1. ]{.class_4938}["Arise, O God" ]{.class_4939}[(Ps. 3:7). What for? To smite Israel's enemies.]{.class_4938}

[2. ]{.class_4938}["Arise, O God" ]{.class_4939}[(Ps. 10:12). What for? To save them from the Antichrist (10: 13).]{.class_4938}

[3. ]{.class_4938}["Arise, O God" ]{.class_4939}[(Ps. 82:8). What for? To ]{.class_4938}["judge the earth."]{.class_4939}

"[The voice of thine enemies\...the tumult of those that rise up against thee increaseth continually" ]{.class_4939}[(see Ps. 8:2, 18:37, 21:8, 27:2, 37:20, 7:6, 60:12, 66:3) will double and triple in the next three years as the United States and Europe (led by the Pope) will turn anti Semitic and join Arabia, Edom, Moab (see Ps. 83:5--9), Asia, Africa, and Russia against the Israel of God and the God of Israel. PSALM 75

75:1 "Unto thee, O God, do we give thanks, unto thee do we give thanks: for that thy name is near thy wondrous works declare.

2 When I shall receive the congregation I will judge uprightly.

3 The earth and all the inhabitants thereof are dissolved: I bear up the pillars of it. Selah.

4 1 said unto the fools, Deal not foolishly: and to the wicked, Lift not up the horn:

5 Lift not up your horn on high: speak not with a stiff neck.

6 For promotion cometh neither from the east, nor from the west, nor from the south.

7 But God is the judge: he putteth down one, and setteth up another.

8 For in the hand of the LORD there is a cup, and the wine is red; it is full of mixture; and he poureth out of the same: but the dregs thereof, all the wicked of the earth shall wring them out, and drink them.

9 But I will declare for ever; I will sing praises to the God of Jacob.

10 All the horns of the wicked also will I cut off, but the horns of the righteous shall be exalted."

[The entire Psalm is connected with Israel and the Second Advent. Jesus Christ Himself is speaking in the first person in verses 2--6. David speaks for himself and Christ in verses 8--9, but it is Christ alone, again, in verse 10 who cuts off the ]{.class_4938}["horns of the wicked." ]{.class_4939}[The Jewish signs to Israel for the Second Advent (see Matt. 24; Mark 13; and Luke 17) are mentioned in verse 1. Nothing is difficult about anything in the entire Psalm, but when our good "godly" friends---who have spent their lives trying to destroy the revelations of the 1611 English Bible---hit verses 5--6, you can expect a scene that will resemble a ton of spaghetti going through a shredder.]{.class_4938}

"[Unto thee do we give thanks" ]{.class_4939}[is repeated in verse 1.]{.class_4938}

1\. Giving thanks to God is one thing that cannot be repeated too often.

2\. Repetition is necessary in case you didn't get a message the first time (World War II cadreman, "There's always some jerk that doesn't get the message.").

3\. Repetition is good in case you don't feel like doing it the first time you hear it.

4\. And in case you might have thought it was not necessary, see Philippians 3:1.

[We may say that ]{.class_4938}["thy name is near" ]{.class_4939}[in the sense of Romans 10:8, because any sinner has already been granted enough "repentance" and enough "sovereign grace" to confess Jesus Christ. Of verse 2 we ask, ]{.class_4938}[devotionally: ]{.class_4937}["Will YOU judge uprightly if God puts you into a place of spiritual rulership (1 Tim. 5:17; Heb. 13:17) where judgment is required (1 Cor. 6:4--5)?"]{.class_4938}

[Verse 4, the only way ]{.class_4938}["fools" ]{.class_4939}[can ]{.class_4938}["deal" ]{.class_4939}[is ]{.class_4938}["foolishly."]{.class_4939}[ A man is not a thief because he steals; he steals because he is a thief.]{.class_4938}

We may say of verses 6 and 7 that God promotes whom He will (see Rom. 13:1--5; Dan. 4:17; and John 19:11). Nebuchadnezzar and Pilate, as well as Hitler and Stalin, get their power to rule, destroy, pillage, and kill from God (see Ps. 62:11). Our popular expression, from verse 7 is "He sure put him down!" Or "She really put 'em down."

[The ]{.class_4938}["horns" ]{.class_4939}[of a wicked man can most easily be ]{.class_4938}["cut off"]{.class_4939}[ when he sticks them up too high. The ]{.class_4938}["horn"]{.class_4939}[ stands for ]{.class_4938}[power, ]{.class_4937}[not only in the Scriptures (see Gen. 22:13 for the first mention; then Exod. 27:2; Dan. 7:7--8, 20; 8:3, 6--7, 20; Amos 6:13; Hab. 3:4; Zech. 1:18, etc.), but also in the world. The Indians mounted buffalo horns on their headdresses; the Vikings mounted horns on their helmets; and they are prominently displayed on the walls of American males from Maine to San Diego and from Seattle to Jacksonville (elk, moose, deer, antelope, rams, etc.). It is a ram's HORN that is used to call men into battle (Josh. 6:8).]{.class_4938}

[Now we shall examine the Psalm ]{.class_4938}[doctrinally, ]{.class_4937}[for that is the first and primary reason why the Scriptures were ]{.class_4938}["given by inspiration" ]{.class_4939}[(see 2 Tim. 3:16). We can expect a catastrophe at verses 6 and 7, so you may as well "buckle up." At that intersection, there will be a ten car pile up that will keep traffic blocked on the Interstate for at least fifty minutes. ]{.class_4938}["For that thy name is near."]{.class_4939}[ This]{.class_4938}[ "name" ]{.class_4939}[is the one found in Luke 1:30--33, and it is ]{.class_4938}["near"]{.class_4939}[ at the end of Daniel's Seventieth Week because the ]{.class_4938}["congregation" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 2) is about to receive the perfect King (see 2 Sam. 23:3). ]{.class_4938}["The earth and all the inhabitants" ]{.class_4939}[are in rough shape after Armageddon (see Isa. 2:21, 24:1--5; and Joel 1:2--12).]{.class_4938}

"[Selah" ]{.class_4939}[warns the revisors, translators, commentators, and Bible "scholars," again, that they had better start studying the Bible instead of wasting time with "plenary, verbally inspired" spooks. The earth and its populace will look like Judges 16:30 after Samson pulled down the ]{.class_4938}["pillars."]{.class_4939}[ The ]{.class_4938}["pillars" ]{.class_4939}[are the supports for anything. In Galatians 2:9, they are the "mainstays" of the church at Jerusalem (Gal. 2:2, 9). The reference here is to 1 Sam. 2:8, which again lands the reader right "smack dab" onto the Second Advent. Two of the pillars in Solomon's temple are named in 1 Kings 7:21--22. ]{.class_4938}[Heaven is ]{.class_4937}[said to have ]{.class_4938}["pillars" ]{.class_4939}[(Job 26:11). Plainly, the references are ]{.class_4938}[figurative ]{.class_4937}[and have to do with whatever sustains the heavens and whatever keeps the earth from sinking into the oceans. Atlas won't do the job. The ]{.class_4938}["it"]{.class_4939}[ in verse 3 is a reference to the fact that the earth is still there at the Advent; ]{.class_4938}[it is destroyed at the end of the Millennium.]{.class_4937}

[Back in 1990, we had a converted Jew named Rosenthal who sidetracked several hundred Christians from waiting for the Lord to show up to waiting for the Antichrist to show up. He did this by claiming that the "Day of the Lord" was a twenty-one month period that occurred ]{.class_4938}[before ]{.class_4937}[the Millennium started. This forced him to cite 2 Peter 3:10--13 and make it take place at the ]{.class_4938}[beginning ]{.class_4937}[of the Millennium. No such thing, of course, happens until Revelation 20, after the Millennium is OVER. Such are the ways of those who say "the translation in our ]{.class_4938}[Authorized Version is ]{.class_4937}[misleading," or "a better translation should be," or "the word should properly be rendered as\..."---as what I ]{.class_4938}[want ]{.class_4937}[it to say instead of what it ]{.class_4938}[says. ]{.class_4937}[The Nicolaitans are as easy to read as a highway stop sign at twenty feet. Isaiah 13:13 and 14 is the doctrinal ftilfillment of Psalm 75:3, so you know what happened. Kroll, Motyer, Spurgeon, Dummelow, Scofield, Bullinger, Hengstenberg, Duhm, Baethgen, Davidson, Briggs, Hupfeld, Ewald, DeWette, and Hitzig missed it like Cain's descendants "missed the boat."]{.class_4938}

"[Speak not with a stiff neck" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 5) Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown convert it to an "uplifted neck" because they never preached a day in their life. The dead orthodox apostates who put out the Nutty Idiot's Version (]{.class_4938}[NIV]{.class_4937}[) say "outstretched neck"---since none of them could preach a Girl Scout under conviction, and then Kroll tells us that what it means (in Lynchburg) is that the fool is not to "flex his muscles in the face of God." (Or "in the original" it says, "Don't bench press more than 500 pounds if you can't dead lift 250.") The ]{.class_4938}[Living Bible ]{.class_4937}[warns you to "lower your insolent gaze." (Or "in the original" text: "Sing in the lower register without blinking your eyes.")]{.class_4938}

[The ]{.class_4938}[AV ]{.class_4937}[English already interpreted the text ]{.class_4938}[nine times ]{.class_4937}[before Psalm 75 showed up; with the first reference clear back in Exodus 32:9. The ]{.class_4938}["stiff neck" ]{.class_4939}[is the result of resisting pressure, so the most stiff necked people you will see today are football linemen. Ninety percent of the blows received in football are received by someone butting their head into you, or you butting your head into someone else. Most tackles are made with the head down, and every runner and "receiver" is told to lower his head when he is about to run into a tackler. This produces "stiff necks" between 17 and 20 inches in diameter. From the standpoint of revelation---which all of the commentators missed (naturally)---the ]{.class_4938}["stiff neck"]{.class_4939}[ is what ruins a preacher's vocal chords. PSALM 76

76:1 "In Judah is God known: his name is great in Israel.

2 In Salem also is his tabernacle, and his dwelling place in Zion.

3 There brake he the arrows of the bow, the shield, and the sword, and the battle. Selah.

4 Thou art more glorious and excellent than the mountains of prey.

5 The stouthearted are spoiled, they have slept their sleep: and none of the men of might have found their hands.

6 At thy rebuke, O God of Jacob, both the chariot and horse are cast into a dead sleep."

[The Psalm is on the Second Advent. God is not known in Judah now, nor has His ]{.class_4938}["name" ]{.class_4939}[been ]{.class_4938}["great in Israel" ]{.class_4939}[for 1,950 years. The name "Jehovah" may be great in Israel now, but if we are to believe Deuteronomy 32:15--22; Mark 7:3--13; and Romans 10:1--3, the name is only ]{.class_4938}[lip service. ]{.class_4937}[God is NOT known, and will not be "known in Judah and Israel" until Hebrews 8:8--12. In this age, God is known, and His ]{.class_4938}["name is great," ]{.class_4939}[among the Gentiles (Acts 28:28), but only because of the Jew (]{.class_4938}["Judah"]{.class_4939}[). The term "Jew" was given to Judean Jews in Jerusalem (John 5:16, 18), and inspite of tons of anti-Semitic hogwash about "Khazars," "Edomite usurpers," and the "ten lost tribes," salvation is still of the ]{.class_4938}["Jews," ]{.class_4939}[and I don't mean "Israel" or "the Israel of God" or the "house of Israel." I mean "Jews," as in Judean Jews from Judah (vs. 1).]{.class_4938}

"[In Salem." ]{.class_4939}[The word is kin to "Shalom" and "Shiloh," meaning "peace." God's dwelling place in this age is NOT in Zion, and He has no tabernacle there; Asaph is prophesying (see the introductory notes). Those who tried to historicize verse 2, and lay it on David or Solomon, have two problems. In the first place, wars do NOT stop in David's time, nor do they stop in Solomon's time. The breaking up of weapons in verse 3 is punctuated by our good, old friend ]{.class_4938}["Selah," ]{.class_4939}[showing anyone but a Bible-correcting Hebrew scholar that we are to look at Haggai 2:9 and Zechariah 14 for the meaning of the verse. The word ]{.class_4938}[Jerusalem ]{.class_4937}[means "city of peace," so:]{.class_4938}

1\. It is captured by the Jews in Judges 1:8, but they have to fight against it again to retake it in 2 Samuel 5:6--10.

[2. ]{.class_4938}[Shishak ]{.class_4937}[attacks it in 2 Chronicles 12:9.]{.class_4938}

[3. ]{.class_4938}[Jehoash ]{.class_4937}[goes after it in 2 Kings 14:13, ]{.class_4938}[Rezin ]{.class_4937}[in 2 Kings 16:5, and ]{.class_4938}[Sennacherib ]{.class_4937}[in Isaiah 36 and 37.]{.class_4938}

[Nebuchadnezzar ]{.class_4937}[goes up to it three times; ]{.class_4938}[Ptolemy Soter ]{.class_4937}[attacks it in 320 B.C., ]{.class_4938}[Antiochus ]{.class_4937}[defiles it in 203 B.C., ]{.class_4938}[Scopus ]{.class_4937}[attacks it in 199 B.C., and ]{.class_4938}[Antiochus ]{.class_4937}[hits it again in 168 B.C., and then again "for good measure" in 162 B.C.]{.class_4938}

"[City of Peace." Fantastic, isn't it? Imagine the commentators thinking that God stopped all the wars with the destruction of David's foes or Solomon's or even Hezekiah's foes (Sennacherib)! Kroll is as tongue tied when he stares at the passage as a calf looking at a "new gate." He doesn't know what on earth to do with it.]{.class_4938}

[Hyracannus ]{.class_4937}[attacks Jerusalem in 65 B.C. ]{.class_4938}[Pompey ]{.class_4937}[follows suit in 63 B. C. ]{.class_4938}[Herod ]{.class_4937}[does a bang up job in 39 B.C., and then ]{.class_4938}[Titus ]{.class_4937}[finishes it off in A.D. 70. But the best is yet to come. ]{.class_4938}[Chosroes ]{.class_4937}[the Persian attacks it in A.D. 559 after the Romans did it in A.D. 135. Then ]{.class_4938}[Afdal ]{.class_4937}[takes it in A.D. 1098, after ]{.class_4938}[Omar ]{.class_4937}[destroyed it in A.D. 637. Then the ]{.class_4938}[Crusaders ]{.class_4937}[take it (A.D. 1099) only to lose it to ]{.class_4938}[Saladin ]{.class_4937}[(A.D. 1187). Never fear. ]{.class_4938}[Allenby ]{.class_4937}["liberated" it in A.D. 1917, and then the Arabs took over fighting against it with Lebanese, Egyptians, the PLO, the Pope, and the American news media to help them out.]{.class_4938}

"[City of Peace." ]{.class_4938}["On earth peace, good will toward men" ]{.class_4939}[(Luke 2:14). There will be no peace on this earth until the King of Peace judges this earth from Jerusalm. Until then, the forecast is "bigger and better wars" and more bloodletting---by the barrel. There have been more people killed in wars since 1914 (that would be 77 years) than were killed in all the American and European wars fought between 300 B.C. and A.D. 1914.]{.class_4938}

"[Thou art more\...excellent than the mountains of prey."]{.class_4939}[ The ]{.class_4938}["thou" ]{.class_4939}[is Mt. Zion (see Ps. 68:15 and comments). Two thoughts are present. Mountains which have wild animals on them who take ]{.class_4938}["prey"]{.class_4939}[ (Song of Sol. 4:8), are not to be compared with a mountain like Mt. Zion, which not only had the temple on the place where God ordained sacrifices to be made, but also was the location of the Ark of the Covenant which held the Book (Deut. 31:26). The ]{.class_4938}["oracle" ]{.class_4939}[of God (1 Kings 6:5) was located on Mt. Zion. The second thought is that the earthly powers represented by mountains (see Rev. 17, for example, and Jeremiah 51:25) are no equal for the "mountain of the Lord" (see Isa. 2:2). He is "king of the mountain," and His "mountain" will tower above McKinley, Blanc, Whitney, Ararat, Everest, etc., for its prototype (see Heb. 12:22) is already a good bit higher than the entire Milky Way.]{.class_4938}

"[Slept their sleep\...into a dead sleep." ]{.class_4939}[Literally, ]{.class_4937}[in the sense of Saul (1 Sam. 26:12), who is a type of the Antichrist; ]{.class_4938}[doctrinally, ]{.class_4937}[in the sense of being dead (see Isa. 26:14; Dan. 12:2). One of them is that the Antichrist's troops will kill each other (Judg. 7:22), their flesh will rot on their faces (Zech. 14:12), and their horses will go blind and rabid in the midst of the attack (Zech. 12:4). These are the UN troops (Rev. 19:19) who hope to overthrow the rider on the "white horse" (Rev. 19:10--14). Observe the advanced revelation found in the ]{.class_4938}[AV ]{.class_4937}[text which all of the commentators (naturally) missed. When Kroll sees that the]{.class_4938}[ "chariot"]{.class_4939}[ goes into a ]{.class_4938}[dead sleep, ]{.class_4937}[as well as the "horses" he mumbles, "the cavalries of the oppressors were stopped." The ]{.class_4938}[RSV, NRSV, NNRSV ]{.class_4937}[(and ]{.class_4938}[NNNRSV]{.class_4937}[) take the word ]{.class_4938}["chariot" ]{.class_4939}[clean out of the text so they don't have to deal with the problem: "Both rider and horse lay stunned." [But after 1910, the ]{.class_4938}["chariot" ]{.class_4939}[could go to ]{.class_4938}["sleep." ]{.class_4939}[What auto mechanic doesn't know that? As a matter of fact, the motor can even "die," and what driver didn't know that? If it is "awake" but not moving, it is "idling," and who didn't know that but the commentators who couldn't imagine chariots going to sleep? They went to sleep on the Russian front (1942--1944) because the tank fluids froze, or the rats shortcircuited the controls by chewing through the insulation. In my generation (1921--1949), we called ]{.class_4938}[automobiles ]{.class_4937}[by a peculiar name; we called them "chariots." A chariot can "malfunction."]{.class_4938}

76:7 "Thou, even thou, art to be feared: and who may stand in thy sight when once thou art angry?

8 Thou didst cause judgment to be heard from heaven; the earth feared, and was still,

PSALM 77

77:1 "I cried unto God with my voice, even unto God with my voice; and he gave ear unto me.

2 In the day of my trouble I sought the Lord: my sore ran in the night, and ceased not: my soul refused to be comforted.

3 I remembered God, and was troubled: I complained, and my spirit was overwhelmed. Selah.

4 Thou holdest mine eyes waking: I am so troubled that I cannot speak.

5 I have considered the days of old, the years of ancient times.

6 I call to remembrance my song in the night: I commune with mine own heart: and my spirit made diligent search."

[The Psalm continues the refrain of the two previous Psalms, although it tones down the main subject of the Second Coming of Christ. This is "camouflaged" in the first twelve verses as a personal meditation by an individual about his own condition. The reader will note that once the Psalmist has said, ]{.class_4938}["I will remember the years" ]{.class_4939}[and ]{.class_4938}["I will remember the works" ]{.class_4939}[and ]{.class_4938}["I will remember thy wonders" ]{.class_4939}[(vss. 10--11) that there follows a review of those years, works, and wonders in the next Psalm (78).]{.class_4938}

Everything in the first twelve verses is transparent. If any commentator had ever been in any real kind of trouble, he would have no problem writing two paragraphs on every verse in the passage. Those of us who have been "through many dangers, toils, and snares" in the ministry don't have to consult a "newer version," a Greek LXX, a Hebrew lexicon, an Aramaic dictionary, or a Hebrew teacher to handle the verses.

Verse 1 is self explanatory (see Ps. 5:1, 17:1, and comments).

[Verse 2 needs little explanation. The ]{.class_4938}["sore"]{.class_4939}[ that ]{.class_4938}["ran in the night" ]{.class_4939}[is from the ]{.class_4938}["loathsome disease"]{.class_4939}[ mentioned in Psalm 38:7, which see. In ]{.class_4938}[application, ]{.class_4937}[there are thousands of saints whose "bed sores" and "running sores" kept them in misery weeks at a time. When the "Champions for Christ" at Lynchburg face this common place truth of life, they "head for the hills." Kroll says, "It appears this clause is better translated\...." (Ho hum; so what's new? ]{.class_4938}[Time ]{.class_4937}[magazine: "God is dead." Oral Roberts: "God is not dead." God: "Who is Oral Roberts?") Well, who is "Kroll" but one more link coming out of a baloney sausage factory that has been putting out destructive critics for one hundred years. "My hand was stretched out in the night," says Kroll, aping the Reversed Vision (]{.class_4938}[RV]{.class_4937}[), the Nutty Retarded Vision (]{.class_4938}[RSV]{.class_4937}[), the Living Blockheads with the Nicolaitans Imitation Version (]{.class_4938}[NIV]{.class_4937}[),]{.class_4938}[ ]{.class_4937}[and the usual: ]{.class_4938}[ASV, NASV, NKJV, ]{.class_4937}[etc.]{.class_4938}

[If you have a running sore, you will find your case documented in ]{.class_4938}[one ]{.class_4937}[Bible and one Bible only. It is not in any English Bible, but an ]{.class_4938}[Authorized Version. ]{.class_4937}["Dead Duck Otherism" fails to mention it. Like 2 Timothy 2:15; 1 Thessalonians 5:23; and 2 Corinthians 2:17, there remains only "King James Onlyism" to give you the whole truth; any other version will LIE to you just as quickly as Bob Jones III, Bob Jones IV, or Bob Jones V. ]{.class_4938}["Dead Duck Otherism."]{.class_4937}

[The reading of the ]{.class_4938}[ASV, NASV, NKJV, RSV, ]{.class_4937}[and ]{.class_4938}[NRSV ]{.class_4937}[comes from spiritualizing]{.class_4938}[ "the sore." ]{.class_4939}[That is, the ]{.class_4938}["sore" ]{.class_4939}[is not a literal sore, even though the Hebrew word is describing something "flowing out." This is altered to "stretched out" to make the verse figurative. Leaving the text as it stands, we have an "issue of blood" (Lev. 12:7) or pus (Lev. 15:2) that will not dry up. It is a dripping infection coming from a limb; in this case, probably from the hands.]{.class_4938}

[1. ]{.class_4938}["I sought the Lord" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 2).]{.class_4938}

[2. ]{.class_4938}["I remembered God" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 3).]{.class_4938}

[3. ]{.class_4938}["I complained" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 3).]{.class_4938}

[4. ]{.class_4938}["My spirit was overwhelmed" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 3).]{.class_4938}

[5. ]{.class_4938}["I am\...troubled" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 4).]{.class_4938}

[6. ]{.class_4938}["I have considered" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 5).]{.class_4938}

[7. ]{.class_4938}["I call to remembrance" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 6).]{.class_4938}

[8. ]{.class_4938}["I commune with mine own heart" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 6).]{.class_4938}

[9. ]{.class_4938}["My spirit made diligent search"]{.class_4939}[(vs. 6).]{.class_4938}

[This is what a man does when he gets in trouble, at least if he has any sense. God is the one to seek, for He is the Creator and Sustainer of life. He is the One that needs to be remembered. He is often forgotten (Job 35:10; Rom. 1:21), since He is ]{.class_4938}["a Spirit" ]{.class_4939}[(John 4:24) and not subject to the physical senses of observation. Job complains (Job 7:19), Moses complains (Num. 11:15), Elijah complains (1 Kings 19:4), Hezekiah complains (Isa. 38:12--14), and David complains (1 Sam. 29:8). Griping seems to be a "fundamental" of the faith with human beings. Paul is the only saint who never lodges one complaint in thirteen epistles. His continual "living above sin" and his sustained "victorious life" make a modern Christian nervous when he reads the account. But if a complaint is to be lodged, Job and Jonah (Job 21:4, 10:2, 12:4; Jonah 2:2) know where to lodge it: with God.]{.class_4938}

The "overwhelmed spirit" is the prelude to shipwreck (Prov. 18:14). Sometimes all of the advice to "get out from under the circumstances" is just Swindollian Gothardian-MacArthurian "rose water." Some of these modern, slick, smooth, amateur "copers and sharers" ought to spend a little time ministering to children in a spastic clinic or to patients in a leper colony. I can just see "Chuck" Swindoll or MacArthur---or anyone like them---sitting down on the ash heap with Job and "counseling" him on how to live "the victorious life." Much that passes these days for "Bible study" is just Norman Vincent Peale and Dale Carnegie diluted with corrupt versions of the Bible.

[When a man is ]{.class_4938}["so troubled"]{.class_4939}[ that he]{.class_4938}[ "cannot speak"]{.class_4939}[ (vs. 4), he is in real trouble. The prayers become more abbreviated until they are reduced to groans. The Psalmist is speechless as well as sleepless (vs. 4). The case is Psalm 38:14, which see.]{.class_4938}

"[Reminiscing" is what takes place when trouble stops a man's forward progress: there is no place to look but BACK (vs. 5). This explains why "old folks" tend to "live in the past," but it is not always as futile as it sounds. Note that the "old ways" are the ways God intends for you to walk in (Jer. 6:16). The problem comes when reviewing the past gives you a false picture of the present. The Psalmist in verse 5 is obeying the direct commandment found in Deuteronomy 32:7 and Job 8:8. A look back to the]{.class_4938}[ "pit"]{.class_4939}[ from which we were ]{.class_4938}["digged" ]{.class_4939}[(Isa. 51:1) will help cheer any saint up; that is, if he ever came up from a pit. A man who does not consider ]{.class_4938}["the days of old,"]{.class_4939}[ where they are ]{.class_4938}["ancient times" ]{.class_4939}[(say, 4000 B.C. to A.D. 1000), can never really tell what is going on in his own day (Ecc. 1:9).]{.class_4938}

[The ]{.class_4938}["song in the night" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 6) is the one that Job asked about (Job 35:10), and in this case, it means that the singer can actually recall a time when he praised God while he was right in the midst of a mess. ]{.class_4938}["My song in the night" ]{.class_4939}[is a ]{.class_4938}["new song" ]{.class_4939}[(see Ps. 33:3, 40:3, and comments), and I am told to sing it out loudly when I am lying in my bed (Ps. 149:5). Verse 6 is describing a heart searching "introspection." Watch old Nehemiah doing it in Nehemiah 5:7.]{.class_4938}

All of the terms in verses 1--6 are general: they do not tell us exactly about what the Psalmist was thinking, but in verse 7 the subject of his introspection comes up. He is meditating on the goodness and mercy of God in the past and wondering whether these mercies have been terminated in his case, or are they still available? He has been delivered before.

77:7 "Will the Lord cast off for ever? and will he be favourable no more?

8 Is his mercy clean gone for ever? doth his promise fail for evermore?

9 Hath God forgotten to be gracious? hath he in anger shut up his tender mercies? Selah.

10 And I said, This is my infirmity: but I will remember the years of the right hand of the most High.

11 I will remember the works of the LORD: surely I will remember thy wonders of old.

12 I will meditate also of all thy work, and talk of thy doings."

Six questions are asked, and all six resemble each other.

[The first is, ]{.class_4938}["Will the Lord cast off for ever?" ]{.class_4939}[Yes, He will, in some cases (1 Chron. 28:9). Where He promised not to (vs. 8), as in Jeremiah 33:26 and 2 Samuel 7:15, He will NOT. ]{.class_4938}["Will he be favorable no more?" ]{.class_4939}[Notice how we are moving into references that deal with Israel, and not just the "sweet Psalmist." This is confirmed immediately after ]{.class_4938}["Hath he in anger shut up his tender mercies?" ]{.class_4939}[with the familiar watchword we have come to know so well: ]{.class_4938}["Selah." ]{.class_4939}[Observe that in Jeremiah's time---one of the greatest pictures of the Antichrist's future destruction of Jerusalem (Rev. 11)---the ]{.class_4938}["mercies"]{.class_4939}[ are taken away (Jer. 16:5). We are back at Lamentations 3:22 again, having been here a dozen times since Psalm 1. From the standpoint of Israel, they must remember God's mercies in the past; note how Ezra, Joshua, and Nehemiah harp on this note (Ezra 9:7--13; Josh. 24:4--11; Neh. 9:7--26). The past is the monitor of the future. Thus we read ]{.class_4938}["I will remember the works of the LORD" ]{.class_4939}[and ]{.class_4938}["thy wonders of old."]{.class_4939}

[Inspirationally]{.class_4937}[, the saint should "count his many blessings, name them one by one," and it will surprise him "what the Lord hath done." Think about what He saved you from. Think about all of the diseases and afflictions you did NOT get. Think about the years he protected you from broken bones, hospital bills, deadly diseases, bankruptcy, starvation, death in combat, plane crashes, hurricanes, earthquakes, car wrecks, tornadoes, etc. For many of us, if it is really "rough" the rest of the way---and maybe I will be in that number!---it certainly has been decades and decades of mercy and "gracious" dealings from God in the past.]{.class_4938}

"[And I said, This is my infirmity" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 10). You didn't say that in any Bible but one. The ]{.class_4938}[NKJV ]{.class_4937}[does not have the reading neither does the ]{.class_4938}[RV, RSV, NRSV, ASV, NASV, NIV, ]{.class_4937}[or any other apostate corruption parading around as a "Bible." An infirmity is a "weakness," as in 2 Corinthians 12:5, 11:30. The Psalmist has two weaknesses: one, a physical sickness that keeps him awake and makes him complain; and two, a spiritual weakness that makes him doubt God's mercies and His promises (vss. 8--9). Jeremiah is a perfect illustration. He gets so "put out" with God's lot for his life and God's dealings with him (Jer. 20:7), that he is tempted to charge God with lying (Jer. 15:18).]{.class_4938}

(Oh yeah, man! You can't get this stuff in any Christian college!)
