They also ]{.class_4938}[are ]{.class_4937}[troubled forever (Rev. 14:10--11), and they perish (Mal. 4:1--3).]{.class_4938}

[In the commentators, the prayer is never actually answered, ]{.class_4938}[literally. ]{.class_4937}[Kroll can't find any kind of fulfillment at any time in the past, present, or future. William Smith, speaking for Spurgeon, probably makes the best representative confession for blockheads like himself who cannot see beyond their noses. "In IMAGERY both obvious and vivid to every native of the gusty hills and plains of Palestine, though to us comparatively unintelligible, the Psalmist describes\...."]{.class_4938}

[He describes something that an unintelligent man like Smith, Kroll, Clarke, Lange, Dummelow, Ellicott, Adams, Ryle, Duhm, Jamieson, Fausset, Brown, and Yates would find completely unintelligible. Don't say "US," Brother Smith. Speak for the Scholar's Union to which you belong. Some of "us" ]{.class_4938}[can ]{.class_4937}[read, and we ]{.class_4938}[believe ]{.class_4937}[what we read. Don't say "US" just because you are too blind to know what is going on and want some company. ("Where is the English Channel?" "I don't know; I can't get it on my set.")]{.class_4938}

"[That men may know." ]{.class_4939}[They did not know in 1000 B.C., 800 B.C., A.D. 800, A.D. 1200, or A.D. 1800; and they don't know yet (see Ezek. 38:23). They won't know until the One who has the "name" (vs. 18) on His thigh (Rev. 19:12) shows up. Nothing was fulfilled in the past: ]{.class_4938}[not one item.]{.class_4937}

"[Art the most high over all the earth." ]{.class_4939}[This is the Millennial reign as already identified and documented on twenty previous occasions (Ps. 2:8; 8:1, 9; 22:27; 24:1; 23:5; 37:11, 22; 46:8--10; 47:2, 7, 9; 48:10; 58:11; 59:13; 65:5, 9; 66:4; 67:2). But why reject a man's scholarship just because he flunked twenty tests in a row? "Ain't none of us perfect."]{.class_4938}

"[Whose name alone is JEHOVAH." ]{.class_4939}[This is the unpronounceable "tetragrammaton" composed of ]{.class_4938}[Yod, He, Vau, ]{.class_4937}[and ]{.class_4938}[He---]{.class_4937}[varying in pronunciation, depending upon which vowel points you put under the Yod, He, Vau. In America, in the 1980s, a whole cult of "Jeshua"-"Yehovistic"-"Yehosuahs" was formed which insisted the name "Jesus" was a false name applied to the wrong Christ. It was built on the idea that you could not have a Greek name for the Saviour; the Gentiles were "out." You had to have the Old Testament name given to Moses (see ]{.class_4938}[The Bible Believer's Commentary on Exodus, ]{.class_4937}[Exod. 6:3).]{.class_4938}

[The word (however pronounced) means "the eternal One" or the "immutable and unchanging One." The translation ]{.class_4938}["I AM THAT I AM" ]{.class_4939}[(Exod. 3:14, ]{.class_4938}[AV]{.class_4937}[) is the best one any translator ever adopted; it has never been improved upon and never will be, although Lynchburg cannot tolerate it (]{.class_4938}[NKJV]{.class_4937}[), Bob Jones University can't tolerate it (]{.class_4938}[NASV]{.class_4937}[),]{.class_4938}[ ]{.class_4937}[Wheaton and Moody can't stand it (]{.class_4938}[NIV]{.class_4937}[), and the United Methodist Church can't stand it (]{.class_4938}[RSV]{.class_4937}[). Kenneth Taylor (]{.class_4938}[TLB: ]{.class_4937}[The Lamentable Baloney) says "The Sovereign God," which doesn't translate any Hebrew tetragammaton. ]{.class_4938}["EL" is ]{.class_4937}[the Hebrew form for God as an all powerful Sovereign. There are ten "Jehovahs" in Scripture (Gen. 22:14; Exod. 15:26, 17:15, 31:13; Judg. 6:24; 1 Sam. 1:3; Jer. 23:6; Ezek. 48:35; Ps. 7:17; and Ps. 23:1). These are different titles for the One ]{.class_4938}["Jehovah."]{.class_4939}[ They describe His attributes ("He will provide," "He will heal," "He is your banner," "He sends peace," etc.). See ]{.class_4938}["Jah"]{.class_4939}[ in Psalm 68:4.]{.class_4938}

Psalm 83:18 is answered in Revelation 19 and 20, literally. The fact that the greatest Hebrew scholars and Hebrew exegetes who ever lived couldn't find it is no disgrace to them, because Premillennial, New Testament Baptists in the twentieth century couldn't find it either.

[]{#Uxd5JyNYSWCtgFM2T9UhJQ916.xhtml}

PSALM 84

84:1 "How amiable are thy tabernacles, O LORD of hosts!

2 My soul longeth, yea, even fainteth for the courts of the LORD: my heart and my flesh crieth out for the living God.

3 Yea, the sparrow hath found an house, and the swallow a nest for herself, where she may lay her young, even thine altars, O LORD of hosts, my King, and my God.

4 Blessed are they that dwell in thy house: they will be still praising thee. Selah.

5 Blessed is the man whose strength is in thee; in whose heart are the ways of them.

6 Who passing through the valley of Baca make it a well; the rain also filleth the pools.

7 They go from strength to strength, every one of them in Zion appeareth before God.

8 O LORD God of hosts, hear my prayer: give ear, O God of Jacob. Selah."

[We have noticed many "blind spots" are in the Alexandrian Cult---that elite group of self appointed Bible correctors who devoted their lives to erasing the revelations of the Holy Spirit given in 1611. We now hit a typical blind spot: the restored millennial temple at Jerusalem (Ezek. 40--45). This temple simply doesn't exist in the minds of people like Motyer, Hitzig, Yates, Briggs, Davidson, Hupfeld, Baethgen, Hengstenberg, and others; but in this Psalm we are going to find plurals ascribed to a temple, ]{.class_4938}["tabernacles\...courts\...altars\..." ]{.class_4939}[etc., which is ]{.class_4938}["thy house" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 4).]{.class_4938}

[The doctrinal "thrust" of the passage was aimed at the rebuilt temple at Jerusalem in the Millennium. Notice the constant appearance of plurals in describing what was only ONE sanctuary in the wilderness (the tabernacle) and ONE sanctuary under Solomon. The temple to be rebuilt is a huge multiple-partitioned structure (see Ezek. 40--46) containing ]{.class_4938}["tabernacles" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 1), ]{.class_4938}["courts" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 2), and ]{.class_4938}["altars" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 3). First Kings 8:64 showed the need for ]{.class_4938}["altars."]{.class_4939}

[Kroll acknowledges the millennial temple (see his notes on Zech. 6), but ]{.class_4938}[here, ]{.class_4937}[with the temple before him, he throws the verses into the past again, although ]{.class_4938}["Selah" ]{.class_4939}[should warn him (vss. 4, 8) that he is dealing with more than Korah back in the time of Solomon.]{.class_4938}

"[How amiable are thy tabernacles" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 1). This will not do. It must go. Such words cannot be tolerated in a "modern, up to date translation" where the "dynamic equivalent" can "communicate to the receptor" the "intent of the blankety, blankety, blank, blank." Out goes ]{.class_4938}["amiable" ]{.class_4939}[and in comes "lovely." Out goes ]{.class_4938}["amiable" ]{.class_4939}[and in comes "dear." (This is the result of too many Moon Pies and RCs, while watching the "late late" movie.) The original word meant "friendly or peaceful" and referred to something ]{.class_4938}[worthy ]{.class_4937}[of love---not something "lovely." By going to a base root (Latin "amo') instead of the second form of the root (Latin "amicabilis"), the scholars misled the modern reader. The modern thinks of "lovey-dovey," "Ain't that jes lovely," and "Lovely to look at, delightful to hold,\...etc." or "Those flowers are simply LOVELY." This is not translating. This is the work of someone who has been cut off from contact with the century in which he lives.]{.class_4938}

[The word chosen by "the King's men" indicates something that is attractive because it is warm, peaceable, and friendly. It is a harmonious disposition that breathes of PEACE. Without being Premillennialists, the ]{.class_4938}[AV ]{.class_4937}[translators got the right word in, for the prophetic element is there. All the commentators missed ]{.class_4938}["Selah" ]{.class_4939}[again (vss. 4 and 8), although God stuck it in TWICE so they would "get the message." (They couldn't get a message off a phone answering service if it was repeated ten times.)]{.class_4938}

[Verse 1 is self explanatory, at least as it stands in the ]{.class_4938}[Authorized text]{.class_4937}[.]{.class_4938}

"[Tabernacles" ]{.class_4939}[must go. Out it goes. It is not in the ]{.class_4938}[NKJV,; ]{.class_4937}[it is not in the ]{.class_4938}[RV, RSV, NRSV, ASV, NASV, ]{.class_4937}[or ]{.class_4938}[NIV ]{.class_4937}[("Dead Duck Otherism"). After praising the ]{.class_4938}[NKJV ]{.class_4937}[to high heaven at Lynchburg, Falwell returned to reading ]{.class_4938}[The Living Bible, ]{.class_4937}[and his faculty members didn't dare print the ]{.class_4938}[NKJV ]{.class_4937}[in their commentary. Kroll, therefore, must use the plural of the King James (]{.class_4938}["tabernacles"]{.class_4939}[) and explain it away by saying, "As Alexander points out\...it has reference to the subdivisions and apurtenances of the sanctuary." The ]{.class_4938}[NIV, Living Bible, ]{.class_4937}[and the ]{.class_4938}[New Rotten Sick Version ]{.class_4937}[(]{.class_4938}[NRSV]{.class_4937}[) simply correct the Hebrew word with "new findings from the Dead Sea Scrolls" and say "temple" or "dwelling place."]{.class_4938}

[Notice that all was clear without messing with the text. There is an outer court and an inner court (courts) to the millennial temple (Ezek. 40:19--20). It is called God's ]{.class_4938}["house"]{.class_4939}[ (singular, as in verses 4 and 10), and ]{.class_4938}["tabernacles" ]{.class_4939}[as in verse 1, for the millennial temple contains dozens of]{.class_4938}[ "chambers"]{.class_4939}[ within it (Ezek. 41--42) where the offerings are slain for sacrifice, thus justifying the word ]{.class_4938}["altars" ]{.class_4939}[in verse 3, which would include the brazen altar and the golden altar of incense.]{.class_4938}

Kroll's exegesis of verse 2 is that it is the modern equivalent of saying "I am dying to visit your house, Lord." This is about as appropriate as exegeting Psalm 82:5 as "people have a hard time when they don't know what to believe." The Psalmist, in verse 2, is in such a "fix" that Luther (and the Syriac) ascribe the Psalm to David during one of his times of persecution. Whoever it is, he is longing for God with his (1) soul, (2) flesh, and (3) heart. This is the first commandment demonstrated. A heart that "cries out" for the living God is certainly not to be matched with a twenty-first-century churchgoer getting up Sunday morning and saying, "I am just DYING to go to church today!" Comedy is all right in its place, but this is not the place.

"[Yea, the sparrow hath found an house, and the swallow\...even thine altars" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 3).]{.class_4938}

There is no chance that the birds will lay hard boiled eggs on the altar of sacrifice outside the Holy Place, nor are they going to fly into the Holy Place and lay eggs on the golden incense altar. This time, the plural must be taken as figurative, representing the places of sacrifice---the temple. The millennial temple is of such a size that the birds can literally build their nests under the eaves and arches of it. Furthermore, in the millennial land, the temple area doesn't have to be a bare, bald expanse on top of Mt. Zion. From the material found in Isaiah 24, 30 and Romans 8, you can expect two-hundred-foot trees where sagebrush now grows.

[Observe ]{.class_4938}["my King,"]{.class_4939}[ the Jewish designation. Christ, to the Church, is our ]{.class_4938}["head"]{.class_4939}[ and our ]{.class_4938}["Lord" ]{.class_4939}[(Eph. 5:23; 1 Cor.8:6), but this is the One who inhabits ]{.class_4938}["the city of the great King"]{.class_4939}[ (Ps. 48:2). Those that]{.class_4938}[ "dwell" ]{.class_4939}[in ]{.class_4938}["thy house" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 4) are doing more than going in and out of it like they did in Moses' tabernacle and Solomon's temple. They are changing clothes in it (Ezek. 42:14), and they are ]{.class_4938}["still praising" ]{.class_4939}[God when Gog and Magog surround Jerusalem to annihilate it (Rev. 20:9). ]{.class_4938}["They will be still praising thee." ]{.class_4939}[THEN, ]{.class_4938}["Selah." "The ways" ]{.class_4939}[of these servants are described in verse 5 (]{.class_4938}["blessed is the man\...in whose heart are the ways of them"]{.class_4939}[;]{.class_4938}[ ]{.class_4939}[i.e., those who serve God in the temple and praise Him). This must be done away with immediately, so the Scholar's Union lands on the ]{.class_4938}[Authorized ]{.class_4937}[text with scalpel, laser beam, crowbar, ramrod, and "zap" guns and gives you this:]{.class_4938}

[1. "Have set their hearts on pilgrimage" (]{.class_4938}[NIV]{.class_4937}[).]{.class_4938}

[2. "Are set on the pilgrim ways" (]{.class_4938}[New English Bible]{.class_4937}[).]{.class_4938}

[3. "Are the highways to Zion" (]{.class_4938}[RSV, NRSV]{.class_4937}[).]{.class_4938}

[4. "To follow your steps" (]{.class_4938}[The Living Bible]{.class_4937}[).]{.class_4938}

[5. "Whose heart is set on pilgrimage" (]{.class_4938}[NKJV]{.class_4937}[).]{.class_4938}

6\. "The steeps are on their hearts" (John Fry).

7\. "Bent on climbing the steep ascents" (Horsley).

[8. "Are the highways to Zion" (]{.class_4938}[NASV]{.class_4937}[).]{.class_4938}

[Dead Duck Otherism. There is only one English Bible left on this earth that tells you that the WAYS of servants who ]{.class_4938}["still" ]{.class_4939}[praise God---in spite of trouble and danger---should be kept in the heart. In the very next verse an illustration is given of how these ]{.class_4938}["ways" ]{.class_4939}[operate when a man is faced with trouble or danger. ]{.class_4938}["Who passing through the valley of Baca make it a well" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 6). Observe that the man who can do this goes ]{.class_4938}["from strength to strength" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 7), and this strength was placed in God (vs. 5) because the man was ]{.class_4938}["blessed" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 5). Most of the scholars identify ]{.class_4938}["Baca" ]{.class_4939}[as being the valley of Bochim mentioned in Judges 2:1, 5. The word signifies a mulberry tree, and we are told that when the berries of th:is tree are squeezed the juice that comes out resembles a ]{.class_4938}[tear drop.]{.class_4937}

[Since the Psalmist has gone into spiritual and devotional matters in verses 4 and 5, we can make application in verses 6 and 7, not forgetting, of course, that the weeping, troubled saint here is primarily the one of Luke 6:21--25 and Matthew 5:4, 10. ]{.class_4938}["Selah" ]{.class_4939}[warns us that the ones who will appear ]{.class_4938}["before God" ]{.class_4939}[in Mt. Zion (vs. 7) are Tribulation Jewish saints. All of the commentators miss all the connections, as usual.]{.class_4938}

"[O God of Jacob. Selah" ]{.class_4939}[shows that the things said above have a direct application for the "blessed" who weep and mourn (see Luke 6:20--21) BEFORE the Lord returns to Zion (Ps. 2:6, 9:11, 14:7, 48:2, 11--12, etc.).]{.class_4938}

[Blessed is the man who can make "stepping stones" out of his "stumbling blocks" (Bob Jones Sr.). Blessed is the man who can turn the Bataan Death March into a call to be a missionary to the Japanese. Blessed is the man who can turn two years in a concentration camp into a biography that will magnify Jesus Christ and glorify God. Blessed is the man who can turn a cancer ward,or a burn ward, into an opportunity to lead a nurse or a doctor to Jesus Christ. Verse 6 is Romans 8:28 in action. A barren, dry valley watered with nothing but tears becomes a well, and then more tears rain down until they form a pool of living water where a sinner can quench his ]{.class_4938}["thirst" ]{.class_4939}[(see Matt. 5:6).]{.class_4938}

84:9 "Behold, O God our shield, and look upon the face of thine anointed.

10 For a day in thy courts is better than a thousand. I had rather be a doorkeeper in the house of my God, than to dwell in the tents of wickedness.

11 For the LORD God is a sun and shield: the LORD will give grace and glory: no good thing will he withhold from them that walk uprightly.

12 O LORD of hosts, blessed is the man that trusteth in thee."

"[Behold, O God our shield." ]{.class_4939}[A shield was anointed (2 Sam. 1:21), it was kept in memorium (1 Kings 10:17), it had to be taken into battle (1 Sam. 17:7), and its purpose was for stopping fire brands (Eph. 6:16), or deflecting the blows of swords, daggers, and spears (1 Chron. 12:24). God is said to be the shield for Jacob. The prayer has a double application. First of all, "look upon our human king David," who was "God's anointed" (see Ps. 18:50), and protect him. Secondly, look upon the face of Your Messiah (see Hab. 3:13) with favor and accept Him for us. It turns out that the ]{.class_4938}["anointed," ]{.class_4939}[in this case, is not only a ]{.class_4938}["shield"]{.class_4939}[ (see vs. 11) but ]{.class_4938}["a sun"]{.class_4939}[ (vs. 11. Cf. Mal. 4:2).]{.class_4938}

[This brings up an interesting question which all of the twentieth-century commentators failed to see---how any Fundamentalist who wanted to stand up for the Bible could have missed it, I don't know---"How did a writer in 1000 B.C. know there was more than one 'sun'"? He didn't say "THE sun"; he said ]{.class_4938}["a sun." ]{.class_4939}[From whence cometh this pre scientific knowledge 2,500 years ahead of time?]{.class_4938}

[The ]{.class_4938}["shield" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 11) is commented on under Psalm 59:1. The ]{.class_4938}["sun,"]{.class_4939}[ of course, is a reference to the ]{.class_4938}["Sun of righteousness" ]{.class_4939}[(Mal. 4:2) who will show up ]{.class_4938}["as a bridegroom" ]{.class_4939}[returning from the marriage (see Ps. 19:4--5 and comments), at the Second Advent. ]{.class_4938}["Thine anointed" ]{.class_4939}[will then be a reference to two things: a Jew, like Korah, praying for God to bless his king (David or Solomon, 2 Chron. 6:4), and Korah asking God to bless His Messiah (]{.class_4938}["the Lord's Christ," ]{.class_4939}[Luke 2:26).]{.class_4938}

"[For a day in thy courts" ]{.class_4939}[is better than a year in the Vatican. It is better than a thousand days anywhere else. The verse points ]{.class_4938}[prophetically ]{.class_4937}[to the Millennium again, as pointed out in 2 Peter 3. ]{.class_4938}[Devotionally ]{.class_4937}[(as we handled it above), the]{.class_4938}[ "day"]{.class_4939}[ in the Lord's courts is better because the thousand days could be spent in the ]{.class_4938}["tents of wickedness."]{.class_4939}[ Thus: "One evening with Lester Roloff and Bob Gray is better than seven evenings with Madonna and Elvis Presley." Thus "One night with Maze Jackson and Billy Kelly is better than ten nights with Bing Crosby and Frank Sinatra." Again: "One day with John R. Rice and Jack Hyles is better than ten days with Phil Donahue and Mike Wallace." "I had rather be a janitor in a Bible-believing Baptist church than be a Supreme Court Justice," or "I had rather be an usher in a Bible-believing Baptist church than mayor of Washington, D.C., or the Secretary of State."]{.class_4938}

[There are all kinds of applications. "One hour spent in reading a ]{.class_4938}[King James Bible is ]{.class_4937}[better than 300 hours studying Nestle's Greek New Testament," etc. Note that 2 Peter 3:8 lands you in the Millennium. This ]{.class_4938}["day"]{.class_4939}[ is 1,000 years long, when the Levites serve the Lord in His ]{.class_4938}["house"]{.class_4939}[ and in His courts. The ]{.class_4938}["doorkeeper"]{.class_4939}[ ]{.class_4938}[is literal, ]{.class_4937}[for Korah was literally a ]{.class_4938}["doorkeeper" ]{.class_4939}[(see 1 Chron. 9:19). The privilege of being NEAR to God and the privilege of being called to SERVE Him, or WAIT on Him, outshines and outclasses any other two callings that could come to ]{.class_4938}[any ]{.class_4937}[man on the face of this earth in ]{.class_4938}[any ]{.class_4937}[age of the world's history, on ]{.class_4938}[any ]{.class_4937}[continent, under ]{.class_4938}[any ]{.class_4937}[condition. That is an "absolute." There is no competing opinion worth considering.]{.class_4938}

[This has nothing to do with a fake, Gentile priesthood (Roman Catholics) pretending they are serving God, or "waiting on Him, " by inventing a fake sacrifice that is no more efficacious than a bingo card (see Heb. 10:4). Being near to God has nothing to do with traveling to Lourdes or Rome and kissing some idiot's hand or some idol's foot. Nearness to God, ]{.class_4938}[in this age, ]{.class_4937}[comes from worshipping God ]{.class_4938}["in spirit and in truth" ]{.class_4939}[(John 4:23) and liking what He likes, loving what He loves, hating what He hates, believing what He believes, honoring what He honors, and doing what He commands (John 15:10--14). Abortion, drinking, popes, smoking, bingo parties, cussing, race mixing, prayers to Mary, rape, extortion, masses for the dead, baby sprinkling, kidnapping, child molesting, purgatory, pornography, evolution, and rosaries are NOT in the set up.]{.class_4938}

"[No good thing will he withhold" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 11). Note: the condition in the Old Testament is works (]{.class_4938}["from them that walk uprightly"]{.class_4939}[). In the New Testament it is conditioned on ASKING (Luke 11:13; Matt. 7:11). The greatest conditional promise along this line is found in the New Testament in 2 Corinthians 9:8, where a blank check is written out which contains so many "riches" that the promise is almost impossible to believe; however, this promise is ]{.class_4938}[conditioned ]{.class_4937}[upon a Christian not being STINGY (see comments on 2 Cor. 9 in that commentary).]{.class_4938}

Verse 12 is self explanatory. See Psalms 21:7, 32:1, and 57:1.

[]{#Uxd5JyNYSWCtgFM2T9UhJQ966.xhtml}

PSALM 85

85:1 "LORD, thou hast been favourable unto thy land: thou hast brought back the captivity of Jacob.

2 Thou hast forgiven the iniquity of thy people, thou hast covered all their sin. Selah.

3 Thou hast taken away all thy wrath: thou hast turned thyself from the fierceness of thine anger.

4 Turn us, O God of our salvation, and cause thine anger toward us to cease.

5 Wilt thou be angry with us for ever? wilt thou draw out thine anger to all generations?

6 Wilt thou not revive us again: that thy people may rejoice in thee?

7 Shew us thy mercy, O LORD, and grant us thy salvation."

Note that the Advent is misplaced chronologically in the first seven verses. Verses 1--3 refer to the restoration of Israel after the Seventieth Week is over, while verses 4--7 refer to the remnant in the Tribulation waiting for the Advent.

This juxtaposition of time unhorses the dudes who ride the hobby horse of destructive criticism. We take one for a classic example of the muddled confusion and utter helplessness of a twentieth-century Fundamentalist teaching at a Baptist university. This is Kroll, trying to plow his way through a sandstorm that has him so blinded that he can't even locate the points on a compass:

"[Because of the reference to the captivity\...and the total lack of any distinct or definite historical allusions in the rest of the Psalm, the most common belief among scholars today \[Don't you know, buster!\] is that this Psalm was written either at the time of Zerubbabel or more likely during the days of Ezra and Nehemiah\...beyond the historical allusions found in verse 1, our commentators are hard pressed to interpret the rest of the Psalm \[Not any more "hard pressed" than YOU are, son!\] historically\...there are many allusions in this Psalm that have no historical reference\...there is surely no day or epic in history in which this can have been said to be historically a fact\...it ]{.class_4938}[appears ]{.class_4937}[that the most appropriate interpretation is ]{.class_4938}[not ]{.class_4937}[historical at all, but prophetic\...the future restoration of Israel\...it is from that ]{.class_4938}[perspective ]{.class_4937}[that we interpret this Psalm."]{.class_4938}

[Having said this, Kroll denies that the ]{.class_4938}["captivity" ]{.class_4939}[of verse 1 is]{.class_4938}[ literal: ]{.class_4937}["The word for captivity is used metaphorically, for calamities short of an actual historical captivity\...it is not the Psalmist's intent to point to a specific event" because "God has often been favorable to Israel." He then says that God removed Israel's sins in the Old Testament (vs. 2) and caused His anger to cease from them! This, in view of 1 Thessalonians 2:16; Romans 11:9--10; Acts 7:42; and Hebrews 10:27, 30. Realizing he has blown it, Kroll now says, "together these verses SEEM to point to a time when judgments are over for Israel forever, yet the Scriptures indicate that the greatest tribulation the nation Israel will ever undergo is yet future."]{.class_4938}

[Now, tell me, where are you? Where is he? Where is the truth? Old Kroll can't land anywhere. The ]{.class_4938}["captivity" ]{.class_4939}[is found in Job 42:10 and Jeremiah 29:14, and TWO captivities are mentioned (Isa. 11:11). Kroll is looking at fulfillment in front of his eyes but cannot believe it. Verses 1--3 are the END of the second captivity; they are the END of the Tribulation when what Israel will undergo "is yet future," according to Kroll. Kroll finally sees that verses 6--8 ]{.class_4938}[are ]{.class_4937}[Tribulation prayers that will be answered at the end of the Tribulation, but my, my, what a tortuous route to follow simply because he couldn't believe what he read the first time. He couldn't put verses 1--3 into the Millennium because they didn't match his ]{.class_4938}[chronological order, ]{.class_4937}[but he had to give in when he hit verses 9--11.]{.class_4938}

[All was apparent anyway. Verse 1 is the end of the Tribulation; verse 2 is the end of the Tribulation. Verse 3 is the end of the Tribulation. Verses 4--7 are Tribulation prayers that are answered at the end of the Tribulation. ]{.class_4938}[Not one problem anywhere in the passage. ]{.class_4937}["Wilt thou not revive us again"?]{.class_4939}

[Yes, and the date is given in Hosea 6:2. It is A.D. 2000, if our calendar is correct.]{.class_4938}[ Kroll missed it.]{.class_4937}

85:8 "I will hear what God the LORD will speak: for he will speak peace unto his people, and to his saints: but let them not turn again to folly.

9 Surely his salvation is nigh them that fear him; that glory may dwell in our land.

10 Mercy and truth are met together; righteousness and peace have kissed each other.

11 Truth shall spring out of the earth; and righteousness shall look down from heaven.

12 Yea, the LORD shall give that which is good; and our land shall yield her increase.

13 Righteousness shall go before him; and shall set us in the way of his steps."

"[His saints" ]{.class_4939}[in verse 8 are the ]{.class_4938}["saints"]{.class_4939}[ of Daniel 7:18, 21, 22. They are the ]{.class_4938}["his people" ]{.class_4939}[of Deuteronomy 32:43. They are warned not to turn back, exactly as they were instructed in Hebrews 10:38--39 when God spoke from heaven in the ]{.class_4938}[Tribulation ]{.class_4937}[(Heb. 12:25--27, and Hag. 2:6). Kroll missed it again. He missed it after identifying the context; Spurgeon, Dummelow, Yates, Motyer, Lange, Clarke, Briggs, and Ewald could not even identify the context.]{.class_4938}

"[Righteousness and peace have kissed each other." ]{.class_4939}[They never can until then. They never can unless the King of Righteousness precedes the King of Peace (see Heb. 7:2), for there is no ]{.class_4938}["on earth peace" ]{.class_4939}[until there is righteousness (Matt. 6:33), and there is no righteousness until there is ]{.class_4938}["glory to God in the highest" ]{.class_4939}[(Luke 2:14). Isaiah gives it as James gives it: no peace without righteousness (Isa. 32:17), and no peace without purity first (James 3:17). Kroll misses all of the revelations in both Testaments. He has enough sense to see that peace will not show up before the ]{.class_4938}[Millennium, ]{.class_4937}[but he fails to see that the righteousness preceded the peace by ]{.class_4938}[two thousand years. ]{.class_4937}[Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown finish everything at Calvary without the "foggiest" of how "peace" came in with the Civil War, the Hundred Years War, the War of the Roses, the Gothic invasions, the French Revolution, the Crusades, the Inquisition, World War I, World War II, Vietnam, Korea, the War of 1812, the Franco Prussian War and the next two world wars coming up (Rev. 6:4, 19:19). Truth certainly did not ]{.class_4938}["spring out of the earth" ]{.class_4939}[anywhere between the Crucifixion and 2003. Truth fell in the streets (Isa. 59:14). The context of ]{.class_4938}["righteousness and peace"]{.class_4939}[ kissing each other, and ]{.class_4938}["mercy and truth"]{.class_4939}[ meeting together is ]{.class_4938}["our land shall yield her increase" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 12) and glory dwelling in ]{.class_4938}["our land" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 9).]{.class_4938}

[The problem in this age is simple. Man has rejected God's righteousness and so cannot have any peace. Further, if the truth is known (the whole truth), then mercy is out of the question, for the truth of the matter is Romans 1--3. It is MERCY ]{.class_4938}[in spite of the truth]{.class_4937}[ (Eph. 2:4--5)]{.class_4938}[ that saves the sinner. ]{.class_4937}["Righteousness shall go before him" ]{.class_4939}[tells Kroll that the King of righteousness (]{.class_4938}["Melchisedec,"]{.class_4939}[ Heb. 7:2) had to show up ]{.class_4938}[before ]{.class_4937}[the King of Peace came (King of Salem). He showed up. But when He showed up, He put ]{.class_4938}["righteousness and peace"]{.class_4939}[ so far apart that "kissin' distance" was out of the question; how do you "kiss" when you are two thousand years apart? You don't.]{.class_4938}

[Inspirationally, ]{.class_4937}[there are all kinds of things here. 1. God's people need reviving from time to time.]{.class_4938}

2\. Times of revival are times of rejoicing.

3\. We are to listen to God, not to man.

[4. Your peace (vs. 8) will depend upon whether you keep in your heart ]{.class_4938}["the ways of them" ]{.class_4939}[(Ps. 84:5) or turn back to ]{.class_4938}["folly" ]{.class_4939}[(Ps. 85:8).]{.class_4938}

[5. God will set a man in ]{.class_4938}["the way of his steps" ]{.class_4939}[if he desires to go in that direction (see Psa. 27:11, 1:1, 16:11, 25:4).]{.class_4938}

[]{#Uxd5JyNYSWCtgFM2T9UhJQ1000.xhtml}

PSALM 86

86:1 "Bow down thine ear, O LORD, hear me: for I am poor and needy.

2 Preserve my soul; for I am holy: O thou my God, save thy servant that trusteth in thee.

3 Be merciful unto me, O Lord: for I cry unto thee daily.

4 Rejoice the soul of thy servant: for unto thee, O Lord, do I lift up my soul.

5 For thou, Lord, art good, and ready to forgive; and plenteous in mercy unto all them that call upon thee.

6 Give ear, O LORD, unto my prayer; and attend to the voice of my supplications.

7 In the day of my trouble I will call upon thee: for thou wilt answer me."

[Most of what is before us is ]{.class_4938}[devotional.]{.class_4937}

[Verse 1 is plain; it matches such passages as Psalm 4:1, 3; 5:3; 10:17; and 13:3 (which see). ]{.class_4938}["Preserve my soul" ]{.class_4939}[refers to physical life in Genesis 12:13, 19:20; Joshua 10:28, 30, and other Old Testament passages, but it is applicable to eternal security in the New Testament if an inspirational application is made.]{.class_4938}

"[For I am holy" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 2). Kroll (anxious to prove that salvation under the law is identical to salvation under grace) immediately quotes Spurgeon verbatim; the idea being that David is not referring to his ]{.class_4938}[personal holiness. ]{.class_4937}[How this is done in view of David's own testimony as he recorded it in Psalm 7:3--4, 8, and Psalm 15 is a marvel to behold. David claims salvation and prosperity according to his ]{.class_4938}[personal righteousness ]{.class_4937}[(see Psa. 18:20--26). Spurgeon and Kroll are off on a wild goose chase.]{.class_4938}

[It is the word ]{.class_4938}["holy"]{.class_4939}[ that rattles the cage of the apostate critics. The ]{.class_4938}[NIV ]{.class_4937}[quickly gets rid of it and says "devoted to you." ]{.class_4938}[The Living Boo Boo ]{.class_4937}[says, "follow all your laws," and the ]{.class_4938}[RSV]{.class_4937}[, with the ]{.class_4938}[New English Bible, ]{.class_4937}[alters ]{.class_4938}["holy"]{.class_4939}[ to "godly": like Lot, perhaps (2 Pet. 2:9).]{.class_4938}

[There are ]{.class_4938}[three things ]{.class_4937}[to consider. In the first place, every Israelite was commanded to be ]{.class_4938}["holy"]{.class_4939}[ (Exod. 22:31); in the second place, Israel as a nation was ]{.class_4938}["holy"]{.class_4939}[ (Exod. 19:6); and in the third place, David can be speaking for Christ exactly as he spoke for Christ in Psalm 16:10. God took the trouble to explain that last reference in the New Testament (Acts 2:27--31) so the commentators could write intelligently. Instead of ]{.class_4938}[believing ]{.class_4937}[what they read---in both Testaments---they rigged up readings that would force the Bible to teach the ]{.class_4938}[heresy ]{.class_4937}[that salvation under the law matches salvation under grace. Typical: absolutely standard. When they can't understand the Bible, they not only refuse to confess their stupidity; they drag YOU down to THEIR level of ignorance. Standard. "Alles in Ordnung!"]{.class_4938}

[God is ]{.class_4938}["good," ]{.class_4939}[even when He seems to be our enemy (vs. 5). God is ]{.class_4938}["ready to forgive" ]{.class_4939}[even when we are stalling and putting it off. God is ]{.class_4938}["plenteous in mercy" ]{.class_4939}[even when the sinner refused to call on Him for it. He is merciful to the just and the unjust alike. Note: ]{.class_4938}["All them that call upon thee." ]{.class_4939}[Inspirationally, ]{.class_4937}[this could be ]{.class_4938}["whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved" ]{.class_4939}[(Rom. 10:13).]{.class_4938}

Verses 6 and 7 are self explanatory.

86:8 "Among the gods there is none like unto thee, O Lord; neither are there any works like unto thy works.

9 All nations whom thou had made shall come and worship before thee, O Lord; and shall glorify thy name.

10 For thou art great, and doest wondrous things: thou art God alone."

"[Among the gods" ]{.class_4939}[hearkens back to Psalm 82, which see. These "]{.class_4938}[gods"]{.class_4939}[ are certainly real and present, according to the New Testament (1 Cor. 8:5). They will show up one of these days (Matt. 24:37). Spurgeon, in pure ignorance, calls these ]{.class_4938}["gods" ]{.class_4939}[just "kings and magistrates," forgetting that the principalities and powers in ]{.class_4938}["heavenly places" ]{.class_4939}[are a good bit higher than kings (see Isa. 10:33, 24:21). Where this puts ]{.class_4938}["the god of this world" ]{.class_4939}[(2 Cor. 4:4), I have no idea, but I am sure he appreciates the support of the faculty at Liberty University. Count on the "godly scholars" to help out Satan any time they have an opportunity (see Ps. 52:3, 68:21, 80:13).]{.class_4938}

Verse 9 takes place in the Millennium (see Ps. 22:27, 47:7, 64:9, 72:10; Isa. 2:3, 11:9, 66:19--20).

"[Thou art God alone" ]{.class_4939}[in the sense of ]{.class_4938}["to us there is but one God," ]{.class_4939}[(1 Cor. 8:6). The gods of Greek mythology are the distorted picture of real ]{.class_4938}["gods" ]{.class_4939}[who were here before the flood. The statues and images of those gods are the remembrances of beings who were actually there, and Kroll's comment about them being "imaginary" is something he imagined in his imagination. There are everywhere on this earth mementos of a race of GIANTS who had supernatural powers of some kind. Their images don't show up until AFTER the flood (Gen. 31:19). You don't put up a statue of a "celebrity" until he is dead. There are several thousand of these]{.class_4938}[ "gods"]{.class_4939}[ who have yet to leave ]{.class_4938}["their first estate" ]{.class_4939}[(Jude 6; Rev. 12:4) and come to this earth with Satan (Rev. 12:7--9). Kroll just temporarily lost his mind long enough to write a commentary. It happens regularly.]{.class_4938}

86:11 "Teach me thy way, O LORD; I will walk in thy truth: unite my heart to fear thy name.

12 I will praise thee, O Lord my God, with all my heart: and I will glorify thy name for evermore.

13 For great is thy mercy toward me: and thou hast delivered my soul from the lowest hell.

14 O God, the proud are risen against me, and the assemblies of violent men have sought after my soul; and have not set thee before them.

15 But thou, O Lord, art a God full of compassion, and gracious, longsuffering, and plentous in mercy and truth.

16 O turn unto me, and have mercy upon me; give thy strength unto thy servant, and save the son of thine handmaid.

17 Shew me a token for good; that they which hate me may see it, and be ashamed: because thou, LORD, hast holpen me, and comforted me."

[There is much ]{.class_4938}[devotional ]{.class_4937}[material here.]{.class_4938}

In verse 15, God's attributes are appealed to:

[1. He is ]{.class_4938}["full of compassion."]{.class_4939}

[2. He is ]{.class_4938}["gracious. ]{.class_4939}["]{.class_4938}

[3. He is ]{.class_4938}["longsuffering."]{.class_4939}

[4. He is ]{.class_4938}["plenteous in mercy and truth."]{.class_4939}

[Here, all is positive. The Psalmist knows these things about God because he has been delivered ]{.class_4938}["from the lowest hell" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 13). It is true, spiritually, of David, and it is true, literally, of Jesus Christ, whose ]{.class_4938}["soul was not left in hell" ]{.class_4939}[(see Acts 2:31). This is privately interpreted in Spurgeon's ]{.class_4938}[Treasury ]{.class_4937}[not to mean ]{.class_4938}["hell," ]{.class_4939}[but "the direst need and deepest dishonour. " (Hello, Origen, how did you get from Alexandria to London?) How about "delivered from the HRS and high insurance rates?" If it doesn't mean what it says, how can you say what it means? How about, "Thou hast saved my bank account from an IRS audit?" The ]{.class_4938}[New King Jimmy ]{.class_4937}[can't handle it, so they adopt the reading of one of the worst unsaved Liberals in England: the character who put out the ]{.class_4938}[New English Bible.]{.class_4937}[ All the apostates refuse to translate the Hebrew and leave it as "the depths of Sheol." But David is probably writing this Psalm after Bathsheba and Psalm 51. ]{.class_4938}["Hell,"]{.class_4939}[ to him, was a ]{.class_4938}[literal ]{.class_4937}[place. ]{.class_4938}["The lowest hell" ]{.class_4939}[is mentioned in Deuteronomy 32:22, and it certainly is NOT a "dire need" or a "deep dishonour." It is a pit filled with live coals (Ps. 140:10) in ]{.class_4938}["a furnace of fire" ]{.class_4939}[(Matt. 13:42).]{.class_4938}

In the passage are a number of things that God can do for a sinner:

1\. Deliver him (vs. 13).

2\. Show him mercy (vs. 13).

3\. Help him (vs. 17).

4\. Comfort him (vs. 17).

Again David "counts his many blessings and names them one by one," in these verses, to show that someone else can get the same treatment. Meanwhile, a number of requests are made:

1\. To be taught (vs. 11).

2\. To be united to the fear of God (vs. 11).

3\. To turn to him (vs. 16).

4\. To give strength to him (vs. 16).

5\. To show him a token for good (vs. 17).

6\. To save him (vs. 16).

In return for these blessings he says he will:

1\. Walk in the truth (vs. 11).

2\. Praise the Lord (vs. 12).

3\. Glorify God's name for ever (vs. 12).

[Any one of these three subjects could be preached in the New Testament sense with Scriptural applications made to the saint who has been saved by believing ]{.class_4938}["the gospel of the grace of God" ]{.class_4939}[(Acts 20:24), but where they stand here, they are in an Old Testament context.]{.class_4938}

PSALM 87

87:1 "His foundation is in the holy mountains.

2 The LORD loveth the gates of Zion more than all the dwellings of Jacob.

3 Glorious things are spoken of thee, O city of God. Selah.

4 I will make mention of Rahab and Babylon to them that know me: behold Philistia, and Tyre, with Ethiopia; this man was born there.

5 And of Zion it shall be said, This and that man was born in her: and the highest himself shall establish her.

6 The LORD shall count, when he writeth up the people, that this man was born there. Selah.

7 As well the singers as the players on instruments shall be there: all my springs are in thee."

[Now we are in some "sure enough" trouble. Let me preface my comments by saying that at no time in the forty-five years since my conversion have I ever intimated in writing or speech that I had ever mastered "The Book." Not once have I professed to be smart enough to correct it---like every Alexandrian who ever breathed---and not once have I ever professed to understand every verse in it, or even every chapter. Those who correct it with ]{.class_4938}[ASVs, RSVs, NIVs, NRSVs, NASVs, ]{.class_4937}[Greek and Hebrew texts, etc., are the "wiseacres." They are the "scholars." I have never even professed to be a "scholar" one time since I drew breath on this earth (1921).]{.class_4938}

Here we run into one of those passages like Zechariah 1 or the Song of Solomon 6 or Isaiah 15:5. It will create more agnostics and ignoramuses in thirty seconds than the NEA can turn out of high school in a year. Any man who thinks he can nail verses 4 and 5 down is living in Disneyworld.

[Verse 1 is self explanatory. The ]{.class_4938}["His" ]{.class_4939}[is God, and the foundation is in ]{.class_4938}["the holy mountains," ]{.class_4939}[which are called such because they picture the ]{.class_4938}[Lord Himself, ]{.class_4937}[according to the similitude given in Psalm 125:2. They are called ]{.class_4938}["my mountains" ]{.class_4939}[in Isaiah 14:25. The Lord's use of the word ]{.class_4938}["holy"]{.class_4939}[ in the Bible (see Ps. 86:2) is very instructive. It is never applied to Mary or Joseph individually. It is never applied to John the Baptist. It is never applied to Rome or the Vatican. There are no "holy sacraments" in the Bible, there is no "holy church," and there cannot be found one drop of "holy water" or "holy relics" or "holy rosaries" anywhere in the New Testament. There is a ]{.class_4938}["Holy Father," ]{.class_4939}[but He is the only Holy Father in the universe (John 17:11), and there never has existed even a semblance of Him since Adam threw the deck out the window. In the Old Testament, the word ]{.class_4938}["holy"]{.class_4939}[ is applied to things that deal with the Jewish theocracy, so the first time the word ]{.class_4938}["holy"]{.class_4939}[ shows up it refers to a ]{.class_4938}[piece of real estate ]{.class_4937}[(DIRT) where Moses is commissioned to lead the nation out of Egypt (Exod. 3:5; see ]{.class_4938}[ISRAEL: A Deadly Piece of Dirt,]{.class_4937}[ BB Bookstdore, Pensacola, FL, 2001). Note that not ONE time can the word ]{.class_4938}["holy"]{.class_4939}[ in the New Testament be applied to anything more than the following:]{.class_4938}

[1. The ]{.class_4938}[Scriptures ]{.class_4937}[(2 Tim. 3:15). [4. ]{.class_4938}[Earthly Jerusalem ]{.class_4937}[(Rev. 11:2) and ]{.class_4938}[New Jerusalem ]{.class_4937}[(Rev. 21:10).]{.class_4938}

[5. A ]{.class_4938}["holy kiss" ]{.class_4939}[(not a "kiss of peace") in 1 Thessalonians 5:26.]{.class_4938}

[6. ]{.class_4938}["Holy hands" ]{.class_4939}[(1 Tim. 2:8); that is, clean hands with no blood on them (Acts 20:26). When a pope lifts up his hands, the blood streams out between his fingers and down his sleeves until he is soaked in it.]{.class_4938}

[7. The ]{.class_4938}["calling" ]{.class_4939}[of the child of God (2 Tim. 1:9).]{.class_4938}

[8. The ]{.class_4938}[body of believers ]{.class_4937}[as ]{.class_4938}["brethren" ]{.class_4939}[(1 Pet. 1:15, 16; 2:5, 9).]{.class_4938}

[9. ]{.class_4938}["The holy mount" ]{.class_4939}[in 2 Peter 1:18 as you have ]{.class_4938}["holy mountains" ]{.class_4939}[here.]{.class_4938}

[10. The ]{.class_4938}[conduct of ]{.class_4937}[the child of God (2 Pet. 3:11, Jude 1:20).]{.class_4938}

[11. ]{.class_4938}[Angels ]{.class_4937}[(Rev. 14:10),]{.class_4938}[ apostles, and prophets ]{.class_4937}[(Rev. 18:20).]{.class_4938}

[Note, in all of this, that Roman Catholics are not "holy," Rome is not "holy," the pope is not "holy," Mary is not "holy," and the Roman Catholic sacraments are not "holy." Pisgah, Mt. Hermon, Mt. Olivet, Mt. Carmel, Mt. Gilboa, Mt. Calvary, and Mount Zion (see Ps. 76:4 and comments).]{.class_4938}

Spurgeon's exegesis is "off the wall"; i.e., "The foundation of the church, which is the mystical Jerusalem, is laid in the eternal, immutable, and invincible decrees of Jehovah. He wills that the church shall be\...like the mountains round about Jerusalem\...."

[Good old Kroll takes the "s" off of ]{.class_4938}["mountains" ]{.class_4939}[and converts the plural mountains into "the hill country." (There is nothing on earth, I say: "nothing on earth," to compete with the blundering, blithering, blinding, bumfoozling baloney put out by the faculties and staffs of Liberty University, Moody, Fuller, Grace, Wheaton, Pacific Coast, PCC, or BBC. Once they correct that Book---and they all do, and do it continually---the Lord just gently removes their marbles so they can't stay in the game.) He got them from four sources: the ]{.class_4938}[NIV, RSV]{.class_4937}[, and Liv]{.class_4938}[ing Bible]{.class_4937}[---which also took the "s" off ]{.class_4938}["mountains." ]{.class_4939}[And he got his "hills" from one of the most liberal, corrupt, modernistic translations ever published by an unsaved man: the ]{.class_4938}[New English Bible.]{.class_4937}

[Not even Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown bought it. They said the word ]{.class_4938}["mountains" ]{.class_4939}[referred to the mountain range Zion was on. God's ]{.class_4938}["foundation" ]{.class_4939}[was a CITY, not a mountain (see Heb. 12:22 and 11:10, ]{.class_4938}["a city with hath foundations, whose builder and maker is God"]{.class_4939}[). This ]{.class_4938}[earthly ]{.class_4937}[Jerusalem in the Millennium is not set up until the UN troops are destroyed ]{.class_4938}["upon the mountains of Israel" ]{.class_4939}[(Ezek. 38, 39).]{.class_4938}

"[The gates of Zion" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 2). This is a CITY (Ezek. 48:22--35), not the temple. There could be no doubt about it, for the next verse says ]{.class_4938}["O city of God." ]{.class_4939}[The ]{.class_4938}["Selah" ]{.class_4939}[puts you right slap into the millennial ]{.class_4938}[earthly ]{.class_4937}[Jerusalem of Hebrews 11:10, which all the commentators (bar NONE) converted into "New Jerusalem" (see ]{.class_4938}[The Bible Believer's Commentary on Hebrews]{.class_4937}[).]{.class_4938}

Now! These are the men who are going to expound verses 4--6! Don't you know they will do a "bang up" job on it? ("Let's give a hand to our parking attendants; they're doing a great job out there tonight: a lot more 'compacts' are leaving than the ones that entered the lot.")

[Spurgeon, after spiritualizing Zion as the church, has the Postmillennial conversion of Tyre and Zidon by being "born again" in this age; no Tribulation, no Rapture, no Armageddon, no earthly Jerusalem restored, and no restoration of political Israel (]{.class_4938}[Treasury,]{.class_4937}[ p. 478). Tell us some more funny stories; we need a little humor.]{.class_4938}

Sensing that he is slipping and sliding around like a greased pig on ice, Spurgeon says (thank God!) that "the passage is not so clear that anyone should become dogmatical as to its meaning." (You tell 'em, buster; you've got the balloon.)

Observe how deadly Calvinism is when an "elected elect" by the "Sovereign Grace" of the "Sovereign God" gets "chosen before the foundation of the world." This is Charles Haddon Spurgeon---a born again child of God in the Body of Christ, bone of His bone and flesh of His flesh (Eph. 5:30): "May it be our happy lot to be numbered with the Lord's chosen, both in life and death\...let us pray then for that adoption and regeneration which will secure us a place among the heaven born"!!

Heah come Kroll wid de Coal! Rock an' Roll, man, wid de Scroll!

"[When he writeth up the people"]{.class_4939}[ (vs. 6) is a blockbuster. The saved of this age already have their names "written up" (see Phil. 4:3). The apostles before the crucifixion had their names ]{.class_4938}["written in heaven" ]{.class_4939}[(Luke 10:20), but one of them got his ]{.class_4938}[blotted out ]{.class_4937}[(John 17:12), according to a pre New Testament warning (Exod. 32:31--33). Kroll seizes the text, mops the sweat off his brow, clutches at his tie, loosens his collar, and says that "writing up the people" (vs. 6) is not anyone's NAME being written anywhere; it is rather a record of their "exceptional privileges." [Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown follow Spurgeon into the ditch. Imagine this after reading Galatians 4:26 and Revelation 21:2--9! ]{.class_4938}[Earthly Jerusalem, in this age, couldn't give life to a sick possum. ]{.class_4937}["Each man that is spiritually born derives his birth from her," bleat Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown, while citing the wrong city. The commentators are incapable of dealing with heaven. Their knowledge of ]{.class_4938}["science" ]{.class_4939}[(see 1 Tim. 6:20) incapacitated them way back in the first chapter of Genesis (see comments in that commentary on verses 1:6, 8, and 16), and they never recovered in the next sixty five books.]{.class_4938}

[Jamieson, Fausset, Brown, Dummelow, Yates, and Motyer have the city that threw Jesus Christ out and crucified Him (Matt. 27:31; Acts 2:23) as the ]{.class_4938}[spiritual mother ]{.class_4937}[of the Body of Christ! Motyer calls Zion "the mother of all men."]{.class_4938}

This time Kyle Yates hits it "on the money." Although he doesn't really believe what he is writing (since he has no concept about Premillennialism), he still grasps the fact that the Psalm is about "the FUTURE Jerusalem" as "the mother" of all peoples. He says, "as the mother or cradle of ISRAEL's future generations." This was the sense of the passage exactly, as interpreted by the Holy Spirit in the infallible ENGLISH (see Isa. 2, 60, 65, 66, and numerous other places.)

[Now, what is the exact meaning of verses 4 and 5? I will tell you. I ]{.class_4938}[don't have an idea in the world. ]{.class_4937}[All I can do is guess, but at least I will keep my guesses in line with Galatians 4:26; Hebrews 13:12--14; Revelation 22, 21:2; and Ezekiel 48. After all, that is the least any Bible student can do.]{.class_4938}

[Verse 4. God will make mention of Rahab and Babylon to Israelites who are in the Tribulation and Israelites who survive the Tribulation. ]{.class_4938}["Rahab" ]{.class_4939}[is a reference to a DRAGON, not a crocodile, as given in Kroll's fairy tale for grownups. (See Isa. 27:1 and Ps. 89:10.) The prostitute has the same name because it is a prostitute who rides this monster in Revelation 17:1--5. This prostitute is not just connected with ]{.class_4938}["Babylon" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 4); she is ]{.class_4938}["MYSTERY, BABYLON THE GREAT." ]{.class_4939}[All the commentators missed all the references. With their positive outlook, they all ]{.class_4938}[assumed ]{.class_4937}[that this was Egypt and Babylon getting converted to Christ. They did this by skipping down to verse 6 and pretending that verse 6 was a reference to Egypt and Babylon getting into the "book of life." ]{.class_4938}[Egypt is devastated with famine during the Millennium: see Zechariah 14:17--19.]{.class_4937}

"[Behold Philistia and Tyre with Ethiopia" ]{.class_4939}[is uniformly translated as "Look AT Philistia, " etc. Why it never occurred to them to make it "Look Philistia," etc., as a ]{.class_4938}[command, ]{.class_4937}[no one knows. The trouble is ]{.class_4938}["this man," ]{.class_4939}[and that is why Spurgeon got rid of him. Lynchburg assists and removes him. The ]{.class_4938}[NKJV ]{.class_4937}[assists and removes him. The ]{.class_4938}[ASV ]{.class_4937}[and ]{.class_4938}[NASV ]{.class_4937}[cheerfully cooperate, converting ]{.class_4938}["this man" ]{.class_4939}[to "this one."]{.class_4938}

I smell an ecumenical movement here that reminds me of Psalm 84:5.

[RSV ]{.class_4937}[and ]{.class_4938}[NIV]{.class_4937}[? No ]{.class_4938}["man."]{.class_4939}[ Living Bible? No ]{.class_4938}["man."]{.class_4939}[ Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown? No ]{.class_4938}["man."]{.class_4939}[ They say the word was a figure for Philistia and Tyre, etc. Motyer and Yates agree: one hundred percent agreement among the "majority of recognized scholars," right? Okay, do you know what to do when 100 percent of the qualified authorities agree on altering or omitting a word in the Holy Bible? I know what to do.]{.class_4938}

[First, look at the text carefully, and this time instead of being swept away with the incoming tide of allegorizing, spiritualizing, Bible-correcting "experts," READ: ]{.class_4938}["And of Zion it shall be said, This and that man was born in her."]{.class_4939}[ The]{.class_4938}[ "her"]{.class_4939}[ of verse 5 is NOT the ]{.class_4938}["Rahab" ]{.class_4939}[of verse 4. Somebody was born in Tyre, Babylon, Ethiopia, or Philistia that was NOT born in Zion. Do you see that? ]{.class_4938}["This man" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 4) was NOT born in Zion. That is exactly the place where he was NOT born, so all of the commentators say that he was, but since they have a defiled conscience (from having messed with the Book to start with), they gaily "liberate" the male down to a neuter: "ONE." ]{.class_4938}["This man," ]{.class_4939}[eliminated from verse 4, reappears in verse 6, but neither man is Jesus Christ; He was not born in Zion. Zion ]{.class_4938}[rejected ]{.class_4937}[Him (Matt. 23:37--39) and threw Him off of Mt. Zion (Heb. 13:12--13). In the text, the first man (vs. 4) is born in BABYLON. Philistia, Tyre, and Ethiopia are to take note of that because they came from the same Hamitic stock (see Gen. 10:6--14; Isa. 23; Joel 3:4). Tyre, as Rahab, is a prostitute (Isa. 23:15--17). The ]{.class_4938}["this man" ]{.class_4939}[of verse 6 is born in Zion (Note: ]{.class_4938}["and of Zion it shall be said\..."]{.class_4939}[), and if he is an individual he is the ]{.class_4938}["anointed" ]{.class_4939}[one of Lamentations 4:20---the ]{.class_4938}["man child" ]{.class_4939}[of Revelation 12:5, 13. ]{.class_4938}["Selah" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 6).]{.class_4938}

"[When he writeth up the people" ]{.class_4939}[is mentioned in Malachi 3:16, although not one commentator, with all of his talk about "written records," could find the direct reference in the same Testament. ]{.class_4938}["As well the singers as the players on instruments" ]{.class_4939}[are certainly saved Israelites rejoicing after the Advent; according to Psalm 68:25. Ethiopia may ]{.class_4938}["stretch out her hands unto God" ]{.class_4939}[(Ps. 68:31) in the Millennium, and thereby get some names recorded---which have not been recorded yet (see vs. 6), but that does not explain her connection with Tyre and Rahab in this Psalm.]{.class_4938}

[I agree with Spurgeon on not being "dogmatic" about the Psalm, but my guess is that Ethiopia (Ezek. 30:5, 38:5), Philistia, and Tyre are commanded to notice something; the fact that the Son of Perdition has Hamitic blood in him and comes from ]{.class_4938}[Babylon ]{.class_4937}[(as Nebuchadnezzar) and is connected with a dragon and a harlot (Rev. 17). My guess is ]{.class_4938}["this man" ]{.class_4939}[of verse 6 is the individual who opposes him in Daniel's Seventieth Week while ]{.class_4938}["this and that man" ]{.class_4939}[is a plural reference to Yates' "generations" that will come out of Jerusalem in the Millennium. If you can do better, try it; but don't make the ghastly mistake of thinking that earthly Jerusalem since A.D. 70 has ever been connected with the regeneration of one Jew or Gentile on the face of this earth.]{.class_4938}

[]{#Uxd5JyNYSWCtgFM2T9UhJQ1113.xhtml}

PSALM 88

We are nearing the end of the third collection of the Psalms, and not until now has the Holy Spirit ever left the theme of the Second Advent and the Millennium. The last Psalm in this collection will go right into Jesus Christ's return to reign, visibly on this earth at Jerusalem, on David's throne.

[Here we briefly deal with ]{.class_4938}[three ]{.class_4937}[persons. (1) ]{.class_4938}[Jesus Christ ]{.class_4937}[Himself in His sufferings, death, burial, and resurrection. (2) A Jew in the Tribulation. (3) Any ]{.class_4938}[saint ]{.class_4937}[in deep distress who is "at the end of his rope." This must never be confused with so called "pressures" and "tensions" and "youth conflicts" and all of the modern American middle-class garbage that have become preaching themes for the Horton-Swindoll-MacArthur-Bob Jones IV crowd. I am speaking of ]{.class_4938}[real trouble, ]{.class_4937}[not high blood pressure, cranky husbands, bossy wives, disobedient children, irregular eating and sleeping habits, peer presure, or "coping" with teenagers. I am talking about a saint out in the Red Sea up to his NOSE in the water, without any sign of anything "parting" in any direction, and Pharaoh's army on the beach behind him.]{.class_4938}

88:1 "O LORD God of my salvation, I have cried day and night before thee:

2 Let my prayer come before thee: incline thine ear unto my cry;

3 For my soul is full of troubles; and my life draweth nigh unto the grave.

4 I am counted with them that go down into the pit: I am as a man that hath no strength:

5 Free among the dead, like the slain that lie in the grave, whom thou rememberest no more: and they are cut off from thy hand.

6 Thou hast laid me in the lowest pit, in darkness, in the deeps.

7 Thy wrath Heth hard upon me, and thou hast afflicted me with all thy waves. Selah."

[The Psalm is attributed to ]{.class_4938}["Heman the Ezrahite." ]{.class_4939}[This is the Heman of 1 Kings 4:31. He was a Kohathite, whereas Ethan was a "Merarite" (1 Chron. 6:44). This means they were not ]{.class_4938}[brothers ]{.class_4937}[(see also 1 Chron. 2:6). The Psalm means "concerning afflictive sickness" (]{.class_4938}[Mahalath Leannoth]{.class_4937}[), at least according to Alexander (]{.class_4938}[Treasury,]{.class_4937}[ p. 2).]{.class_4938}

The condition of the afflicted man here is critical. He is described as a man dying of an incurable disease or a prisoner enduring torture or a man thrown into a dungeon for life. No such flimsy thing is discussed here as making your car payments, keeping your gas and water turned on, getting along with your mother in law, satisfying your wife, or "coping with nervous tension." The sinner here is in terrifying circumstances.

[1. He is crying ]{.class_4938}["day and night"]{.class_4939}[ (vs. 1).]{.class_4938}

2\. His life is drawing near the grave (vs. 3).

3\. He has already been buried as far as his friends are concemed (vs. 4).

4\. He is underground in darkness, and the wrath of God lies on him (vss. 6--8).

"[Selah"]{.class_4939}[---Second Coming. He is describing, among other things, the condition of Israelites in Daniel's Seventieth Week.]{.class_4938}

[Notice how many of these words apply to Jesus Christ bearing our sins, taking the wrath of God into His bosom, and then descending into Hell after being deposited in the tomb (vss. 2, 4, 6, and 7 in particular). Note particularly the great ]{.class_4938}["deeps"]{.class_4939}[ in verse 6, where ]{.class_4938}[drowning]{.class_4937}[ is, again, a type of the wrath of God poured out on the sinner (Ps. 42:7 and comments with 18:16 and 69:1). He was saved from DEATH (Heb. 5:7) when he ]{.class_4938}["offered up prayers and supplications ]{.class_4939}[\[look at the entire Psalm\] ]{.class_4938}[with strong crying and tears\...."]{.class_4939}[ There is no doubt about the Messianic applications to the First Advent. Spurgeon lightly mentions the matter, indirectly, but if ever a Christ-exalting preacher had a chance to make applications to the First Advent it is HERE.]{.class_4938}

88:8 "Thou hast put away mine acquaintance far from me; thou hast made me an abomination unto them: I am shut up, and I cannot come forth.

9 Mine eye mourneth by reason of affliction: LORD, I have called daily upon thee, I have stretched out my bands unto thee.

10 Wilt thou shew wonders to the dead? shall the dead arise and praise thee? Selah."

[The Psalmist is likened to a man who: (1) Is in prison and cannot get out. (2) Is about to die and stay dead. (3) Those who could help them have been removed from all contact and communication with him. From a ]{.class_4938}[devotional ]{.class_4937}[standpoint---to fulfill the conditions described---one would have to picture a "lifer" in "solitary" without "benefit of attorney," dying of AIDS, and no doctor to help him. That is the picture if you project it into the twentieth century. The commentators cannot even attempt to apply the terminology. Typical of the shallow, helpless, sterile, and ineffective comments on the text are those of Liberty University, Motyer, and Yates:]{.class_4938}

"[Distress has reached critical proportions\...faith greets each new day with prayer, in spite of the fact that he is utterly perplexed\...the situation which prompts this tireless prayer is in general that his troubled life is about to terminate in death\...he complains about the wrath of God, the alienation of his friends and his bitter personal distress\...he seems to feel that the Lord no longer remembers him\...his petition becomes more impassioned with each outcry\...he makes no further request but leaves his burden with the Lord\... with trouble filled soul\...weak and feeble\...Heman felt that God had smitten him mortally and laid him among the corpses\...suspected of being unclean, the Psalmist was banished from his people\...assumed the appropriate position of one who calls earnestly unto God\...doubts that God will show the glories of His grace to those who are in the grave\...a euphemism for Sheol\...laid to rest in the depths of calamity where no sunlight ray of hope can ever reach."]{.class_4938}

Bunk, baloney, bat feathers, and "bushwa" (American, circa 1920).

[No Christ, no sufferings of Christ, no connection with any of the rest of the Messianic Psalms, no practical application to anyone reading the comments, and destruction of truth after truth by calling ]{.class_4938}["the deeps" ]{.class_4939}[and the ]{.class_4938}["waves" ]{.class_4939}[the "depths of calamity," and making ]{.class_4938}["the lowest pit" ]{.class_4939}[the "deepest calamity\...metaphorically." (Yeah, and "so's your old man!")]{.class_4938}

[Heman was never, one time in his life, in the condition described, and neither was David, and neither was Asaph or Korah. You could only compare it to the Albigenses and Waldenses being buried alive in the Dark Ages, or being imprisoned in an underground dungeon below water level---which ]{.class_4938}[happened ]{.class_4937}[in the Dark Ages. The man so afflicted cannot get a trial, he cannot contact a lawyer, he has no physician for his wounds or disease; in truth, he would be starving to death in DARKNESS. His prayers are bounding off the ceiling (vss. 1, 9, 13) and are NOT getting answered. A milder picture of it would be a man in a field hospital on the battlefield, mortally wounded, and having no anesthesia to ease his pain, no personal friends present, the bombardment continuing while the "medics" are running for their lives and leaving the wounded to be captured, killed, or mutilated. These things actually happen to people. In World War II, Japanese skinned American boys ]{.class_4938}[alive ]{.class_4937}[at night and left them hanging on trees with their genitals cut off and stuffed in their mouths. In Nam, GI's were found lying staked out by rice paddies with all of the skin removed from their backs and chest. In France, in the 1200s and 1300s, they would pitch Christians into an underground dungeon shaped like an inverted funnel and then slam a concrete lid on the top of it. The victims would fall twenty to thirty feet onto a pile of dead and dying bodies lying in complete darkness, without a ray of light coming in from any direction. They would then rot with the rotting bodies, without one chance of getting any kind of help from anyone. ]{.class_4938}[Their prayers accomplished nothing.]{.class_4937}

[This is what is going on in Psalm 88, although it is even more horrible than that, for here when the Psalmist speaks for Christ, he adds the judgment of God against a sinner in hell (vss. 7, 16). You will find the same thing in Lamentations 3:1--15 and Job 30:15--20. ]{.class_4938}["Selah" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 10). The passage also has some reference to Tribulation saints who are killed (Rev. 6:9--11). Spurgeon can't find the truth. Robert Bolton and Albert Barnes can't find it. Martin Bucer and Adam Clarke can't find it. Thomas Goodwin and John Welch can't find it. Lange, Dickson, Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown can't find it. And Duhm, Davis, Briggs, Hengstenberg, DeWette, Hupfeld, and Hitzig could find a pecan tree on Mt. McKinley before they could find it. Kroll actually dumps verses 11--18 on ]{.class_4938}[Heman ]{.class_4937}[without making ]{.class_4938}[one ]{.class_4937}[reference to Jesus Christ. (This is the kind of "Christ exalting" exegesis that one gets when one deals with "militant Fundamentalists" who believe in the "Deity of Christ.")]{.class_4938}

88:11 "Shall thy lovingkindness be declared in the grave? or thy faithfulness in destruction?

12 Shall thy wonders be known in the dark? and thy righteousness in the land of forgetfulness?

13 But unto thee have I cried, O LORD; and in the morning shall my prayer prevent thee.

14 LORD, why castest thou off my soul? why hidest thou thy face from me?

15 I am afflicted and ready to die from my youth up: while I suffer thy terrors I am distracted.

16 Thy fierce wrath goeth over me; thy terrors have cut me off.

17 They came round about me daily like water; they compassed me about together.

18 Lover and friend hast thou put far from me, and mine acquaintance into darkness."

Five appeals are made to God for an answer to prayer.

1\. Can a dead man talk about God's lovingkindness? No.

2\. Can a dead man talk about God's faithfulness? No.

3\. Can a man in darkness (see Job 10:21, 17:13) find out what God is doing? No.

4\. Can a man know anything about God's righteousness if he stays in the grave? No.

[5. Why should God cast my soul off and hide His face from me? NOTE: ]{.class_4938}["My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?" ]{.class_4939}[(Matt. 27:46).]{.class_4938}

"[Thy fierce wrath goeth over me; thy terrors\...daily like water" ]{.class_4939}[(vss. 16--17). Compare: ]{.class_4938}["He hath made him to be sin for us\...being made a curse for us\...bare our sins in his own body on the tree\...." ]{.class_4939}[Robert Hawker is the one man in Spurgeon's ]{.class_4938}[Treasury ]{.class_4937}[who grasps the truth that the ]{.class_4938}[main theme ]{.class_4937}[of the Psalm is the sufferings of Christ (p. 18). This is offset by ten full pages of devotional applications that avoid the direct ]{.class_4938}[doctrinal ]{.class_4937}[references. The Psalm is nearly as good a Crucifixion Psalm as Psalm 22, although it goes into more detail on Christ's actual "exercises of the soul" in Gethsamene and in ]{.class_4938}["the heart of the earth" ]{.class_4939}[(Matt. 12:40). Again, the supernatural elements knock the Fundamental Baptists completely out of their sockets. They will not believe that a dead man in ]{.class_4938}["the lowest pit" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 6) could be praying, although that is where Jonah prayed. Jonah is not just in a whale's belly; he is in ]{.class_4938}["hell" ]{.class_4939}[(Jonah 2:1--2).]{.class_4938}

"[Unbelief in the Book" is the name of the game. Fundamentalists foremost, then Evangelicals, then Conservatives, then Catholics, then Liberals, then atheists.]{.class_4938}

[If it takes ]{.class_4938}[ten ]{.class_4937}[cans of beer to make a young man drunk, then he is one tenth drunk after the ]{.class_4938}[first ]{.class_4937}[can.]{.class_4938}

[When it comes to ]{.class_4938}[apostasy, ]{.class_4937}[the apostates who meet in the "Congress of Fundamentalism" are a good mile away from Norman Vincent Peale and Karl Marx, but a mile is just as good as a FOOT, and those one hundred yards away (Glasser, Bakunin, and Shaw) are just a little closer in to atheism than those a quarter of a mile out (Peale, Fosdick, Tillich, Pike, Neibhur, et al.) or those a half mile out (Westcott, Hort, Beecher, Emerson, Dickens, Franklin, Schweitzer) or three-fourths of a mile out (Machen, Warfield, Schaff, Green, Dummelow, et al.). But a miss is as good as a mile.]{.class_4938}

[If you want to learn the Bible, the first thing you need to learn is that a man who ]{.class_4938}[messes with it ]{.class_4937}[does so because he has ]{.class_4938}[not ]{.class_4937}[learned it.]{.class_4938}

[]{#Uxd5JyNYSWCtgFM2T9UhJQ1165.xhtml}

PSALM 89

[This is the last Psalm in the third collection. It is usually given this position because the ending on the Psalm ]{.class_4938}[("Blessed be the Lord for evermore. Amen, and Amen," ]{.class_4939}[vs. 52) matches the ending on the first section (]{.class_4938}["Blessed be the LORD God of Israel\...Amen, and Amen," ]{.class_4939}[Ps. 41:13), and the second section (]{.class_4938}["Amen, and Amen," ]{.class_4939}[Ps. 72:19). There is no double "amen and amen" to the fourth section (Ps. 106:48) but only ]{.class_4938}["Blessed be the LORD God\...Amen. Praise the Lord."]{.class_4939}

[We will not have much trouble with the Scholar's Union in Psalm 89, as it is so obviously ]{.class_4938}[Messianic ]{.class_4937}[that the clumsiest expositor can get thorough it without too many "pit stops" and refueling stations. The speaker sometimes will be Ethan the Ezrahite, at other times the Lord Jesus Christ, and at other times God the Father.]{.class_4938}

89:1 "I will sing of the mercies of the LORD for ever: with my mouth will I make known thy faithfulness to all generations.

2 For I have said, Mercy shall be built up for ever: thy faithfulness shalt thou establish in the very heavens.

3 I have made a covenant with my chosen, I have sworn unto David my servant,

4 Thy seed will I establish for ever, and build up thy throne to all generations. Selah."

[The Psalmist is speaking in the first verse. It is self-explanatory. But verse 2 can come out of two mouths, the first clause being God Himself and the second one being the Psalmist's acknowledgement of that statement. This is not apparent to the commentators, who all assume that ]{.class_4938}[Ethan ]{.class_4937}[is saying ]{.class_4938}["I have said."]{.class_4939}[ But why Ethan would give himself credit in the first person for saying this is rather obscure. In what follows---]{.class_4938}["I have made a covenant\...I have laid help\...I have exalted one chosen\...I have found David my servant\...I will beat down his foes\...I will set his hand\...I will make him my firstborn\...I ]{.class_4939}[\[will\]]{.class_4938}[ make to endure forever\...I will not lie unto David\....etc." ]{.class_4939}[(vss. 3, 19, 20, 23, 25, 27, 29, 35, etc.)---it is apparent that ]{.class_4938}["I"]{.class_4939}[ ]{.class_4938}[is not ]{.class_4937}[Ethan. The word ]{.class_4938}["I"]{.class_4939}[ doesn't occur one time in the next fourteen verses (vss. 5--18), so if Ethan said ]{.class_4938}["Mercy shall be built up for ever" ]{.class_4939}[as his own personal opinion, it is rather strange that he then shut his mouth and never gave another personal opinion again until verse 50.]{.class_4938}

[Verse 2 is clear ]{.class_4938}[doctrinally, ]{.class_4937}[although all of the commentators spiritualize faithfulness being established ]{.class_4938}["in the very heavens." ]{.class_4939}[They forget that the heavens right ]{.class_4938}[now ]{.class_4937}[are inhabited by ]{.class_4938}["principalities," "powers," ]{.class_4939}[and ]{.class_4938}["spiritual wickedness" ]{.class_4939}[(Eph. 6:10--12), so they blow a fuse again. It is the supernatural element in Scripture that always short-circuits them. God did not say that ]{.class_4938}["it was good" ]{.class_4939}[on the ]{.class_4938}[second day ]{.class_4937}[of creation (Gen. 1:7), because there were "gods" out there who will disappear in Revelation 20:11 (see Jer. 10:11). At that time, God's faithfulness will be established ]{.class_4938}["in the very heavens"]{.class_4939}[ (note the plural). Spurgeon puts it into ]{.class_4938}[the present]{.class_4937}[---which, of course, is where it does NOT belong.]{.class_4938}

[Verses 3 and 4 are self explanatory and are described in 2 Samuel 7:12--16. Observe the ]{.class_4938}["Selah," ]{.class_4939}[telling us that Solomon as ]{.class_4938}["the son of David" ]{.class_4939}[(2 Chron. 1:1) was NOT the doctrinal fulfillment of the covenant given in 2 Samuel. This covenant cannot be completely realized until the Second Advent; ]{.class_4938}["Selah" ]{.class_4939}[again is the marker. (All the commentators missed it.)]{.class_4938}

[Kroll, at this point, is slicker and smoother than a greased eel. After telling you the ]{.class_4938}[truth ]{.class_4937}[about the doctrinal application, he slips this in: "Jesus, the Seed of David, who is the Christ and ]{.class_4938}[in whom ]{.class_4937}[the everlasting Kingdom would be preserved"---not "show up," not "begin with the Second Advent." Beautiful, isn't it? That is a masterpiece if you ever saw one. Kroll's duplicity implies: (1) the Kingdom ]{.class_4938}[began ]{.class_4937}[with David and was preserved by Jesus Christ. (2) The everlasting Kingdom began at Christ's First Advent. Neither implication is true. ]{.class_4938}["Thy throne" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 4) is ]{.class_4938}[David's throne ]{.class_4937}[(Luke 1:30--33), and the Kingdom most certainly was NOT built up ]{.class_4938}["to all generations" ]{.class_4939}[with Solomon or AFTER Solomon. Nothing was "preserved." The Kingdom was torn to pieces (1 Kings 12--13), and the throne was DUMPED (Jer. 22:30; Lam. 5:16; 2 Kings 25:2--5). Kroll worded it so you could assume three different positions, but one of these would be Postmillennial because he said the everlasting Kingdom would be preserved (not set up) "IN Christ" (not "BY" Christ).]{.class_4938}

See how it's done? Just as smooth and slick as the rink in the Joe Lewis Arena (Detroit) after the Zamboni has circled the ice.

[The word for ]{.class_4938}["sworn," ]{.class_4939}[in Hebrew, means to "seven" yourself; to put yourself under seven. It also means "to bind in bundles." (See our publications on ]{.class_4938}[The Seven Mysteries, The Seven Baptisms, The Seven Resurrections, ]{.class_4937}[and ]{.class_4938}[The Seven Sevens]{.class_4937}[.)]{.class_4938}

89:5 "And the heavens shall praise thy wonders, O LORD: thy faithfulness also in the congregation of the saints.

6 For who in the heaven can be compared unto the LORD? who among the sons of the mighty can be likened unto the LORD?

7 God is greatly to be feared in the assembly of the saints, and to be had in reverence of all them that are about him.

8 O LORD God of hosts, who is a strong LORD like unto thee? or to thy faithfulness round about thee?

9 Thou rulest the raging of the sea: when the waves thereof arise, thou stillest them.

10 Thou hast broken Rahab in pieces, as one that is slain; thou hast scattered thine enemies with thy strong arm."

"[The heavens shall praise thy wonders." ]{.class_4939}[Compare this to Psalm 148:1--6 and Psalm 77:11, 14.]{.class_4938}

[God's faithfulness will be praised ]{.class_4938}["in the congregation of the saints." ]{.class_4939}[These are the saints of Daniel 7:18, called also ]{.class_4938}["the assembly of the saints" ]{.class_4939}[in verse 7. They are located not only on earth after the Tribulation but up in Glory on]{.class_4938}[ "Mount Sion" ]{.class_4939}[(Heb. 12:22) with ]{.class_4938}["an innumerable company of angels" ]{.class_4939}[and ]{.class_4938}["the general assembly and church of the firstborn" ]{.class_4939}[(Heb. 12:23).]{.class_4938}

[Out the bay window goes Kroll again. He cannot grasp anything INVISIBLE. He limits all the expressions to the saints on earth, and having done this, he converts ]{.class_4938}["the sons of the mighty" ]{.class_4939}[to princes on earth, in complete violation of Isaiah 24:21. There were five classes associated with the LORD in the context:]{.class_4938}

[1. ]{.class_4938}["The congregation of the saints" ]{.class_4939}[(on earth, vs. 5).]{.class_4938}

[2. ]{.class_4938}["The assembly of the saints" ]{.class_4939}[(in heaven, vs. 7).]{.class_4938}

[3. The ]{.class_4938}["sons of the mighty" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 6).]{.class_4938}

[4. Those that are ]{.class_4938}["about" ]{.class_4939}[God in His presence (heaven or ]{.class_4938}[the tabernacle, ]{.class_4937}[vs. 7).]{.class_4938}

[It is the gatherings of 1 Kings 22:19--23 and Job 1:6 that Kroll and his Alexandrian buddies can't "cut."
