89:11 "The heavens are thine, the earth also is thine: as for the world and the fulness thereof, thou hast founded them.

12 The north and the south thou hast created them: Tabor and Hermon shall rejoice in thy name.

13 Thou hast a mighty arm: strong is thy hand, and high is thy right hand.

14 Justice and judgment are the habitation of thy throne: mercy and truth shall go before thy face.

15 Blessed is the people that know the joyful sound: they shall walk, O LORD, in the light of thy countenance.

16 In thy name shall they rejoice all the day: and in thy righteousness shall they be exalted.

17 For thou art the glory of their strength: and in thy favour our horn shall be exalted.

18 For the LORD is our defence; and the Holy One of Israel is our king."

[Verse 11 is referred to in 1 Corinthians 10:26 (see comments in that commentary), and along with it the statement that they belong to US by virtue of being ]{.class_4938}["joint heirs with Christ" ]{.class_4939}[(Rom. 8:17, 1 Cor. 3:22--23). Note that the heavens and earth are the personal possession of a living Person. They are ]{.class_4938}[not ]{.class_4937}[the products of "mother nature" or evolution or reoccurring "big bangs" and "collapses" that keep everything in perpetuity. God made them (Isa. 42:5; Mark 13:19), so they are His. In them, man is a passenger or a pilgrim or a sojourner or a fugitive or a boarder or a resident or a stranger, but he is not the ]{.class_4938}[creator ]{.class_4937}[or owner, and never will be.]{.class_4938}

"[The north and the south\...Tabor and Hermon" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 12). ]{.class_4938}[Tabor ]{.class_4937}[is on the west side of Jordan with ]{.class_4938}[Mt. Hermon ]{.class_4937}[on the east, so the idea is that God Himself determined all four points of the compass (see Ps. 75:6), and two of them are fixed: north and south. Two more vary with the rotation of the earth (see Ps. 103:12) so that they never come together at any point. God's vertical line on this earth separating east from west is a vertical line drawn from the North Pole to the South Pole through Galilee and the Jordan River, with ]{.class_4938}[Hermon ]{.class_4937}[on the east and ]{.class_4938}[Tabor ]{.class_4937}[on the west. This line is a "continental rift" which goes through the Dead Sea (1,300 feet below sea level) and on into Africa down to Queen Victoria Falls.]{.class_4938}

[The strong]{.class_4938}[ "arm"]{.class_4939}[ ]{.class_4938}[and ]{.class_4937}["right hand" ]{.class_4939}[of verse 13 are figurative references to the Lord Jesus Christ.]{.class_4938}

[The ]{.class_4938}["high" ]{.class_4939}[hand is the one of Exodus 14:8 and Numbers 33:3. The gesture is one of power and authority (see Ps. 21:8). ]{.class_4938}["The habitation" ]{.class_4939}[of God's throne is built by the attributes which surround it. In the eternal sense, it is the throne of Revelation 20:11 and Revelation 3:21 and refers to justice and judgment being dispensed from there, not only in the absolute sense of Acts 17:31, but in the ]{.class_4938}[historical ]{.class_4937}[sense of Daniel 4:17, 32 and Psalm 103:19. His ]{.class_4938}["face"]{.class_4939}[ is an anthropomorphic expression, but still has very real application: see comments under Psalm 85:10.]{.class_4938}

"[The joyful sound\...the light of thy countenance" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 15) escapes all of the commentators. If they lived in the Tribulation, it would probably escape them then (see Heb. 9:28, 12:25; Matt. 25:8--12, 5:8). The reference is to a Mid  or Post-tribulation appearance of Jesus Christ, as He appeared to Paul. As then, there will be some who don't ]{.class_4938}["know" ]{.class_4939}[the sound: look at Acts 22:8--9. Kroll can't find one single reference in either Testament, with Psalms 31:16; 67:1; 80:1, 3, 7, 19; 44:3, and Isaiah 9:2 right in front of him---in English. First, Kroll thinks it is Jews making a noise at Jewish festivals in the Old Testament, and then, completely in the dark and completely at a loss to say anything, he piously notes: "there is a real sense in which only those who have experienced the grace of God have heard the joyful sound."]{.class_4938}

Kroll has been reading a hymn book instead of the Bible.

[Do you think that all those who have experienced the grace of God ]{.class_4938}["walk\...in the light of thy countenance"]{.class_4939}[? Beside that, ]{.class_4938}["the light of thy countenance" ]{.class_4939}[is a Light that is PHYSICALLY brighter than the sun (Acts 26:13, see Ps. 4:6). The ]{.class_4938}["light of thy countenance" ]{.class_4939}[in Psalm 44:3 is God appearing to Moses and appearing to Joshua preparatory to the conquest of Palestine (Josh. 5:13). Furthermore, there is a visible ]{.class_4938}["pillar of FIRE"]{.class_4939}[ before they ever get to Jordan (Exod. 13:21).]{.class_4938}

There are a number of things about God mentioned in the passage:

[2. His ]{.class_4938}["right hand" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 13).]{.class_4938}

[3. His ]{.class_4938}["face"]{.class_4939}[ (vs. 14).]{.class_4938}

[4. His ]{.class_4938}["countenance" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 15).]{.class_4938}

[5. His ]{.class_4938}["righteousness" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 16).]{.class_4938}

[6. His ]{.class_4938}["favour" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 17).]{.class_4938}

[7. His ]{.class_4938}["holy one" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 19).]{.class_4938}

[Kroll, who always can be counted on to leaven a passage as much as he can without destroying it, carefully avoids all expressions which would make the Holy One the King ]{.class_4938}[of Israel. ]{.class_4937}[Instead, he piously generalizes with this: "He who protects should also govern; he who defends is acknowledged as king." This was copied out of Spurgeon's ]{.class_4938}[Treasury ]{.class_4937}[(pg. 28) where the same writer says that the word ]{.class_4938}["Israel" ]{.class_4939}[means the ]{.class_4938}[elect in the church age: ]{.class_4937}["Who among the saints will not rejoice in the God of election"---]{.class_4938}[not Israel.]{.class_4937}[ You can see why Liberty University is so mealy mouthed.]{.class_4938}

89:19 "Then thou spakest in vision to thy holy one, and saidst, I have laid help upon one that is mighty; I have exalted one chosen out of the people.

20 I have found David my servant; with my holy oil have I anointed him:

21 With whom my hand shall be established: mine arm also shall strengthen him.

22 The enemy shall not exact upon him; nor the son of wickedness afflict him.

23 And I will beat down his foes before his face, and plague them that hate him.

24 But my faithfulness and my mercy shall be with him: and in my name shall his horn be exalted."

"[Then thou spakest in vision to thy holy one." ]{.class_4939}[If it is ]{.class_4938}[historical]{.class_4937}[, it is an indirect vision communicated to David by Nathan (2 Sam. 7:4). The problem is that Nathan says nothing to David about God "laying help" upon someone that is "mighty." To the contrary, Nathan's message to David is that God found somebody who was NOBODY (2 Sam. 7:8, 16). There isn't one word in the Davidic covenant about God "finding David" and anointing him. These words are not addressed to DAVID. God is telling someone else what He has done with David. The One to whom He is speaking is called ]{.class_4938}["thy holy one" ]{.class_4939}[(see Ps. 16:10). The best you can do is say that the message God gave Nathan had more in it than what He gave David, because what follows cannot be applied to David ]{.class_4938}[doctrinally ]{.class_4937}[at all, at least not in verses 26, 27, 29, 36, and 37. "David," from verse 25 on, is the ]{.class_4938}[Lord Jesus Christ. ]{.class_4937}[You might give Nathan credit for writing the content of the Psalm from verses 19 through 24, and Ethan credit for writing the rest of the Psalm.]{.class_4938}

[All of verses 20--23 are true of David's reign and his battles. He doesn't lose one battle in a lifetime, sharing this rare achievement with Alexander the Great, Oliver Cromwell, and Charlemagne. Napoleon, Rommel, Field Marshall Ney, Jeb Stuart, Meade, McClellan, Lee, and Pershing didn't make out that well. Patton and MacArthur half achieved, but Patton didn't lead the cavalry charges as David did, and neither did "Dugout Doug." David's ]{.class_4938}["horn" ]{.class_4939}[is exalted (2 Sam. 8:5--6; 10:17--19).]{.class_4938}

89:25 "I will set his hand also in the sea, and his right hand in the rivers.

26 He shall cry unto me, Thou art my father, my God, and the rock of my salvation.

27 Also I will make him my firstborn, higher than the kings of the earth.

28 My mercy will I keep for him for evermore, and my covenant shall stand fast with him.

29 His seed also will I make to endure for ever, and his throne as the days of heaven.

30 If his children forsake my law, and walk not in my judgments;

31 If they break my statutes, and keep not my commandments;

32 Then will I visit their transgression with the rod, and their iniquity with stripes.

33 Nevertheless my lovingkindness win I not utterly take from him, nor suffer my faithfulness to fail.

34 My covenant will I not break, nor alter the thing that is gone out of my lips.

35 Once have I sworn by my holiness that I will not lie unto David.

36 His seed shall endure for ever, and his throne as the sun before me.

37 It shall be established for ever as the moon, and as a faithful witness in heaven. Selah."

[Everything is clear. Christ is not only ]{.class_4938}["the son of David" ]{.class_4939}[(Matt. 1:1; Mark 10:47--48, 12:35; and Luke 20:41, but ]{.class_4938}["David," ]{.class_4939}[in this context.]{.class_4938}

[The territory promised to Abraham (Gen. 15:18--21) becomes David's ]{.class_4938}[historically ]{.class_4937}[(2 Sam. 8:3) and Christ's pro]{.class_4938}[phetically ]{.class_4937}[(see Ezek. 47--48). ]{.class_4938}["Thou art my father" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 26) is not true of David. There is no new birth in the Old Testament, for all were born in Adam's image, lived with Adam's image, and died with Adam's image (1 Cor. 15:22). ]{.class_4938}["My father" ]{.class_4939}[is not ]{.class_4938}["Our Father" ]{.class_4939}[(Matt. 6:9); the latter designating the Father of Israel. Kroll, as anxious as Curtis Hutson or John Rice to prove that Old Testament salvation (under the law) is equivalent to New Testament salvation (under grace), says that verse 26 was fulfilled in 2 Samuel 7:14, which is not even AIMED at David, let alone ]{.class_4938}[fulfilled ]{.class_4937}[by him. Second Samuel 7:14 is addressed to David's son. ]{.class_4938}["The son of David" ]{.class_4939}[(Matt. 1:1) is Jesus Christ.]{.class_4938}

[Observe that at Liberty University, as at Bob Jones University and BBC, nobody can identify ]{.class_4938}[anything. ]{.class_4937}[Solomon would have been the historical fulfillment, but Solomon never prayed ]{.class_4938}["my father" ]{.class_4939}[when he prayed. Solomon prays one of the longest prayers in the entire Bible (1 Kings 8, nearly fifty verses!), and the expression ]{.class_4938}["my father" ]{.class_4939}[doesn't occur one time in the prayer. Nor does it occur one time in the books of Ecclesiastes or Proverbs as a reference to God. No ]{.class_4938}[one ]{.class_4937}[in the Old Testament knows anything about the father son relationship of individuals after a new birth; not even the ]{.class_4938}["'master of Israel" ]{.class_4939}[of John 3:5--10.]{.class_4938}

[Kroll is being droll. Verse 26 wasn't fulfilled one time before the birth of Christ. Note two items in verse 26: ]{.class_4938}["MY father"---]{.class_4939}[referring to His ]{.class_4938}[deity ]{.class_4937}[(the Son of God---and]{.class_4938}[ "my God"]{.class_4939}[---referring to His ]{.class_4938}[humanity ]{.class_4937}[as the Son of Man. This explains the cry from Calvary recorded not as "My FATHER, my FATHER, why hast thou forsaken me?" but ]{.class_4938}["My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?" ]{.class_4939}[(Matt. 27:46). God the Father is ]{.class_4938}["the rock of my salvation" ]{.class_4939}[for Israel even though Christ is typified by that expression (1 Cor. 10:4).]{.class_4938}

"[My firstborn." ]{.class_4939}[This is ]{.class_4938}[literal ]{.class_4937}[and not "typical," as in Exodus 4:22. Jesus Christ is the first man born of God on this earth; that is why He is called ]{.class_4938}["the only begotten," ]{.class_4939}[(John 3:18) even though ]{.class_4938}["many sons" ]{.class_4939}[(Heb. 2:10) are later ]{.class_4938}["born of God" ]{.class_4939}[(1 John 3:9) and ]{.class_4938}["begotten" ]{.class_4939}[(1 John 5:1) of God. The ]{.class_4938}["church of the firstborn" ]{.class_4939}[(Heb. 12:23) is the church of the Lord Jesus Christ (Heb. 12:24). God's mercy is kept with Christ (as in Ps. 2, 110), and so is His covenant (Luke 1:30--33). See verse 34 in connection with this, and Isaiah 42:6, 54:10, 59:21, Jer. 31, 33:20--21.]{.class_4938}

"[His seed also will I make to endure forever." ]{.class_4939}[Now comes the good part, for as surely as the speaker addressed by God is His Son, the ]{.class_4938}["seed"]{.class_4939}[ mentioned has to be the ]{.class_4938}["seed" ]{.class_4939}[of Psalm 22:30, which all the masters in Israel missed. ]{.class_4938}["He shall see his seed" ]{.class_4939}[(Isa. 53:10) is the cross reference, and it has nothing to do with the literal sons and grandsons of David. We are now facing a prophesy on the new birth, and that is why Kroll was so anxious to confuse things at verse 26 and drag you back to David and Solomon. Upon grasping the truth that verses 29 and 36 ]{.class_4938}[can ]{.class_4937}[apply to a believer in this age, Kroll immediately does all he can to stop the reader from finding the truth. He quickly disconnects verses 30--32 from the body of the text and throws them back into 1 Kings and 2 Kings so they ]{.class_4938}[will not apply to the believer in this age. ]{.class_4937}[He did this when the Holy Spirit gave him the greatest doctrinal application of ]{.class_4938}["the sure mercies of David" ]{.class_4939}[(Acts 13:34) found anywhere in the Bible. This time the doctrinal truth dealt with the eternal security of the believer, ]{.class_4938}[which no saint under the law had: ]{.class_4937}[see Ezekiel.]{.class_4938}

Such are the ways of "militant Fundamentalists" who hold to "historic positions" in "bastions of orthodoxy" where they train "champions for Christ"!

"[WHEATIES! ]{.class_4938}[The breakfast of champions! ]{.class_4937}[Jack Armstrong. Jack Armstrong. Jack Armstrong never tires of them, and neither will you. So won't you try Wheaties, the best breakfast food in the land!" (America, circa 1935)]{.class_4938}

"[If his children" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 30). That's YOU if you are His child, for you were born of ]{.class_4938}["incorruptible" ]{.class_4939}[seed (1 Pet. 1:23). If you: (1) forsake His law, (2) walk not in His judgment, (3) break His judgments, (4) keep not His commandments\.... He will WHAT? Cast you away? NO. Make you fall from grace? No. Take the Holy Spirit from you, like Saul and Samson? No. Pluck you out of the Father's hand? No. Damn you in hell? No. Say "I never knew you, depart from me"? No. Make you "endure to the end"? No. Make it impossible to renew you again to repentance? No.]{.class_4938}

"Nevertheless my lovingkindness will I not utterly take from him, nor suffer my faithfulness to fail. My covenant will I not break]{.class_4939}[ \[Rom. 8:29; John 3:36, 5:24; 1 Cor. 1:7--9; Phil. 1:6; 1 Thess. 5:24; 1 John 3:1--3\]]{.class_4938}[,]{.class_4939}[ ]{.class_4938}[nor alter the thing that has gone out of my lips" ]{.class_4939}[(vss. 33--34).]{.class_4938}

[Well ]{.class_4938}[GLOOORREE! ]{.class_4937}[Well, ]{.class_4938}[Glory to God! ]{.class_4937}[I'm adopted. I'm in the family (Eph. 3:15). [Psalm 89:27--37 is a prophecy on the new birth and the ]{.class_4938}[eternal security ]{.class_4937}[of the believer in "the Son of David." This is the ]{.class_4938}["throne of his glory" ]{.class_4939}[found in Jeremiah 14:21 and 3:17, which is located at Jerusalem (Matt. 19:28), when Jerusalem becomes ]{.class_4938}["the city of the great King" ]{.class_4939}[(Matt. 5:35) in Matthew 25:31. This throne is ]{.class_4938}["established for ever" ]{.class_4939}[in Isaiah 9:7 and Luke 1:30--33, and we are established forever with it (Rev. 22:3--5). ]{.class_4938}[There is not one difficult word in the entire thirteen verses.]{.class_4937}

[Having completed a paragraph of nineteen verses glorifying the ]{.class_4938}["anointed," ]{.class_4939}[and confirming God's covenant to Him, the Holy Spirit suddenly leads the writer of the Psalm to do a complete reversal and ]{.class_4938}[deny everything He just wrote! ]{.class_4937}[Note:]{.class_4938}

89:37 "It shall be established for ever as the moon, and as a faithful witness in heaven. Selah.

38 But thou hast cast off and abhorred, thou hast been wroth with thine anointed.

39 Thou hast made void the covenant of thy servant: thou hast profaned his crown by casting it to the ground.

40 Thou hast broken down all his hedges; thou hast brought his strong holds to ruin.

41 All that pass by the way spoil him: he is a reproach to his neighbours.

42 Thou hast set up the right hand of his adversaries; thou hast made all his enemies to rejoice.

43 Thou hast also turned the edge of his sword, and hast not made him to stand in the battle.

44 Thou hast made his glory to cease, and cast his throne down to the ground.

45 The days of his youth hast thou shortened: thou hast covered him with shame. Selah."

[This is the type of thing that caused Simon Peter to record what he wrote in 1 Peter 1:10--12. ]{.class_4938}["The sufferings of Christ, and the glory that should follow" ]{.class_4939}[are found in Psalm 89, in ]{.class_4938}[reverse order. ]{.class_4937}[His sufferings are verses 37--45, but His glory is in verses 23--25, 27, 33--34, and 36. Observe the same thing found in Psalm 44:8--9, where all of the "Hurrahs," "Alleluias," and "Bless my souls," are suddenly replaced with ]{.class_4938}["thou hast cast off, and put us to shame."]{.class_4939}

[Verse 38 is a direct reference to Psalm 22:1, 6, 15, etc. Note how "contrary" the Holy Spirit is when He wishes to withhold revealed truth from ]{.class_4938}["the wise and prudent" ]{.class_4939}[(Luke 10:21) in Psalm 22, the "suffering" and the ]{.class_4938}["glory that should follow" ]{.class_4939}[is given in the correct order: suffering in verses 1--21; glory in verses 22--31.]{.class_4938}

For a similar list, where the changes are abrupt and dramatic, see Isaiah 7:21--25; 33:5--9, and Jeremiah 20:12--15.

[The mystery of the Atonement is found in the following words: ]{.class_4938}["my covenant will I not break\...thou hast made void the covenant" ]{.class_4939}[(Ps. 89:34, 39); ]{.class_4938}["I will make him\...higher than the kings of the earth\...thou hast profaned his crown by casting it to the ground" ]{.class_4939}[(Ps. 89:27, 39); ]{.class_4938}["I have exalted one chosen out of the people\...thou hast covered him with shame" ]{.class_4939}[(Ps. 89:19, 45). There it is. Absolute contradictions, and every statement is the truth. God]{.class_4938}[ "cast off" ]{.class_4939}[His Anointed (vs. 38), He ]{.class_4938}["abhorred" ]{.class_4939}[His Anointed (vs. 38), and He was ]{.class_4938}["wroth" ]{.class_4939}[with His Anointed (vs. 38). The figures of speech that follow "drain the barrel" in trying to describe the total abolition of the "covenant" and a complete "reneging" on all of the promises:]{.class_4938}

1\. His crown is torn off his head and thrown to the ground (vs. 39).

2\. The protection around his life and property (see Job 1:10) is torn down (vs. 40).

3\. The fortresses and castles that he set up to defend himself are in ruins (vs. 40).

4\. All his property and riches have been plundered (vs. 41), and his neighbors ridicule him (like Job's buddies did!).

5\. His armaments (the sword) failed him in combat, and his blade couldn't cut hot butter (vs. 43).

6\. His throne was torn down and strewed all over the dirt (vs. 44).

[7. He has been cut down long before the allotted ]{.class_4938}["threescore\...and ten" ]{.class_4939}[(Ps. 90), and His name (after his burial) has been made "shameful" (cf. Isa. 53 and Ps. 89:45).]{.class_4938}

[This is how the orthodox Jews of Christ's day looked at the crucifixion and burial of Jesus Christ. He was mocked as a ]{.class_4938}[false king ]{.class_4937}[with a ]{.class_4938}[false throne ]{.class_4937}[(see John 18--19), who left his ]{.class_4938}[name ]{.class_4937}[for a curse. One cannot help but notice the strange similiarity between Christ as God's "suffering servant" (see Isa. 49:3, 5) and the nation of Israel (Isa. 44:1) as God's "suffering servant." The items I listed above are true of the ]{.class_4938}[nation of Israel ]{.class_4937}[when it reads the COVENANTS that God made with Abraham (Gen. 12, 14, and 22). Notice, especially, Lamentations 5 (and in particular, 5:16).]{.class_4938}

[Verses 44--45 show that the Lord Jesus will have to be meek and lowly before He ever becomes "KING of kings." Hence: ]{.class_4938}["made his glory to cease." ]{.class_4939}[He will have to serve the Father as a servant. Hence: ]{.class_4938}["cast his throne down." ]{.class_4939}[He would be cut off in the midst of His days. Hence: ]{.class_4938}["the days of his youth," ]{.class_4939}[and He would die a disgraceful death with wicked men. Hence: ]{.class_4938}["thou hast covered him with shame."]{.class_4939}

[Adam Clark applies the ]{.class_4938}["days of his youth" ]{.class_4939}[to the four last kings who reigned in Judah, by altering ]{.class_4938}["his"]{.class_4939}[ to "they," and then throwing the whole Messianic prophesy ]{.class_4938}[backwards ]{.class_4937}[into fulfillment before the Advent of Christ. Spurgeon's ]{.class_4938}[Treasury ]{.class_4937}[is the most short handed, deficient set of comments that have ever been written on the passage (pp. 52--53). The contributions, there, are from Matthew Henry, Adam Clarke, John Trapp, Mollerus, and Spurgeon himself. Note the alarm (]{.class_4938}["Selah"]{.class_4939}[) at verse 45, leading you right to a Tribulation saint, praying for the Second Coming (vss. 47, 49--50, etc.). All of the commentators are so taken up with the devotional and philosophical aspects of the next section---and there is plenty there---that they forget that the Psalm was talking about a victorious King ]{.class_4938}[coming to reign over the earth ]{.class_4937}[(vss. 22--27).]{.class_4938}

89:46 "How long, LORD? wilt thou hide thyself for ever? shall thy wrath burn like fire?

47 Remember how short my time is: wherefore hast thou made all men in vain?

48 What man is he that liveth, and shall not see death? shall he deliver his soul from the hand of the grave? Selah.

49 Lord, where are thy former lovingkindnesses, which thou swarest unto David in thy truth?

50 Remember, Lord, the reproach of thy servants; how I do bear in my bosom the reproach of all the mighty people;

51 Wherewith thine enemies have reproached, O LORD; wherewith they have reproached the footsteps of thine anointed.

52 Blessed be the LORD for evermore. Amen, and Amen."

[The opening two words (]{.class_4938}["How long"]{.class_4939}[) put the reader right into Daniel's Seventieth Week, as they placed him there in Psalm 80:4, 82:2, 90:13, 94:3, and 94:4. The petitioner is praying for the ]{.class_4938}[truth ]{.class_4937}[and ]{.class_4938}[lovingkindness ]{.class_4937}[sworn to David (vss. 20, 35, 49) to replace ]{.class_4938}["wrath" ]{.class_4939}[and ]{.class_4938}["fire" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 46). The references match Lamentations 5:16--22. Notice how the petitioner switches from a suffering servant who is an ]{.class_4938}[individual]{.class_4937}[ (]{.class_4938}["I do bear in my bosom\...how short my time is"]{.class_4939}[) to a ]{.class_4938}[group]{.class_4937}[ (]{.class_4938}["all men\...thy servants"]{.class_4939}[).]{.class_4938}

[Four ]{.class_4937}[petitioners are represented.]{.class_4938}

[1. ]{.class_4938}[Jesus Christ ]{.class_4937}[Himself in His state of humiliation (see vss. 48 and 50, and compare these with Ps. 18:4--5 and 22:15; and Ps. 22:6 with 31:11 and 69:7).]{.class_4938}

[2. ]{.class_4938}[David ]{.class_4937}[himself in distress (vss. 47, 51--52).]{.class_4938}

[3. ]{.class_4938}[The nation of Israel ]{.class_4937}[at the end of the Church Age vss. 46, 49, and 51).]{.class_4938}

[4. ]{.class_4938}[Any sinner ]{.class_4937}[in bitter circumstances, who is appealing to God's promises for help. For the last case, note this outline for "The Hand of God." God's "hand" is to be acknowledged 1). In the nature of affliction (]{.class_4938}["Wilt thou hide thyself"]{.class_4939}[) 2). The duration of the affliction (]{.class_4938}["How long, LORD?]{.class_4939}[) 3). The severity of the affliction (wrath burning like fire). 4). The ISSUE of affliction (God's contest with His enemies: vs. 51).]{.class_4938}

[William Puley deals with verse 47 as follows: Did God create man to heap up wealth? Did God create man to seek after power? Did God make man to eat, drink, and be merry? Why then did He create him? The answer is: if a man will not believe Revelation 4:11, he really has no answer, for to all appearances, man was created in vain; notwithstanding Isaiah 45:18. It is Solomon (see ]{.class_4938}[The Bible Believer's Commentary on Ecclesiastes, ]{.class_4937}[1994) who reinforces the Psalmist's question: ]{.class_4938}["Vanity of vanities; all is vanity." ]{.class_4939}[The Psalmist is talking exactly like Habakkuk talked in Habakkuk 1:13--14. God did NOT do these things, but it ]{.class_4938}[appears ]{.class_4937}[that He did. Note that when Jeremiah gets bitter he parks in the same lot (Jer. 15:18).]{.class_4938}

"[What man\...shall not see death?" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 48). Well, ]{.class_4938}["Enoch was translated that he should not see death," ]{.class_4939}[but he was the one exception in over 6,000,000,000 people that have lived on this earth. And after Ethan writes, no man escaped death; not even the Messiah. John and Paul were both caught up, before dying (2 Cor. 12; Rev. 4), but then had to ]{.class_4938}[return ]{.class_4937}[to die (John 21).]{.class_4938}

"[Wherewith they have reproached the footsteps of thine anointed" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 51): This is David, if verse 20 and verse 35 are applied to the Davidic Covenant, which God swore to David in 2 Samuel 7. However, it is Jesus Christ, if verse 20 is applied to verses 25--27. The ]{.class_4938}[devotional application ]{.class_4937}[would be that the enemies of God have made fun of the Christian's "walk": the way he chose to follow Christ. If the "anointed" is David, it is David's enemies (like Saul) mocking him, and bringing a reproach upon his walk (see 1 Sam. 22:6--8 and 23:21--23). If the reference is to Jesus Christ, it would be a reproach from His enemies---who will be Israel's enemies (see Ps. 83, 74, and 59). The reproach, here, would be, "Where is your Lord? He is late in showing up, isn't He?" (see 2 Pet. 3 and Matt. 24:48).]{.class_4938}

[The ]{.class_4938}["Amen, and Amen" ]{.class_4939}[officially closes the third collection of the Psalms (cf. Ps. 72: ]{.class_4938}["Amen, and Amen"]{.class_4939}[).]{.class_4938}

[]{#Uxd5JyNYSWCtgFM2T9UhJQ1306.xhtml}

SECTION FOUR

PREFACE

[The fourth set of Psalms, as the third section, contains seventeen Psalms. Only three of these are ascribed to writers: Psalms 90 to ]{.class_4938}[Moses ]{.class_4937}[and Psalms 101 and 103 to ]{.class_4938}[David. ]{.class_4937}[Nearly all scholars assume that Psalm 90 is the oldest Psalm and therefore the first one ever written. I am inclined to doubt this and for a good reason. The Psalms are a collection of prayers and praises that were compiled by someone, and undoubtedly by more than one person. David, Asaph, Korah, and Hezekiah are good candidates for collators, but so is Moses. Enoch prophesied more than one thousand years before Moses was born, and yet we have a record of his message in Jude 14--15, ]{.class_4938}[written more than 1,500 years after Moses was dead.]{.class_4937}

[With someone handling ]{.class_4938}["the pen of the writer" ]{.class_4939}[(Judg. 5:14) before David's time, are we to presume that none of the saints in Genesis committed a prayer or a song of praise to writing? I doubt it. What musicologists call "traditional melodies" (]{.class_4938}["Barbara Allen," "Greensleeves," "God Save the ]{.class_4937}[King," etc.) have deep roots that reach much earlier than the printed score. Lamech has a ]{.class_4938}["speech"]{.class_4939}[ in Genesis 4:23 that Moses records, and Moses knew of a]{.class_4938}[ "book"]{.class_4939}[ that was written before the "wilderness wanderings" (Exod. 32:32).]{.class_4938}

[We mention this because Bullinger insists that the fourth collection of Psalms must match the book of ]{.class_4938}[Numbers: ]{.class_4937}[i.e., the wilderness wanderings recorded in the book of ]{.class_4938}[Numbers. ]{.class_4937}[The ingenious device for arriving at this conclusion is that when Moses says, ]{.class_4938}["The days of our years are threescore years and ten," ]{.class_4939}[(Ps. 90:10) he is not actually referring to anyone's years except the men of war who turned back at Kadesh Barnea! ]{.class_4938}["Our years," ]{.class_4939}[then, would not really be]{.class_4938}[ "our"]{.class_4939}[ at all. They would be a reference only to the disobedient Israelites ]{.class_4938}[over draft age,]{.class_4937}[ (twenty years), in which case the Psalm should have been written "The days of THEIR years\...." This, of course, it does NOT say. This is drawing pretty hard on the deposits.]{.class_4938}

[Furthermore, there are a dozen things in the Psalm that would not "jive" with anything Moses did historically, nor the children of Israel. We will note these verses when we get to them. The Psalm is ]{.class_4938}["a prayer of Moses," ]{.class_4939}[but it certainly is not ]{.class_4938}[answered ]{.class_4937}[for Moses or his flock in Numbers, Joshua, or Judges for the Lord does NOT return (vs. 13), Moses does NOT get into the Promised Land (vss. 15--16), and the work of God's hands is NOT established on Israel (vs. 17) during the entire time of the book of Numbers.]{.class_4938}

[The problem, of course, was how Moses could say that seventy years would be the days of ]{.class_4938}["our years" ]{.class_4939}[when he himself lived to be one hundred and twenty years old! But we will cross that bridge when we get to it.]{.class_4938}

[]{#Uxd5JyNYSWCtgFM2T9UhJQ1318.xhtml}

PSALM 90

90:1 "LORD, thou hast been our dwelling place in all generations.

2 Before the mountains were brought forth, or ever thou hadst formed the earth and the world, even from everlasting to everlasting, thou art God.

3 Thou turnest man to destruction; and sayest, Return, ye children of men.

4 For a thousand years in thy sight are but as yesterday when it is past, and as a watch in the night.

5 Thou carriest them away as with a flood; they are as a sleep: in the morning they are like grass which groweth up.

6 In the morning it flourisheth, and groweth up; in the evening it is cut down, and withereth.

7 For we are consumed by thine anger, and by thy wrath are we troubled.

8 Thou hast set our iniquities before thee, our secret sins in the light of thy countenance."

[Verse 1 is usually taken in the ]{.class_4938}[devotional ]{.class_4937}[sense, where Moses is using ]{.class_4938}["our"]{.class_4939}[ to refer to the patriarchs who had no permanent dwelling place (see Heb. 11:10, 14). This is the only way that ]{.class_4938}["in all generations" ]{.class_4939}[can be explained, for there is only one generation that leaves Egypt and gets into the Promised Land (Deut. 6:22), in spite of the ridiculous wording of Hebrews 3:16--17 in the "bible" which Jerry Falwell says he reads every morning, and to which he gives credit for being a great spiritual influence in his life: The Living Bunko (]{.class_4938}[The Living Bible]{.class_4937}[). But there is more here than meets the eye, for Paul tells us that all men ]{.class_4938}["live, and move" ]{.class_4939}[and have their ]{.class_4938}["being" ]{.class_4939}[in God (Acts 17:28). The words]{.class_4938}[ "we," "our," ]{.class_4939}[and ]{.class_4938}["us"]{.class_4939}[ in verses 7--12 are certainly not limited to Israel, nor do any of the commentators limit them to Israel, even if the first person plurals in verses 13--17 do refer to Israel.]{.class_4938}

Verse 2 is one of the greatest statements in the Bible on the eternity of God (see Isa. 57:15). The verse describes God before Genesis 1:1 and acknowledges His eternal presence, which continues long after Revelation 22:21. Observe that Moses, as Christ (Mark 13:19), is a "creationist," in contrast to the psychotic monkey men of the nineteenth and twentieth centuries who mistook philosophical speculation for "scientific facts."

[Verse 3 is interpreted in the standard fashion to mean that God turns men to dirt by destroying them and then commands them to return to the dirt. This looks a little far fetched. What is the sense in telling man to return to the dirt after you have already turned him to it? There might be an application if you assume that God turns the man over to death and, then, commands him, "Go back to the dust you came from," but there is another possibility. The term ]{.class_4938}["return" ]{.class_4939}[is usually a reference that deals with God telling a man to ]{.class_4938}[return from his wicked ways; ]{.class_4937}[not to return to ]{.class_4938}[dirt ]{.class_4937}[(see Jer. 26:3; Ezek. 3:19, 33:9; Job 22:23, and note, in particular, Job 36:10 and Isa. 44:22).]{.class_4938}

[It is God who "turns" the Amorites, Jebusites, Hittites, Canaanites, and others, to ]{.class_4938}[destruction ]{.class_4937}[(Josh. 11:20). It was God who "turned" Pharaoh to ]{.class_4938}[destruction ]{.class_4937}[(Exod. 5--9), and it was God who turned Israel to ]{.class_4938}[destruction ]{.class_4937}[(Acts 7:39--42; Rom. 11:8). Observe that in all three cases, God commanded those involved to repent and get right with Him (Judg. 11:12--20, Exod. 9:13--17, 1 Kings 13:1--3, and Jer. 33--39). This meaning matches the context much better than the previous explanation, for God's judgment on sin (vss. 7--8) is being discussed. The idea is not that life is brief because God will command you to return to dirt sooner than you think He will; the idea is that since life is so short (vss. 4--6) that you had better ]{.class_4938}[get right while you can. ]{.class_4937}[Notice how this interpretation is driven home in verse 12.]{.class_4938}

"[For a thousand years in thy sight" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 4) puts us right back into Psalm 84:10 and 2 Peter 3:8. We are on prophetic grounds without the permission of one amillennial or postmillennial expositor.]{.class_4938}

[This time, the ]{.class_4938}["thousand years" ]{.class_4939}[are likened not only to a day (]{.class_4938}["yesterday"]{.class_4939}[) but also to ]{.class_4938}["a watch in the night" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 4). Food for thought.]{.class_4938}

[In the Old Testament, there were ]{.class_4938}[three ]{.class_4937}[watches to a Jewish night; in the New Testament, there are ]{.class_4938}[four ]{.class_4937}[(see Judg. 7:19 and Mark 6:48). The Holy Spirit is careful to tell you, in the next section, that the watches must determine the Second Advent (]{.class_4938}["Return, O LORD," ]{.class_4939}[vs. 13), although all of the commentators miss the connection. Kroll---who by now has gained for Liberty University an unenviable reputation as a fountain of muddy waters---claims that the ]{.class_4938}["return" ]{.class_4939}[in the verse was simply a request from Moses for God to " return from his anger," back in the wilderness. Typical, absolutely typical. With Isaiah 21:12 right in front of his face and Luke 12:36 sitting there to interpret it for him, Kroll sails by verse 4 like Hitzig, Baethgen, Hengstenberg, Hupfeld, Delitzsch, Keil, Briggs, Ewald, Lange, Clarke, and Dummelow sailed by it. Leslie M]{.class_4938}[c]{.class_4962}[Caw (]{.class_4938}[New Bible Commentary, ]{.class_4937}[1965) says Moses is asking God to repent in regards to His dealings with Israel in the wilderness. Guess again. Christ comes ]{.class_4938}["in the fourth watch of the night" ]{.class_4939}[(Matt. 14:25), and in the third "day" of 1,000 years (Exod. 19:11, see the booklet ]{.class_4938}[The Seven Sevens, ]{.class_4937}[1970). Without asking any Hebrew or Greek scholar's advice, the Lord sets up the Old Testament in four watches of one thousand years each, and He sets up the New Testament in ]{.class_4938}[three watches ]{.class_4937}[of one thousand years each. The verse was to be taken literally.]{.class_4938}

"[Thou carriest them away as with a flood\...like grass."]{.class_4939}

[Three similitudes are used to show the brevity of life. (Note the twelve illustrations of the same subject found in the book of Job: ]{.class_4938}[The Bible Believer's Commentary on Job, ]{.class_4937}[1980).]{.class_4938}

[1. Life is like sleep (]{.class_4938}["as a dream when one awaketh," ]{.class_4939}[Ps. 73:20). You lie down and then you get up. What happened in the interim you can't really say. Sometimes a sleep of nine hours feels like you just laid down, and sometimes you can be refreshed by falling asleep for thirty minutes.]{.class_4938}

2\. Life is like a flood. Genesis 6 is the literal illustration, but the idea here is that man is swept off his feet and out of life before he can get a footing.

[3. Life is like grass; this is the famous illustration given by Simon Peter (1 Pet. 1:24) and Isaiah (Isa. 40:6).]{.class_4938}[ "In the morning it flourisheth\...in the evening it is cut down, and withereth" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 6). The similitude is obvious. Christ says it is ]{.class_4938}[burned in the oven ]{.class_4937}[(Matt. 6:30), and in another place He calls this "oven" ]{.class_4938}["a furnace of fire" ]{.class_4939}[(Matt. 13:42).]{.class_4938}

[Note how Moses follows this up immediately with ]{.class_4938}["consumed by thine anger, and by thy wrath." ]{.class_4939}[The ]{.class_4938}["secret sins" ]{.class_4939}[referred to in the passage (vs. 8), are judged by Jesus Christ (Rom. 2:16) at the White Throne Judgment (Rev. 20:11--15), and these secret sins were mentioned by Solomon in Ecclesiastes 12:13--14 when he summed up the ]{.class_4938}["whole duty of man."]{.class_4939}

[Spurgeon and others have a terrible time reconciling seventy years of ]{.class_4938}[living]{.class_4937}[ (vs. 10) with seventy years of being under the ]{.class_4938}[wrath of God]{.class_4937}[ (vs. 9), so Spurgeon goes into a long, unnecessary devotional spiel. Kroll decides that all the days the Israelites lived, from Exodus 3 to Numbers 40, were passed in ]{.class_4938}["wrath," ]{.class_4939}[but the statement was given to show that a man's days pass away if the wrath of God is on him during ]{.class_4938}[those ]{.class_4937}[days (see John 3:36 for the unsaved man in this age.)]{.class_4938}

[Life is "tallied" in the sense of a teller counting a number (see Gen. 15:5 where Abraham is told to ]{.class_4938}["tell the stars"]{.class_4939}[).]{.class_4938}

[This ]{.class_4938}["tale" ]{.class_4939}[is also ]{.class_4938}["told" ]{.class_4939}[in the sense that it is ]{.class_4938}[recited ]{.class_4937}[by someone. It is a tale that has been ]{.class_4938}["told" ]{.class_4939}[so many times that it constitutes an advanced biography of almost any man before he is born. It is known what "Adam" will do (Ecc. 6:10). There may be minute exceptions from time to time, but the main "tale" is much more predictable than ]{.class_4938}[Canterbury Tales ]{.class_4937}[or ]{.class_4938}[Tales of Vienna Woods. ]{.class_4937}[If the man comes in with normal equipment and lives the normal ]{.class_4938}["threescore years and ten," ]{.class_4939}[he will:]{.class_4938}

1\. Cry many times before he is five-years-old. He will receive cuts, bruises, bumps, chastisements, rebukes, embarassments, and ridicule.

2\. Have trouble in school, if he goes to school. He will have disagreements and fights with friends and neighbors (if he has any), and he will have fights with brothers and sisters (if he has any).

[3. Have war cross his path, either as a civilian or a soldier. He will love someone madly, get his feelings hurt, be jealous of someone, vow to take vengeance on someone, and he may kill someone. If not, he will ]{.class_4938}[think ]{.class_4937}[about doing it.]{.class_4938}

4\. Experience hunger, physical pain, thirst, and poverty at one time or another; but if he is rich all of his life, he will still be tempted to splurge, steal, swindle, lie, and bribe people.

5\. Commit some brave acts and some cowardly acts; and will do noble things; and some shameful and disgraceful things. He will yield to a number of temptations to do wrong, and he will certainly sin on a variety of occassions.

6\. Probably marry and have children, but if he doesn't, he will devote himself to various projects and causes of a humanitarian or religious nature. He will be sick and bed ridden, at times.

7\. Earn money, spend money, lose money, and give money to someone. He will get old and sick and die, or he will be crippled or killed.

[Ten years after he is dead there will not be a dozen people who think about him twice a day, and if he had become a world renowned leader (like Napoleon, Charlemagne, Hitler, Roosevelt, Churchill, Constantine, et al.), no one but his personal friends would "miss him" for more than five years. He will have to be converted into a "god" by writers (the news media, biographers, et al.) for anyone to commemorate his death, and even then, no one will miss him ]{.class_4938}[personally. ]{.class_4937}[The press creates "gods" (Dolly Parton, the Kennedy family, Joe Paterno, Michael Jackson, Knute Rockne, Mahatma Gandhi, Elvis Presley, Martin Luther King Jr., Nelson Mandela, Gorbachev, Marx, Darwin, et al.) to give you the impression that the ]{.class_4938}["tale"]{.class_4939}[ has not been told, but it has been told over and over and over again, so many times, as to be nearly worthless to tell again. You are "here today and gone tomorrow," and sometimes you are "here today and gone today."]{.class_4938}

90:9 "For all our days are passed away in thy wrath: we spend our years as a tale that is told.

10 The days of our years are threescore years and ten; and if by reason of strength they be fourscore years, yet is their strength labour and sorrow; for it is soon cut off, and we fly away.

11 Who knoweth the power of thine anger? even according to thy fear, so is thy wrath.

12 So teach us to number our days, that we may apply our hearts unto wisdom.

13 Return, O LORD, how long? and let it repent thee concerning thy servants.

14 O satisfy us early with thy mercy; that we may rejoice and be glad all our days.

15 Make us glad according to the days wherein thou hast afflicted us, and the years wherein we have seen evil.

16 Let thy work appear unto thy servants, and thy glory unto their children.

17 And let the beauty of the LORD our God be upon us: and establish thou the work of our hands upon us; yea, the work of our hands establish thou it."

[The ]{.class_4938}["tale" ]{.class_4939}[that is told, can be a long story or a short story; it can be a dull story or an exciting story; it can be a good story or a bad story or a neutral story. To be a "good story," in the Biblical sense, the story must have the right ]{.class_4938}[author, hero, villain, plot, purpose, ]{.class_4937}[and ]{.class_4938}[ending. ]{.class_4937}[There are no amoral biographies in the Scriptures.]{.class_4938}

[The modern, contemporary approach is that for a story to be "good," it simply has to be ]{.class_4938}[realistic. ]{.class_4937}[That is, any number of obscene descriptions, vulgar language, four-letter words, detailed accounts of sex perversion, or "going to the bathroom" can make it a "work of art." This means, in Biblical terms, that the great "story tellers" of the twentieth century (Hemingway, Faulkner, Mailer, Remarque, James Joyce, Margaret Mitchell, Maughm, Woulk, et al.) will all present one side of reality, and one side only: life through the eyes of a degenerate sinner who is ]{.class_4938}["having no hope, and without God in the world" ]{.class_4939}[(Eph. 2:12). True, they will intersperse their works---if they are really true to the facts---with occasional references to prayer or rosaries or "Our Fathers" or religious ideas and religious activities, but the ]{.class_4938}["new man" ]{.class_4939}[(Col. 3:10; 2 Cor. 5:17) is completely "en absentee" in ]{.class_4938}[Gone With the Wind, For Whom the Bell Tolls, From Here to Eternity, All Quiet on the Western Front, The Snows of Kilimanjaro, Babbit, The Ugly American, Lady Chatterly's Lover, Peyton Place, ]{.class_4937}[and ]{.class_4938}[Atlas Shrugged.]{.class_4937}

"[The days of our years" ]{.class_4939}[are not Moses' years, nor are they the years of anyone he is leading, unless it is the men of war (see comments in the introduction). But not even this will do, for many of those men did NOT live to be seventy years of age; furthermore, the ]{.class_4938}["our"]{.class_4939}[ would have to include the whole nation, and neither Joshua nor Caleb stop at ]{.class_4938}["fourscore years." ]{.class_4939}[They both live well beyond that.]{.class_4938}

[One will have to come into the twentieth century to get the application; that is, right before the Lord DOES return (vs. 13) and DOES let His work ]{.class_4938}["appear" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 16) and DOES establish His ]{.class_4938}["beauty" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 17) upon Israel. The time allotted to a twentieth-century, Jew---barring, accidental death or murder---is seventy years and sometimes eighty. Furthermore, this general age bracket could have done for mankind in Moses' time, for Joshua's hundred and ten years are not near the "norm" (Josh. 24:29). Eli hits ninety eight, and Barzillai is eighty (1 Sam. 4:15; 2 Sam. 19:34--35). Note that at eighty, Barzillai is said to be ]{.class_4938}["a very aged man" ]{.class_4939}[(2 Sam. 19:32). David dies at seventy (2 Sam. 5:4). We may say that seventy is the normal life span for a normal man until the Second Advent. ]{.class_4938}[After that ]{.class_4937}[(see Isa. 65:20), things begin to "pick up"; men are going to live to be hundreds of years old in the Millennium.]{.class_4938}

"[Yet is their strength labour and sorrow," ]{.class_4939}[and this is usually the case, although there are exceptions. Some of the world's "great" (Michelangelo, Tintoretto, Ben Franklin, Thomas Edison, et al.) have been healthy and productive in the years after the allotted "seventy," but even then "the old gray mare ain't what she used to be." Wesley passed the "fourscore" mark, but shortly after that the fevers started, the legs gave way\...etc. (see ]{.class_4938}[The Bible Believer's Commentary on Ecclesiastes, ]{.class_4937}[1994).]{.class_4938}

"[According to thy fear, so is thy wrath" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 11). God's wrath is perfectly "equal to the occasion." The occasion is that He tells man to fear Him (Exod. 20:20; Matt. 10:28; Job 28:28; Ecc. 3:14, 12:13; Phil. 2:12), and He gives these warnings because He knows His own nature: ]{.class_4938}[He is holy. ]{.class_4937}[He cannot, and will not, tolerate sin of any kind, so His wrath against sin is going to be poured out on sinners (Rev. 20:11--15). If you fear Him you will be exercising good judgment: FEAR, and I don't mean "reverential trust" (see Ps. 2:11 and 19:9).]{.class_4938}

"[So teach us to number our days" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 12). These days can be ]{.class_4938}[shortened ]{.class_4937}[(Ecc. 7:17) or ]{.class_4938}[lengthened ]{.class_4937}[(Isa. 38:5), but as they approach the ]{.class_4938}["threescore years and ten" ]{.class_4939}[they had better be counted one by one. (]{.class_4938}[Murphy]{.class_4937}['s ]{.class_4938}[Law: ]{.class_4937}[live every day like it was the last one, and pretty soon it will be.) God does not number the days that Israel is out of fellowship with Him (1 Kings 6:1), so we too may count those days as lost. Days should be numbered according to the hours in those days that were spent in fellowship with God, doing His will, Paul would say, ]{.class_4938}["For to me to live is Christ" ]{.class_4939}[(Phil. 1:21). Boris Becker, the German tennis champion (1990) said, "Tennis IS life. " When Fischer, the American chess player, beat Spasky, the Russian, he said, "Chess IS life." The days these gentlemen "numbered" were the days they ]{.class_4938}[played their game.]{.class_4937}

"[Return, O LORD, how long?" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 13). No problem: the prayer is for the Messiah to come in order that Israel might ]{.class_4938}["be glad all our days" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 14), and that ]{.class_4938}["the beauty of the Lord our God be upon us" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 17), and that ]{.class_4938}["the work of our hands" ]{.class_4939}[might be established (vs. 17). If the years of Israel's affliction (vs. 15) are to be taken ]{.class_4938}[literally]{.class_4937}[, then they are due approximately two thousand years of glory in the future. Note the ]{.class_4938}["seven times more for your sins" ]{.class_4939}[found in Leviticus 26:18, 21, 24, 28, which come to four threats of seven each. These take place in the Church Age when Israel has rejected the Messiah and is paying for the terrible cry of ]{.class_4938}["his blood be on us, and on our children" ]{.class_4939}[(Matt. 27:25). Seventy times seven was the yard stick for forgiveness (Matt. 18:21--22). If you compute four periods of 490 years each, you will get a Church Age of 1960 years.]{.class_4938}

[God's ]{.class_4938}["glory," "beauty," ]{.class_4939}[and ]{.class_4938}["work" ]{.class_4939}[(vss. 16--17) certainly do not ]{.class_4938}["appear" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 16) until the Messiah returns, and the ]{.class_4938}["work" ]{.class_4939}[of the Jewish people will certainly not be established until that time.]{.class_4938}

[]{#Uxd5JyNYSWCtgFM2T9UhJQ1374.xhtml}

PSALM 91

91:1 "He that dwelleth in the secret place of the most High shall abide under the shadow of the Almighty.

2 I will say of the LORD, He is my refuge and my fortress: my God; in him will I trust.

3 Surely he shall deliver thee from the snare of the fowler, and from the noisome pestilence.

4 He shall cover thee with his feathers, and under his wings shalt thou trust: his truth shall be thy shield and buckler.

5 Thou shalt not be afraid for the terror by night; nor for the arrow that flieth by day;

6 Nor for the pestilence that walketh in darkness; nor for the destruction that wasteth at noonday.

7 A thousand shall fall at thy side, and ten thousand at thy right hand; but it shall not come nigh thee."

[The "thous," "thees" and "thines" in the Psalm (vss. 3--13) are applied to the Lord Jesus Christ by the Holy Spirit in the New Testament (Matt. 4:6), although it is Satan who makes the application that the Holy Spirit records. We can make all kinds of spiritual applications, and Spurgeon's ]{.class_4938}[Treasury ]{.class_4937}[is full of them, but in the context where these things occur, they are references to the protection of the Lord Jesus Christ by the Father and the protection of Tribulation saints (see specifically vss. 3, 5--6, 10).]{.class_4938}

When Mr. Church wrote his book on "prophecy in the Psalms," he hadn't the slightest idea of what he was doing other than making another million dollars off the mass of Charismatic Christians who had been created between 1970 and 1990 by Roberts, Gorman, Kapps, Copeland, Hickey, Swaggart, Allen, Branam, Ewing, Osborne, and the Bakkers. Those gullible suckers---and that is the kindest way to describe them---will bite at anything if it tickles their ears. When Jim Bakker heard the iron slamming behind him---locking him in a cell with two other queers---Tammy was blubbering (for the benefit of press, worldwide): "Oh, Jesus is going to intervene! Jesus will save us! This is all for the glory of God." The stupid Charismatics, to this day, believe exactly what Tammy and Jimmy believed about positive thinking, "glorifying God," apostolic signs, living "high on the hog and low on the chicken," claiming promises that have nothing to do with them, bilking people out of life savings with talk about "seed faith" and "seed promises," gushing sentimental humanism, and a complete absence of doctrinal teaching from either Testament.

[The ]{.class_4938}["secret place" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 1) turns out to be the shelter of an eagle's wings (Exod. 19:4) or a mother hen's wings (Matt. 23:37), because the ]{.class_4938}["feathers, and\...wings" ]{.class_4939}[show up in verse 4. But in addition to this, four familiar figures (]{.class_4938}["refuge," "fortress," "shield," ]{.class_4939}[and the ]{.class_4938}["buckler"]{.class_4939}[) show up again (cf. Ps. 18:2, 30; 9:9; 14:6; 57:1). The ]{.class_4938}["secret place,"]{.class_4939}[ spiritually, is the place mentioned in Isaiah 57:15, which see. ]{.class_4938}["The shadow of the Almighty" ]{.class_4939}[turns out to be Jesus Christ Himself, in the Song of Solomon 2:3 and Isaiah 4:6, 32:2. This last reference "ties the rag on the bush" for the expositors, for up shows the ]{.class_4938}["ROCK"]{.class_4939}[ of Deuteronomy 32:4, 15, 18, and we are back in the Tribulation again, at the rock city---]{.class_4938}[Sela]{.class_4937}[, Petra. This will bomb out the expositors again, and this time not even their devotional comments will make sense, because they are going to go overboard to prove either that the pestilences (vss. 3, 6) are not ]{.class_4938}[literal, ]{.class_4937}[or else that God simply will not let a ]{.class_4938}[Christian]{.class_4937}[ get them. (One more strike and you're out!)]{.class_4938}

[Spurgeon decides that plagues can hit Christians but not those with "superior faith" (p. 101). In the midst of such things as cholera, yellow fever, small pox, flu, AIDS, syphilis, the black death, and leprosy, the people that it does not ]{.class_4938}["come nigh"]{.class_4939}[ to are ]{.class_4938}[superior Christians. ]{.class_4937}[Nuts.]{.class_4938}

[Joseph Caryl says that the pestilences can]{.class_4938}[ "come nigh"]{.class_4939}[ you, but not so nigh as to hurt you. Well, put it this way: ]{.class_4938}[they can drop you dead in your tracks.]{.class_4937}

[Another class of commentators decided to screw up the doctrinal revelation of the 1611 English by pretending that the pestilences are not ]{.class_4938}[real. ]{.class_4937}[They are just ]{.class_4938}[figures ]{.class_4937}[for "a plague of wickedness" or "the persecuting word" (Hengstenberg, Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown, et al.). At Liberty University, it is just a "poetic picture" of the "grim reaper" (Kroll). "The pestilence appears to be something spoken" (Kroll). Well, it would appear that way to a Bible-rejecting Nicolaitan without a brain in his head, who was so captivated by Hebrew roots that he couldn't translate anyway, even if he had "the original autographs" in his lap.]{.class_4938}

[Had enough? Do you still want to see what Motyer, Dummelow, and Yates have to offer in the way of "recognized, godly, dedicated scholarship"? Yates says the references are just to a violent death of ]{.class_4938}[some ]{.class_4937}[kind, Motyer says it is "invisible things, "and Dummelow converts the literal plagues into "destructive words" and "malicious speech."]{.class_4938}

Want some fresh air? Could any real "environmentalist" stand such pollution? How about a little "Earth Day" for "global citizens?" Let's clear the air.

[1. All of the Antichrist's people are hit with a ]{.class_4938}[literal ]{.class_4937}[plague that produces a ]{.class_4938}["grievous sore"]{.class_4939}[ (Rev. 16:2). Just to make sure that none of the commentators---absolutely NONE of them---could find the reference, the Holy Spirit ]{.class_4938}[stuck in the same English word that is found in Psalm 91:3 ]{.class_4937}["noisome"]{.class_4939}[ (Rev. 16:2). Thus the ]{.class_4938}[AV, ]{.class_4937}[from 1611, carefully prevented any scholar (this would include about four thousand since that time) from finding the truth. All He had to do was hide it in the "archaic Elizabethan English" of the seventeenth century, and every commentator and scholar who went by the "original Hebrew" or the "original Greek text" couldn't find ANYTHING.]{.class_4938}

[2. Either the Angel of the Lord sends the ]{.class_4938}[pestilence ]{.class_4937}[(see comments under Ps. 78:50), or it is ]{.class_4938}[Abaddon, ]{.class_4937}[the death angel (see comments under Ps. 78:49). The latter would be ]{.class_4938}["the fowler" ]{.class_4939}[of verse 3, for it is Satan's ]{.class_4938}["angel" ]{.class_4939}[(see Rev. 9:11) who sets "snares"; at least according to the apostle Paul in the New Testament (1 Tim. 3:7). All the commentators miss all the doctrinal references.]{.class_4938}

[3. In the Tribulation, every deadly thing in Psalm 91 is ]{.class_4938}[literal: ]{.class_4937}[a. the]{.class_4938}[ "snare,"]{.class_4939}[ b. ]{.class_4938}["the terror by night,"]{.class_4939}[ c. the pestilence in the daytime, d. the arrows flying in the daytime, and e. ]{.class_4938}["the destruction"]{.class_4939}[ that comes in the middle of the day. These ferocious disasters are the subject matter of Revelation 6--19, and there is no excuse for any premillennial expositor (Kroll, for example) being unable to find the doctrinal truths involved: absolutely ]{.class_4938}[no excuse.]{.class_4937}

There is a prosaic illustration of this verse in nature, in that the mongoose (of "cobra" fame) gets an antidote for himself every time he is bitten. There has been a reward offered in India, for several centuries, for anyone who can find where the rascal goes to get his herbal treatment. He goes off into the brush, and when he is found later, he is in perfect health. Laboratory experiments have done no good. He won't bite anything in the lab.

[The snare is ]{.class_4938}[literal, ]{.class_4937}[as in Isaiah 24:17--18, and Jeremiah 48:43--44. Jesus Christ Himself uses the term in regards to His Second Coming (Luke 21:35). The ]{.class_4938}["fowler"]{.class_4939}[ is after birds, and according to Ecclesiastes 9:12, the birds are MEN. Hosea 9:8 tells us that the fowler can be a ]{.class_4938}["prophet," so ]{.class_4937}[we need not be surprised to find him in Revelation 13:11--14 catching ]{.class_4938}[men ]{.class_4937}[for the Devil.]{.class_4938}

[Verse 4, you are ]{.class_4938}["under his wings" ]{.class_4939}[when resting and ON His wings when travelling (Deut. 32:10).]{.class_4938}

[The Bible believer has to face reality whether the commentators face it or not. You are ]{.class_4938}[not ]{.class_4937}[promised protection from an ]{.class_4938}["arrow that flieth by day," ]{.class_4939}[nor are you immune from a ]{.class_4938}["destruction that wasteth at noonday," ]{.class_4939}[nor do you have any guarantee that you will not be struck down by a ]{.class_4938}["noisome pestilence,"]{.class_4939}[ or by a ]{.class_4938}["pestilence that walketh in darkness," ]{.class_4939}[at least not in this age. Scores of Christians have taken the arrows right in the chest, and often in the arms, legs, back, and stomach; and thousands of Christians have died of yellow fever, flu, smallpox, cancer, and diptheria. I had a friend whose Christian brother died of AIDS. On his death bed he said: "I hate those queers. They would not let me die with dignity." He thought he had a right to "die with dignity" because he was an American Christian under a Constitution which guaranteed him "life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness." He forgot that Abraham Lincoln did away with the Constitution and the Bill of Rights back in 1864.]{.class_4938}

[The "arrows" have come in the form of 9mm and 45 caliber shells, 30.06 shells, 20mm, 105's, 155's, and 250's, as well as French 75's and German 88's. (There are bigger and better ones coming up.) The destruction of Hiroshima and Nagasaki was ]{.class_4938}[not ]{.class_4937}[at night time; it was in broad daylight (vs. 6). The ]{.class_4938}["terror by night" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 5) fell on Hamburg and Nuremberg, as well as London and Tokyo. Fire bombing is a ]{.class_4938}["terror," ]{.class_4939}[and many a saved Britisher lost his life under the bombings of the Luftwaffe. Spurgeon's flights of devotional rhapsodies look all right on paper, but you had better "face up to it": the promises in Psalm 91 are not aimed at anyone in the Church Age, ]{.class_4938}[doctrinally. ]{.class_4937}[Death (personified), Hell (personified), cannibalism, demonism, famine, floods, pestilences, earthquakes, fires, plagues, and demons will be as common in Daniel's Seventieth Week (Rev. 6--19) as popcorn and peanuts in a ball stadium.]{.class_4938}

Note the Israelish reference in Psalm 27:3.

91:8 "Only with thine eyes shalt thou behold and see the reward of the wicked.

9 Because thou hast made the LORD, which is my refuge, even the most High, thy habitation;

10 There shall no evil befall thee, neither shall any plague come nigh thy dwelling.

11 For he shall give his angels charge over thee, to keep thee in all thy ways.

12 They shall bear thee up in their hands, lest thou dash thy foot against a stone.

13 Thou shalt tread upon the lion and adder: the young lion and the dragon shalt thou trample under feet.

14 Because he hath set his love upon me, therefore will I deliver him: I will set him on high, because he hath known my name.

15 He shall call upon me, and I will answer him: I will be with him: in trouble; I will deliver him, and honour him.

16 With long life will I satisfy him, and shew him my salvation."

[Again, one must face reality. ]{.class_4938}["It shall not come nigh thee" ]{.class_4939}[is not a promise that anyone can claim ]{.class_4938}[in this age. ]{.class_4937}["IT"]{.class_4939}[ may come nigh thee, and it may come ]{.class_4938}["nigh" ]{.class_4939}[enough to put you into a coffin. Many Christians died before they were fifteen years old, and ten thousand certainly did not "fall" at their side. Not even all the saints in the Tribulation can claim the promise, ]{.class_4938}[for many of them are beheaded ]{.class_4937}[(Rev. 20:4), and some of them are EATEN (Isa. 6:13). Don't look for it in McCaw, Hitzig, Bullinger, Price, Afman. Dummelow, Baethgen, Briggs, Driver, Hengstenberg, Kroll, Yates, or Motyer; they don't have a Holy Bible they can hold in their hands. ]{.class_4938}[Directly, ]{.class_4937}[the passage goes on the Lord Jesus Christ (see vs. 11). He is the one who treads on ]{.class_4938}["the lion and adder: the young lion and the dragon" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 13). ]{.class_4938}[You ]{.class_4937}[are not about to do it.]{.class_4938}

Still, in a devotional sense it is true, if a Christian has been able to survive half a century or more, for in that time, at least six million people will have fallen by his side. In the twentieth century the number will be closer to three billion. A man living from 1910 to 1960 would have survived thirty two million war casualties, not counting accidental and natural deaths.

My good friend Ray Sea was a Marine on Iwo (1945). He was trying to spot a sniper who had already "drilled" three Marines. After observing from behind a rock for ten minutes, he belly-crawled to a new position behind a log, about fifteen feet off. Another Marine took up his former position behind the rock, for observation, and in less than ten seconds took a Japanese round right between the eyes, one inch below the helmet liner. Ray Sea came back to the States after the war and preached the gospel forty years before he died.

[Glenn Schunk (ETO), returning from a patrol, in snow drifts four feet deep, lost track of his return path, and came to a place where two depressions branched out about fifteen degrees from each other. Glenn was a Christian, so he prayed before taking the left "path." It was the one he had come out on, and it led back to his forward units. The next day, after an attack, they noticed that at the end of the ]{.class_4938}[right ]{.class_4937}[path (from which they had turned away) was a German machine gun nest. All three gunners had been killed in the attack that morning.]{.class_4938}

[Tim Lee had ]{.class_4938}["it"]{.class_4939}[ come so]{.class_4938}[ "nigh" ]{.class_4939}[him that it cost him both his legs; Noble Boyette had ]{.class_4938}["it" ]{.class_4939}[come so ]{.class_4938}["nigh" ]{.class_4939}[him that forty years after the war was over he still had rusty shrapnel in his legs; James McGinlay, who preached the gospel thirty years before dying of old age, was the only man in a two hundred man Scotch infantry company to survive World War I. Over eight million men fell at McGinlay's]{.class_4938}[ "right hand," ]{.class_4939}[and over twenty one million fell at Audie Murphy's right hand; neither man was killed in action. When Mount Pelee erupted on St. Martinique Island (1902), there was one survivor; twenty six thousand fell at his side. The lone survivor was a prisoner in a small jail.]{.class_4938}

[I take a different view of these matters than some of the brethren. I assume that I could just as well have been one of the casualties in any of four dozen disasters that took place between 1921 and 1990. The gambling ships caught on fire, the zeppelins exploded, the planes crashed in midair or blew up; the night clubs caught on fire, the race cars plunged into the grandstands, the ammunition ships exploded at the piers, there were "head ons" on the highway, floods wiped out forty thousand at a time, the crime rate doubled every other year after 1964, and Washington became the murder capital of the world, with more murders taking place in it in a DAY than took place in Tokyo, Berlin, or Rome in a month. But it did not come ]{.class_4938}["nigh" ]{.class_4939}[me---at least not for ]{.class_4938}[seventy two years.]{.class_4937}

[But no man could claim Psalm 91:7, in this age, simply because he was saved or "living right." The direct ]{.class_4938}[doctrinal ]{.class_4937}[claim is Christ's.]{.class_4938}

[Of course, there is much to be said here ]{.class_4938}[devotionally. ]{.class_4937}[For example, we may say that:]{.class_4938}

[1. Our eyes will behold ]{.class_4938}["the reward of the wicked," ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 8), for we will be part of the troops that follow the Lord Jesus Christ into combat at Armageddon (see Rev. 19 and Isa. 63).]{.class_4938}

2\. No evil will ever befall us again after the Rapture, and no plague will ever effect us again.

3\. We will be delivered (vs. 14).

[4. We will be set ]{.class_4938}["on high" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 14).]{.class_4938}

5\. God certainly is with us now in all trouble (vs. 15).

[6. We certainly will be honored (John 5:44, 12:26) and our ]{.class_4938}["long life" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 16) will last as long as God's life lasts (Isa. 51:6, 8). If you are saved you can put your name into Psalm 91:14--16 where it says ]{.class_4938}["he" ]{.class_4939}[or ]{.class_4938}["him."]{.class_4939}[ GLORY!!]{.class_4938}

[In America, the children of God sometimes forget how many times God has spared them from calamities that regularly befall thousands of human beings in ]{.class_4938}[other ]{.class_4937}[countries. Famines are as common in India and Africa as snowstorms in Minnesota and North Dakota. I wrote an autobiography some time back, and in it will be found a "running account" of the grim reaper's activities from the time I was born (1921) up until 1990. The record is appalling. While I was growing up and going to school, they were "failing at my side" by forest fires, typhoons, hurricanes, floods, earthquakes, diseases, assassinations, car wrecks, plane crashes, tidal waves, and revolutions; in my early manhood, they were going into eternity via bombs, shells, bayonets, knives, torpedoes, grenades, flame throwers, pistols, rifles, rockets, and land mines at the rate of three million a year. If I had not changed Corps areas in 1942, I would have been slaughtered on the beaches of Bougainville, Guadalcanal, New Georgia, Cape Glouster, or Kasserine Pass. If my ROTC unit had been activated a year earlier than it was (1943), I would have been shot down in the D Day invasion at Normandy or killed in the Falaise Pocket or shot trying to liberate Manila and Clark Field. If God had not kept me in the States training troops in 1944, I would have gone on home to Hell from the Ardennes or the "Snow Eiffel" or the beaches of Iwo Jima or Okinawa. I was "shuttled off" onto another track (1943--1944) by an unseen, omnipotent Hand that had stored up something better for me than my lifelong ambition: to be a thirty year man---a combat infantry officer. Instead, I wound up being a "forty four year man" in the "good fight of faith" that Paul wrote about in the New Testament. I, and some like me, can look back and see where literally "thousands" fell at our sides. The German military funeral march ]{.class_4938}["Ich Hatte Einen Kameraden"]{.class_4937}[ tells the story: "A bullet comes a flying; is it for ]{.class_4938}[him ]{.class_4937}[or ]{.class_4938}[me? ]{.class_4937}[He has fallen at my feet as though he were a piece ]{.class_4938}[of my own flesh."]{.class_4937}

[Curly Hollinger was captured at Anzio (Feb. 1944) and nearly died of pneumonia, marching through the snow in twelve below zero weather. He raised a family in Bay Minette, Alabama, ]{.class_4938}[after ]{.class_4937}[the war. Russell Fell, an unsaved Catholic infantry sergeant, fought in Salerno, then Anzio, then Europe---twenty three months in combat---and collapsed at a field dressing station (1945) after a German Schmeisser had "stitched" him five times. He died "in Christ," of old age, in ]{.class_4938}[Elberta, Alabama, ]{.class_4937}[in the 1980s. The last man alive out of the ]{.class_4938}[USS Indianapolis ]{.class_4937}[was a crewman in the boiler room, who came "topside" after the ship was under water. He was jumping companionways in the darkness, with the companionways running up and down vertically, instead of horizontally.]{.class_4938}

[Some of the men who survived the Death March (May 1942) were not liberated till after the Japanese surrender. They were a pitiful remnant of more than sixteen thousand men, of whom more than ten thousand were either starved, tortured to death, or shot on the spot. "You sleepy? You like sleepy? You lie down; you get good long sleepy!" Crack! went the pistol. They couldn't lie down to rest at night after marching thirty-five miles in 105 degrees, with nothing to eat or drink. The Japanese rear guard would follow the column and kill anyone who quit walking. About two hundred of them survived the march, and then Cabanatuan, and then Camp O'Donell, and then the "Tennis Court," and Davao Penal Colony, and then seven survived a slave ship trip to Japan. ]{.class_4938}["But it shall not come nigh thee."]{.class_4939}

[The switching of speakers (in vs. 9) throws all of the commentators into a panic. There is a mad dash to the "Hebrew" on the part of some of them, others simply pass, and then there is a juggling of "you's," "thy's," "thee's," and "mine's" to try to make the Psalm shape up to the ignorance of the unbelieving scholar handling it. Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown assure us that the ]{.class_4938}[AV ]{.class_4937}[is wrong, and it should read "as the Hebrew." ("Because thou, O Lord, art my refuge, Thou hast made the Most High thy habitation.") This is one of Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown's "rathers"; i.e, they would rather you reject the word of God and trust their corrections, than have you believe it and not trust them. The English edition of the Old Testament, published by the Masoretic Society, reads with the ]{.class_4938}[King James Bible.]{.class_4937}

[The problem is this: in verse 2 the ]{.class_4938}["I"]{.class_4939}[ is the Psalmist, but the ]{.class_4938}["thee"]{.class_4939}[ in the next verse (vs. 3) will have to be the]{.class_4938}[ Lord Jesus, ]{.class_4937}[if we are to follow through to verses 12 and 13. When we get to verse 9, it would appear that the Psalmist is talking to Jesus Christ and saying, "Because you have put your faith in the same God in whom I have put my faith, ]{.class_4938}['there shall no evil befall thee']{.class_4939}[." This is the sense, although the commentators cannot imagine it.]{.class_4938}

[A little reading in the Holy Bible will stimulate one's imagination in such matters. For example, in Isaiah 49:1--2 Jesus Christ gives a personal word of testimony, but immediately in verse 5 ]{.class_4938}["me"]{.class_4939}[ is no longer Christ; it is ]{.class_4938}[Israel. ]{.class_4937}[Verse 4---switched again. It is Isaiah speaking ]{.class_4938}[for ]{.class_4937}[Israel. Verse 5---oops! This is ]{.class_4938}[God the Father ]{.class_4937}[speaking (]{.class_4938}["And now saith the Lord"]{.class_4939}[).]{.class_4938}[ ]{.class_4939}[The ]{.class_4938}["me"]{.class_4939}[ is Jesus Christ again (]{.class_4938}["yet shall I be glorious in the eyes of the Lord, and my God shall be my strength"]{.class_4939}[.)]{.class_4938}[ ]{.class_4939}[Note especially the last seven words. Time after time, in the Old Testament, the Lord Jesus is spoken of as a man praying to God, putting His trust in God, hiding in God, confiding in God, and seeking God's protection, shelter, defense, and blessing. This is the case in Psalm 91:9.]{.class_4938}
