True every time, in the sense of Romans 10:13, where a man calls upon the name of the Lord for salvation, and often times true in the case of emergencies and genuine disasters. Cases are too numerous to list, let alone describe (see Ps. 91:7 and comments). Verse 14 would refer to prisoners in chains (vs. 10, ]{.class_4938}["being bound in\...iron"]{.class_4939}[), and although this has not always been the case with God's people (Latimer, Ridley, Cranmer, Huss, Savonarola, Sattler, Hubmaier, for example), it is always true, ]{.class_4938}[eventually, ]{.class_4937}[because the saints wind up in Glory, chaining God's adversaries (see Ps. 149:7--8.)]{.class_4938}

[From the standpoint of ]{.class_4938}[history, ]{.class_4937}[nothing on earth could have been more terrible than to have been a Jew---a son of Abraham---in a concentration camp, with this verse either before his eyes, or in his memory. Can you imagine what it must have been like to have read this promise---written by an Israelite to Israelites---and then trying to claim it in Auschwitz, Buchenwald, or Treblinka? Six million who sat ]{.class_4938}["in the shadow of death" ]{.class_4939}[were not brought out (vs. 14), and instead of having their "bands broken in sunder," ]{.class_4938}[they ]{.class_4937}[were broken in sunder and cremated. Imagine an inmate of Treblinka, under the Roman Catholic Franz Stangl, claiming Psalm 124:6--7 or Psalm 140:12! The ]{.class_4938}["afflicted"]{.class_4939}[ and the ]{.class_4938}["poor"]{.class_4939}[ of Psalm 140:12 had their homes pillaged, their goods stolen, their babies slammed against the wall, their mothers and fathers shot before their eyes, and their bodies starved to death. (See comments under Ps. 12:7). You can't go charging through the Psalm picking up convenient promises at random. Luther got away with claiming Psalm 118:1--11, but Nat Saint and Watchman Nee couldn't, and neither could Haralan Popov or Richard Wurmbrand.]{.class_4938}

[Let us compare Scripture with Scripture and see the doc]{.class_4938}[trine ]{.class_4937}[and ]{.class_4938}[prophecy ]{.class_4937}[here. We have already pointed out the future application of ]{.class_4938}["the]{.class_4939}[ ]{.class_4938}[way to the city" ]{.class_4939}[(vss. 4, 7, 36). Now comes the matter of that ]{.class_4938}["shadow of death" ]{.class_4939}[(vss. 10, 14) of which David spoke (Ps. 23:4). This ]{.class_4938}["shadow" ]{.class_4939}[will be caused by something that ]{.class_4938}[passes overhead ]{.class_4937}[in the Tribulation. Observe how neatly the Holy Spirit rightly divided]{.class_4938}[ "the word of truth"]{.class_4939}[ in Isaiah 9:2 when dealing with this ]{.class_4938}["shadow." ]{.class_4939}[He purposely ]{.class_4938}[omitted ]{.class_4937}[twenty six words from the two verses. Boy! You talk about "subtracting from the word of God." Look at those omissions in Matt. 4:14--16, so that no Alexandrian in the Scholar's Union would think the verse could be ]{.class_4938}[discarded ]{.class_4937}[as soon as it was spiritually applied. (You don't have to be told what Kroll, Motyer, Dummelow, Duhm, Baethgen, and company did with it: one guess will suffice.)]{.class_4938}

[The "Bamboo Curtain" and the "Iron Curtain" (1946--1990) were cheap counterfeits of a ]{.class_4938}["veil"]{.class_4939}[ that will be cast over all nations (Isa. 25:7). Whatever this is, it comes from ]{.class_4938}["the Lord of hosts\...which is excellent in working" ]{.class_4939}[(Isa. 28:22--29). The idea is that some kind of a machine kills anything beneath it; possibly radioactively. It is large enough to ]{.class_4938}[cast a shadow, ]{.class_4937}[and if we are to believe Isaiah 9:1--4 (and we believe it whether the Alexandrian Cult believes it or not), this shadow would have to be well over a half a mile long and perhaps several hundred yards wide. Job locates it in Daniel's Seventieth Week (Job 3:5) and connects it with a CLOUD that accompanies ]{.class_4938}["the blackness of the day." ]{.class_4939}[When Job speaks of the afterlife in darkness (Job 10:21), he mentions this]{.class_4938}[ "shadow."]{.class_4939}[ All of the apostates in the Alexandrian Cult consider the expression to be "poetic" or "figurative," but we know, by now, what to think of such dilettantism. In Job 12:22, Job says that God brings this ]{.class_4938}["shadow" ]{.class_4939}[out to daylight, and even though Job uses the expression in describing a man about to die (Job 16:16), the other associations are clear:]{.class_4938}

[1. ]{.class_4938}["The shadow of death"]{.class_4939}[ is a "terror" (Job 24:17).]{.class_4938}

[2. It is something ]{.class_4938}[that will come up from underground ]{.class_4937}[(Job 28:3, 12:22).]{.class_4938}

[3. It is associated with life after physical death, and life in the ]{.class_4938}[wrong place ]{.class_4937}[(Job 38:17; Ps. 23:4). It, therefore, pictures physical and ]{.class_4938}[spiritual death.]{.class_4937}

[4. It is connected with ]{.class_4938}["dragons"]{.class_4939}[ (Ps. 44:19).]{.class_4938}

[5. It is constantly in contrast with the ]{.class_4938}["shadow" ]{.class_4939}[of God's "wings" (Ps. 17:8, 36:7, 57:1, 63:7, 91:1, and Song of Sol. 2:3), therefore it has something to do with someone trusting a ]{.class_4938}[cherub with wings ]{.class_4937}[(Ezek. 1:6--10, 28:14).]{.class_4938}

[6. Amos 5:8 says the Millennium will convert the ]{.class_4938}["shadow of death" ]{.class_4939}[into ]{.class_4938}["morning," ]{.class_4939}[and Job says that the unsaved followers of the anointed cherub fear that ]{.class_4938}["Sun of righteousness" ]{.class_4939}[rising in the ]{.class_4938}["morning" ]{.class_4939}[like they fear the shadow (Job 24:17).]{.class_4938}

[7. Did you notice at the ]{.class_4938}[end of ]{.class_4937}[Zacharias's prophecy (Luke 1:67--79) that ]{.class_4938}["the shadow of death" ]{.class_4939}[shows up? Notice that the ]{.class_4938}[fulfillment of ]{.class_4937}[this prophecy cannot possibly be during the first coming of Christ by any method of computation. Compare Luke 1:71 and 74 with what ]{.class_4938}[actually happened ]{.class_4937}[at the First Coming: Luke 21:20--22, and 24. There was no political deliverance for Jerusalem at the First Advent, and Israel, as a nation, received anything but ]{.class_4938}["remission of sins" ]{.class_4939}[(Luke 1:77).]{.class_4938}

[The Lord simply messed up the minds ]{.class_4938}[of ]{.class_4937}[the Bible correctors again.]{.class_4938}

[If the shadow is not cast by a cherubic machine like Isaiah suggests, then it could be a shadow cast by ]{.class_4938}["a fiery flying serpent" ]{.class_4939}[(Isa. 14:29). See the comments on the ox and cherubim under Psalm 106:19--20.]{.class_4938}

"[Oh that men would praise the LORD\...for his wonderful works" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 15).]{.class_4938}

107:16 "For he hath broken the gates of brass, and cut the bars of iron in sunder.

17 Fools, because of their transgression, and because of their iniquities, are afflicted.

18 Their soul abhorreth all manner of meat; and they draw near unto the gates of death.

19 Then they cry unto the LORD in their trouble, and he saveth them out of their distresses.

20 He sent his word, and healed them, and delivered them from their destructions.

21 Oh that men would praise the LORD for his goodness, and for his wonderful works to the children of men!"

[Doctrinally, ]{.class_4937}[in verse 16 we have the captives of Abraham's bosom who are released at the resurrection of Christ (see Ps. 68:18). The brass and iron are connected with ]{.class_4938}["behemoth" ]{.class_4939}[(Job 40:18, see comments in that commentary). But what follows is more practical. Men are afflicted because they are ]{.class_4938}["fools"]{.class_4939}[ (Rom. 1:22), and they are fools because they ]{.class_4938}["rebelled"]{.class_4939}[ (vs. 11) ]{.class_4938}["against the words of God"]{.class_4939}[---not just the principles, teachings, fundamentals, precepts, etc. A real fool, about to die or be killed (vs. 18) for his foolishness, loses his appetite completely (vs. 18, see Ps. 88:4 and 102:4). ]{.class_4938}["They cry to the LORD in their trouble, and he saveth them" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 19). Compare this with verses 6 and 13. Three times now we have been told that if a man wants food and water or deliverance from distress or to get out of ]{.class_4938}["trouble,"]{.class_4939}[ or to stay alive, he is to ]{.class_4938}["cry unto the LORD" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 19). This is what Israel did NOT do (Hos. 7:14), and this is what hypocrites do not do when the Lord "binds them" (Job 36:13). If you want to see the proper reaction of a sinner in real trouble and real distress (who is being afflicted and bound), read Lamentations 3:1--66. Note especially verses 4, 8, 17, 19, 21--23, 25--26, 31--32, 38--41, and 50. This is Psalm 107:16--19 in practise.]{.class_4938}

107:22 "And let them sacrifice the sacrifices of thanksgiving, and declare his works with rejoicing.

23 They that go down to the sea in ships, that do business in great waters;

24 These see the works of the LORD, and his wonders in the deep.

25 For he commandeth, and raiseth the stormy wind, which lifteth up the waves thereof.

26 They mount up to the heaven, they go down again to the depths: their soul is melted because of trouble.

27 They reel to and fro, and stagger like a drunken man, and are at their wits' end.

28 Then they cry unto the LORD in their trouble, and he bringeth them out of their distresses.

29 He maketh the storm a calm, so that the waves thereof are still.

30 Then are they glad because they be quiet; so he bringeth them unto their desired haven.

31 Oh that men would praise the LORD for his goodness, and for his wonderful works to the children of men!"

[As one can see, the Psalm is divided neatly into five parts; the number of DEATH. Four sections end with ]{.class_4938}["Oh that men would\...etc."]{.class_4939}

Verse 22 is a spiritual sacrifice (Heb. 13:16) that can be offered by a New Testament Christian (1 Pet. 2:5). In the Old Testament it was a literal offering, but it certainly has always been a spiritual one, at times, even for an Old Testament saint, for it is extremely costly and difficult to offer thanksgiving to God for a situation like 2 Samuel 18:32; 1 Samuel 4:17, 18; Acts 12:1--5; or Ezekiel 24:18. Romans 8:28 is our grounds for doing so, but "doing so" is easier said than "done."

[We come upon a maritime scene about which we have already made some comments (Psa. 104:24--29). ]{.class_4938}["The works of the LORD" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 24), already mentioned three times (vss. 8, 15, and 21), are now brought into focus in one place---the SEA, and eight verses are used to show that if a man can't see the ]{.class_4938}["wonders"]{.class_4939}[ (vs. 24) and works of God in any other place, he can see them on the ]{.class_4938}[ocean.]{.class_4937}[ We will confine our remarks to devotional matters, but the student of the Scriptures should not fail to notice that in the "great deeps" there are "sea monsters" and something resembling CITIES. Using Daniel as a pattern for heavenly counterparts of earthly powers, one should carefully study the material on the downfalls of not only Babylon (Jer. 50--51; Rev. 17), but Tyre (Isa. 23) and Nineveh (Nah. 1--3). The problem that no one has worked out is the problem of ]{.class_4938}["the isles of the sea" ]{.class_4939}[(Isa. 24:15), which are said to be ]{.class_4938}["fires."]{.class_4939}[ The ]{.class_4938}["isles"]{.class_4939}[ of Isaiah 40:15 must be larger and more powerful than the ]{.class_4938}["nations" ]{.class_4939}[mentioned in the same verse. Strangely enough, the expression before us (]{.class_4938}["They that go down to the sea"]{.class_4939}[) occurs in connection with the]{.class_4938}[ "isles"]{.class_4939}[ in Isaiah 42:10 (]{.class_4938}["ye]{.class_4939}[ ]{.class_4938}[that go down to the sea"]{.class_4939}[). It is true that the ]{.class_4938}["isles"]{.class_4939}[ could be taken to be nothing more than Cyprus, Crete, Sicily, Corsica, Malta, etc., but Ezekiel 26:15, 18; 27:3, 6, 7--35 have overtones that don't match the commentators' opinions.]{.class_4938}

[The ones who "do business" in ]{.class_4938}["great waters" ]{.class_4939}[are the sailors, marines, merchant marines, coast guard, commercial fishermen, deep sea divers, and commercial passengers like Jonah (see Acts 27:2; 28:11, 13). The solitary illustration of God's ]{.class_4938}["wonders in the deep"]{.class_4939}[ is a ]{.class_4938}[storm at sea, ]{.class_4937}[although there are many, many other things. It was Charlie Darwin's sea voyage to the Galapagos Islands (1835) that got him to abandon reason and sanity for superstition and hallucinations. It was Columbus' voyage (1492) that opened the way for the founding of an anti Catholic nation in the west: the first one in a thousand years of history. America became THE refuge for Europeans fleeing state church systems (Anglican and Presbyterian as well as Lutheran and Catholic). It was Magellan's voyage (1519) that proved the Bible was right in its statements in Luke 17:31, 34 and Isaiah 40:22 and the scientists were wrong (as usual). An ocean sunset off Tampa or Manila is, in itself, a "wonder" to behold. The greatest seaman who ever lived didn't command a ship; he commanded an ARK, and what he saw out those portals in the period of a year would make your basic "wonder" or "wonderful work" look like a grocery shopping list. ]{.class_4938}[The Lusitania, The Titanic, The Queen Mary, The Queen Elizabeth, The Golden Hind ]{.class_4937}[(Sir Francis Drake), ]{.class_4938}[The Nina, The Pinta, The Santa Maria, The Yorktown, Old Ironsides, The Dorchester, The Prince of Wales, The Bismarck, The Bon Homme Richard, ]{.class_4937}[and ]{.class_4938}[The Graf Spee ]{.class_4937}[were ones that "did business on the great waters."]{.class_4938}

[God has delivered seamen from oil burning on water four acres in area. God has saved men swimming for their lives in a school of sharks. Eddie Rickenbacker, Louis Zamperini, and the survivors of the Bay of Pigs can tell you things about God's deliverance on rafts that would make a "believer" out of you. Paul's shipwreck (Acts 27) would be one in a list of over 500,000 that have taken place on this earth since Noah's day. I mean the big ones, not counting the motor boats and canoes that have run aground, and the skiffs and "smacks" that have crashed into rock jetties and piers. But here, we are dealing with one "wonder": a storm at sea. Storms at sea have been known to raise waves one hundred feet high, and in Noah's day, with the combined catastrophes of erupting volcanoes and earthquakes accompanying the downpour, there were probably tidal waves going around the equator at three hundred miles an hour. The ark was not made to sail anywhere; it was made to ]{.class_4938}[float.]{.class_4937}

[Verses 23--30 describe a storm at sea. The ones who are doing the ]{.class_4938}["business"]{.class_4939}[ ]{.class_4937}[are reeling to and fro like ]{.class_4938}["drunken men," ]{.class_4937}[who are usually vomiting. Modern technology doesn't have all the problems. If you have ever been packed in "below decks" with the hatches "battened" for three days at a time, you know how it goes. There is vomit all over the place, and prone men who can't even turn their head to look at a boatswain's mate or a "swabby" when he reels down the companionway. Sometimes the power goes out; sometimes the auxiliary power goes out. A steel ship rides the surf like a bucking bronco, so a hammock is better than a cot or a bunk. You are simply slung right and left as fast as the ship rolls, and forward and backwards as fast as it mounts the billows.]{.class_4938}

On the old time ships, it was even worse (see Acts 27:17--28). Sometimes the masts would split, the rigging would fall on the deck, killing men and tangling them in the lines. The gunwhales would cave in, and waves would wash over the deck, sweeping any men into the brine. The gun carriages would break loose from their lashings and go rolling back and forth across the deck, smashing in more gunwhales and taking off arms and legs if they rolled over a fallen man.

[Acts 27:14--29 describes a typical, wooden, sailing vessel in the grip of a "northeaster." To say that the men here are]{.class_4938}[ "at their wits' end"]{.class_4939}[ ]{.class_4937}[is the truth, for not ]{.class_4938}[one ]{.class_4937}[scientist in four thousand years has ever been able to invent ANYTHING that would stop nature when God orders her to go on a rampage. Modern scientists and "modern science" could do no more to stop a modern reoccurence of what took place in Noah's day---if God decided that was the appropriate play to call now---than the populace could in Noah's day. That is, four thousand years of "scientific advancement" really didn't "advance" anything.]{.class_4938}

[When the hurricane season comes to Florida (July--October, yearly), you can prepare to EVACUATE if the "eye" of a real howler is getting ready to cross you. When hurricane ]{.class_4938}[Camille]{.class_4937}[ hit, you had six options: 1. Run for your life. 2. Stay still and pray. 3. Run for your life and pray. 4. Curse and swear. 5. Get drugged or drunk. 6. Curse and get drunk. There are no other options. You had the same options they had in Noah's day (2340 B.C.). Science, Philosophy, Education, and modern inventions weren't even worth talking about when Camille hit. Nobody brought them up. After Camille cut her swathe through Mississippi, a Negro preacher who is a friend of mine, said, "Well bless my soul, ah reckon de good Lawd hurd some strange voices last night!" ]{.class_4938}[He sho' did. ]{.class_4937}[When a real, "sure enough" forest fire gets loose, in thirty mile an hour winds, or a real, "sure enough" flood gets loose, or a real, "sure enough" hurricane hits, you pray, swear, run, or do all three. ]{.class_4938}[Science ]{.class_4937}[does nothing but run for the nearest exit.]{.class_4938}

"[Then they cry unto the LORD" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 28), exactly like Jonah 1:5--6---]{.class_4938}["Then the mariners were afraid and cried every man unto his god\...arise! call upon thy God, if so be that God will think upon us that we perish not!" ]{.class_4939}[The winds and the sea obey their Maker (Matt. 8:27). ]{.class_4938}["He maketh the storm a calm" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 29) by saying ]{.class_4938}["Peace, be still" ]{.class_4939}[(Mark 4:39).]{.class_4938}

This sea motif occurs five times in the Bible.

[1. Noah in the Ark shows how a storm represents ]{.class_4938}[the wrath of God ]{.class_4937}[on sins and sinners.]{.class_4938}

[2. Jehoshophat's navy that is broken, so the ships cannot sail (1 Kings 22:48), shows the displeasure of God on ]{.class_4938}[unholy alliances ]{.class_4937}[His people make with the unsaved.]{.class_4938}

[3. Jonah's disastrous voyage shows the grace of God toward ]{.class_4938}[backsliders ]{.class_4937}[and God's ability to raise a man from the dead.]{.class_4938}

[4. The disciples' experience with Jesus Christ on Galilee (Matt. 8:25--27) shows God's ability to quiet the storms of life and ]{.class_4938}[control circumstances ]{.class_4937}[in the life of His children.]{.class_4938}

[5. The shipwreck that Paul was in demonstrates how God spares the lost ]{.class_4938}[for the sake of the saints present.]{.class_4937}

"[So he bringeth them unto their desired haven" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 30). ]{.class_4938}[Spiritually, ]{.class_4937}[New Jerusalem; ]{.class_4938}[practically, ]{.class_4937}[the port they intended to reach. The lesson is similar to the wandering of those in the wilderness, who finally wind up in the right ]{.class_4938}["city" ]{.class_4939}[(see vss. 4--7 and comments). ]{.class_4938}[Historically, ]{.class_4937}[in the Bible, it is in John 6:21, ]{.class_4938}["and immediately the ship was at the land whither they went."]{.class_4939}

[As a ]{.class_4938}[devotional ]{.class_4937}[sideline, have you noticed that there are no pleasure boats in the Bible? Every boat, from David's ]{.class_4938}["ferry boat" ]{.class_4939}[(2 Sam. 19:18) to Solomon's navy (1 Kings 9:26), is either for trade, fishing, or travel. The Christian's vessel (2 Cor. 4:7) is not for ]{.class_4938}["the pleasures of sin for a season" ]{.class_4939}[(Heb. 11:25). ]{.class_4938}[It is the Lord's ]{.class_4937}[(1 Cor. 6:13).]{.class_4938}

[Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown have the watery scene as only "another image of Israel's distressed condition\...the sea is an image of the restless agitation of the world powers on which Israel\...is cast\... The ship is the SPIRITUAL CHURCH OF CHRIST which is safe through His presence\...]{.class_4938}[Metamorphically, ]{.class_4937}[as here\...the waves are called the waves of his people as they toss them up and down\...."]{.class_4938}

[Richard Hampolitanus (]{.class_4938}[Treasury]{.class_4937}[) says, "they that are true prelates and preachers go down from the sublimity of contemplation (to the sea, that is) suiting themselves to the lowly, that they also might be saved in ships, that is in the faith, hope, and charity of the church, without which they would be drowned in the waters of pleasure\...."]{.class_4938}

Or try it this way: "The salesmen who go down to the sea, meet disk jockeys who go down to the bowling alley to save Green Stamps at the snack counter, so their priest can get a personalized carrying case for his bowling ball."

107:32 "Let them exalt him also in the congregation of the people, and praise him in the assembly of the elders.

33 He turneth rivers into a wilderness, and the watersprings into dry ground;

34 A fruitful land into barrenness, for the wickedness of them that dwell therein.

35 He turneth the wilderness into a standing water, and dry ground into watersprings.

36 And there he maketh the hungry to dwell, that they may prepare a city for habitation;

37 And sow the fields, and plant vineyards, which may yield fruits of increase.

38 He blesseth them also, so that they are multiplied greatly; and suffereth not their cattle to decrease.

39 Again, they are minished and brought low through oppression, affliction, and sorrow.

40 He poureth contempt upon princes, and causeth them to wander in the wilderness, where there is no way.

41 Yet setteth he the poor on high from affliction, and maketh him families like a flock.

42 The righteous shall see it, and rejoice: and all iniquity shall stop her mouth,

43 Whoso is wise, and will observe these things, even they shall understand the lovingkindness of the LORD."

[Practically speaking, ]{.class_4937}[verse 32 instructs the seaman (who survived the storm) to go into a local church and publicly praise God and thank Him for getting home safe. ]{.class_4938}[Doctrinally, ]{.class_4937}[it is Israel again after the Tribulation. This is apparent by what follows, for the history of Israel is condensed into about ten verses, in regards to the judgments of God on the land.]{.class_4938}

[Verses 33 and 34 reveal what happened to Israel between A.D. 33 and A.D. 1914. Showing color slides on Palestine, and quoting passages like Deuteronomy 11:11--12 and Ezekiel 20:15 while doing it, is like showing color slides of west Texas and talking about Palm Springs. I was once offered a free, round-trip ticket to Israel, if I would lead a guided tour of Christians. Jokingly I said, "You all go ahead. Jes' dump me off in Frankfurt, and I'll join you on the way back." The tour bought me a round-trip ticket to Germany, and I've been there four times in the last ten years. Palestine, alongside the Austrian Tyrol or the Bavarian Alps, looks like the Oklahoma panhandle. I believe I had read the Bible through more than ninety times before I began to see why God bragged so much about such a small, desolate place. It doesn't have one mountain the size of McKinley or Mt. Whitney, let alone Mt. Blanc or Mt. Ararat. It doesn't have one river the size of the Ohio or the Rhine, let alone the Missouri or the Mississippi. And the whole country doesn't have as much pasture as one county in Montana. I was nearly sixty four years old before I saw that God brags about that country because it is where His Son died, and that is where His Son is going to reign. ]{.class_4938}[Fathers are interested in their sons.]{.class_4937}

[Ahab vs. Elijah (1 Kings 17--18) shows what God does to a land when it lives like America has lived since 1945. No wonder the "environmentalists" and "ecologists" are in an "Earth Day" panic. They think you can have clean air and beautiful forests along with abortion and sex perversion. ]{.class_4938}[Guess again ]{.class_4937}[(Amos 4--7).]{.class_4938}

[Verse 35 would be in "reverse"---referring to the matters in Exodus and Numbers---if it were not for the fact that those things will take place again in the near future, which could be long ]{.class_4938}[after ]{.class_4937}[the desolations of A.D. 70 to 1914. It is the failure to locate the ]{.class_4938}["city for habitation," ]{.class_4939}[in the next verse, that causes 100 percent of the commentators to quench the Holy Spirit and shut out light from the Word. Since we have already located this city (see verses 4 and 7), we know where we are. We are in Revelation 12:14--16 and Hosea 2:14--17, according to the format laid out for us in Psalm 78:1--2. Observe, for the "umpteen-hundredth time," how Scripture with Scripture interprets Scripture (often completely contrary to the entire body of Conservative scholarship for nineteen centuries) without ONE reference to ONE Hebrew or Greek word in ONE Hebrew or Greek manuscript, and without the help of ONE Hebrew or Greek scholar from ]{.class_4938}[ANY ]{.class_4937}[century in church history, on ]{.class_4938}[ANY ]{.class_4937}[continent on earth.]{.class_4938}

(As daddy said, when we emerged from the woodshed: "Let THAT be a lesson to you!")

[The other ]{.class_4938}["day"]{.class_4939}[ of which David spoke (Ps. 95:8; Heb. 4:8) will be so apparent to the Jewish remnant that, this time, there will be no defection of even a thousand armed men. God is going to ]{.class_4938}[repeat ]{.class_4937}[the conditions of the first exodus (Exod. 20:35--38). ]{.class_4938}["He turneth the wilderness into a standing water."]{.class_4939}[ But THIS time, they will be running for their lives leaving Jerusalem, not escaping from Egypt. And this time, instead of the Red Sea parting (the actual literal one), the ground is going to open up and swallow water cast after them (Rev. 12:15--16; Dan. 9:26). There is nothing like an archaic, Elizabethean ]{.class_4938}[King James Bible ]{.class_4937}[to clean up a rat's nest in the Scholar's Union, composed of Bible correcting "Dickkopfs" who think the sun rises and sets on their own scholarship. (You can see now why Dr. Church's book on ]{.class_4938}[Prophecy in the Psalms]{.class_4937}[ is treated with such contempt. He missed more Biblical prophecy in two Psalms \[Ps. 68 and 83\] than John Calvin did in Matt. 24). You see, when the Lord said: ]{.class_4938}["and all iniquity shall stop her mouth" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 42), no "godly, faithful, devoted, accredited, recognized, Conservative scholar" was able to run the passage back into Exodus. All iniquity "stops her mouth" in Daniel 9:24, and NOT ONE DAY BEFORE. A "faithful" expositor would have believed what God said, and a really educated scholar---a ]{.class_4938}["wise"]{.class_4939}[ man (vs. 43)---would ]{.class_4938}["observe"]{.class_4939}[ (vs. 43) it. They didn't.]{.class_4938}

[To utterly confuse and confound the Scholar's Union, the Lord has put two wanderings in the wilderness into the passage: one at verse 35 and the other at verse 40. This makes it appear that verses 35 and 36 take place ]{.class_4938}[before ]{.class_4937}[the possession of the land of Canaan (vss. 37--38). The problem is the ]{.class_4938}["princes"]{.class_4939}[ wandering in the wilderness AFTER the occupation of the land of Canaan. None of them ]{.class_4938}["wander in the wilderness" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 40), and no matter how you force verse 41 into Jeremiah 39:10 and 2 Kings 25:12, you still must deal with verse 42, and verse 42 can't be dealt with in Jeremiah or 2 Kings or Exodus or Numbers. (Or 1 and 2 Samuel, for that matter.)]{.class_4938}

[When Spurgeon hits verse 40, his pen rolls off the desk. The ]{.class_4938}["princes" ]{.class_4939}[in the wilderness are simply "too much." John Calvin and William Walford just pretend there is no reference to any princes wandering in the wilderness ]{.class_4938}["where there is no way." ]{.class_4939}[(I presume that Calvin and Walford believed only the last four words in the verse: ]{.class_4938}["there is no way."]{.class_4939}[) There was no way, Jose, that ]{.class_4938}[they ]{.class_4937}[could handle the verse. Kroll (Liberty University) is entirely in agreement, and Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown make it unanimous. There never was, nor ever will be, any ]{.class_4938}["princes" ]{.class_4939}[wandering in the wilderness, according to ]{.class_4938}[every commentator ]{.class_4937}[that ever attempted Psalm 107. We must assume either that God didn't know what He was talking about, or that He wasn't able to tell anyone what He was talking about. I will take a third option. I will assume that 100 percent of the "good, godly, dedicated, soul winning, fundamental, "Bible believing" (!) scholars are just as UNFAITHFUL as Demas or Diotrophes when it comes to any word or verse in the Bible they don't ]{.class_4938}[like ]{.class_4937}[or can't ]{.class_4938}[understand.]{.class_4937}

Let us not be as perfidious as Kroll, Motyer, Dummelow, Lange, Clarke, Ellicott, Yates, Baethgen, and company. Let us---just to be different!---first compare Scripture with Scripture, and then let us go to the "original Hebrew," which all of our Hebrew scholars conveniently overlooked.

[Look at Hannah's song in 1 Samuel 2. There, is the ROCK (vs. 2). There, war has come to an end (vs. 4). There, the ]{.class_4938}["princes" ]{.class_4939}[have gone completely "to pot" (vs. 5). There, the Lord is doing what He said He would do at the Second Advent (Deut. 32:39 with 1 Sam. 2:6), and there are the very people of Psalm 107:41, elevated to security and dignity just like Hannah said (vs. 7). But the context is what is going to "tan" the hides of the Alexandrians: ]{.class_4938}["and to make them inherit the throne of glory" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 8). By now (see Ps. 2:6; 9:4; 45:6; 47:8; 89:4, 29, 36; Matt. 19:28, and Jer. 3:17), no amount of ignorance or infidelity could blind a normal reader. The context is "inheriting the kingdom," as given in Matthew 25:31--34; Ephesians 5:5; and Colossians 3:24. First Samuel 2:10 finishes off the Scholar's Union and neatly wraps, packages, and mails them to the South Pole. ]{.class_4938}["Iniquity shall stop her mouth"]{.class_4939}[ (Ps. 107:42) when 1 Samuel 2:10 takes place, ]{.class_4938}[and not one hour before. ]{.class_4937}[Got it? You got it out of Scripture (]{.class_4938}[AV ]{.class_4937}[1611) if you "got it" at all, and if you looked to Christian scholarship for authority and fidelity you ]{.class_4938}[didn't ]{.class_4937}[get it; it's that simple.]{.class_4938}

[Now, let us steal into Delitzsch's study (or Duhm's or Keil's) and "snitch" us a man written book made by man for man: a Hebrew lexicon. Ah! Here it is: "tohu" (תהו). Well, bless my soul! Isn't that that word that the Alexandrians made such a stink about back in Genesis 1:2, when they were trying to prove that there was (or wasn't) a "gap" after Genesis 1:1? Yep, that's the word: "tohu." The ]{.class_4938}[AV ]{.class_4937}[translators said it meant ]{.class_4938}["void." ]{.class_4939}[This ]{.class_4938}["wilderness" ]{.class_4939}[then, in Psalm 107:40, is a place that is ]{.class_4938}["without form, and void," ]{.class_4939}[and none of us have to guess WHERE it is or WHEN it will show up again, for Jeremiah 4:23 tells us it will take place again and when it will take place. Look at Jeremiah 4:31--- ]{.class_4938}["I have heard a voice as of a woman in travail\...the voice of the daughter of Zion." ]{.class_4939}[You are right back at the end of Daniel's Seventieth Week again (Isa. 54:1; Micah 4:10; John 16:2; Rev. 12:12--16). Isn't it amazing how much emphasis the Holy Spirit (in His Book) puts on the closing years of this age? His "balance" is completely contrary to the theologians and expositors in the Church Age, who want all the verses in the Old Testament either buried in the historical past or spiritualized for appropriation by ]{.class_4938}[the church. ]{.class_4937}[But the verses resurrect themselves every time you bury them, and you cannot stop their fulfillment by pretending they are "images," "metaphors," "poetic," "figurative language," or "symbolic language." The WORDS of God are alive (Heb. 4:12).]{.class_4938}

[1. This earth will be a wilderness after the Tribulation (see the exact statement with the exact wording, in exactly the same context: Isa. 14:17). The scholars had run head on into Satan again and refused to expose him---]{.class_4938}[again]{.class_4937}[.]{.class_4938}

[2. The ]{.class_4938}["princes"]{.class_4939}[ are worldwide at this time: Psalm 76:12, 82:7 (note here, the ]{.class_4938}["princes"]{.class_4939}[ are FALLEN ANGELS), 83:11; Isa. 19:13, 31:9, 41:23, 41:25; Ezek. 32:30, 39:18, etc. These ]{.class_4938}["princes"]{.class_4939}[ wander through destroyed cities and ruined towns after God has unleashed what you read about in Revelation 6, 8--11, 14--16.]{.class_4938}

[3. ]{.class_4938}["The lords of the heathen" ]{.class_4939}[(Isa. 16:8) ]{.class_4938}["wandered through the wilderness" ]{.class_4939}[without being detected by Hengstenberg, Delitzsch, Bullinger, Clarke, Keil, Yates, Kroll, Hupfeld, Ewald, Gesenius, Perowne, Spurgeon, or any of their friends, mentors, peers, or associates. [Psalm 107:40 was literal ]{.class_4938}[prophecy;]{.class_4937}[ that is why Kroll, Duhm, Briggs, Kay, DeWette, Davidson, Dillman, Yates, and Geiger couldn't handle it. That is why Spurgeon got writer's cramp when he hit the verse, and so did his compatriots: John Calvin and William Walford.]{.class_4938}

"[Whoso is wise, and will observe these things" ]{.class_4939}[(and God knows, the Scholar's Union never observes anything in the Scripture where Scripture interprets Scripture on a "hard case") ]{.class_4938}["Even they shall understand the lovingkindness of the LORD." ]{.class_4939}[It is the ]{.class_4938}["lovingkindness of the LORD" ]{.class_4939}[that turns the earth into a pock marked Dust Bowl and destroys the One World Global leaders with their "One World Citizens." If you doubt it, look at Psalm 136. You are told there that it was God's "mercy" that killed babies in Egypt, drowned Pharaoh, and slew Sihon and Og (vss. 10, 15, 19--20). ]{.class_4938}["These things," ]{.class_4939}[applied to the general tone of the Psalm, are clear, for the general tone of the Psalm (which ends with victory: vs. 42) is God delivering men out of trouble even though they don't deserve to be delivered. That is the theme, ]{.class_4938}[spiritually. ]{.class_4937}[It is like Corporal Wingate's confession while under counter-artillery bombardment in the Reichswald forest (1945). He has been slightly wounded and must "play dead" while a German counterattack goes past him. At this time, the "umbrella barrages" of the English and German batteries bracket his position, both of them stopping about fifty yards short of him. To get the "feeling" for the corporal's position, you need to go out onto the fairway of a golf course during an "electrical storm" in Florida, in August, and lie prone for two hours while the bolts strike between thirty and one hundred feet from you. In such a "posture," Wingate is confessing all the sins he ever committed and several he never committed, and in an effort to redeem himself, he is promising God anything short of becoming a monk in order to save his own "worthless, rotten neck." Those are the corporal's own words. That is the spiritual and devotional theme of Psalm 107 (see verses 11, 17, 34, 39).]{.class_4938}

[The last verse is simple. If you want to understand the ]{.class_4938}["lovingkindness of the Lord,"]{.class_4939}[ as well as ]{.class_4938}["the fear of the LORD" ]{.class_4939}[(see the lengthy comments on vs. 11), you must study and meditate on "case histories" of sinners who have been shown mercy and undeserved GRACE. ]{.class_4938}[Mephibosheth]{.class_4937}[ is a good case (2 Sam. 9:1--8). ]{.class_4938}[Mary Magdalene ]{.class_4937}[is a good case (Mark 16:9). ]{.class_4938}[Manasseh]{.class_4937}[ is an outstanding case (2 Chron. 33:9--14), as is ]{.class_4938}[Jehoiachin ]{.class_4937}[(2 Kings 25:27--28). Paul is a real case (Acts 26:11). Outside the canon of Scripture these marvels of ]{.class_4938}["lovingkindness" ]{.class_4939}[are legion (see Eph. 2:1--9): George Mueller, a thief and a gambler; Billy Sunday, a drunken orphan; Jim Vaus, a thief and a wire tapper; Nicky Cruz, a thief and a brawler; George Myers, a robber and a convict; John Newton, an indescribable degenerate; Edmund Dinant, a gangster and a bootlegger; Mel Trotter, a hopeless drunk; Baxter McClendon, a gambler and a killer; General Keitel, Adolph Hitler's chief of staff; Paul Geri and Herman Fountain, hopeless dope addicts, etc. "Amazing GRACE how sweet the sound that saved a wretch like me\...Twas GRACE that taught my heart to fear, and grace my fears relieved\...."]{.class_4938}

"[Whoso is wise, and will observe these things, even they shall understand the lovingkindness of the LORD."]{.class_4939}

PSALM 108

108:1 "O God, my heart is fixed; I will sing and give praise, even with my glory.

2 Awake, psaltery and harp: I myself will awake early.

3 I will praise thee, O LORD, among the people: and I will sing praises unto thee among the nations.

4 For thy mercy is great above the heavens: and thy truth reacheth unto the clouds.

5 Be thou exalted, O God, above the heavens: and thy glory above all the earth;

6 That thy beloved may be delivered: save with thy right hand, and answer me.

7 God hath spoken in his holiness; I will rejoice, I will divide Shechem, and mete out the valley of Succoth.

8 Gilead is mine; Manasseh is mine; Ephraim also is the strength of mine head; Judah is my lawgiver;

9 Moab is my washpot; over Edom will I cast out my shoe; over Philistia will I triumph.

10 Who will bring me into the strong city? who will lead me into Edom?

11 Wilt not thou, O God, who hast cast us off? and wilt not thou, O God, go forth with our hosts?

12 Give us help from trouble: for vain is the help of man.

13 Through God we shall do valiantly: for he it is that shall tread down our enemies."

Anyone can recognize the repetition of Psalm 57:7--11 in the first five verses of the Psalm. Again, verses 7--10 seem to be lifted right out of Psalm 60:6--9, which see. Since we have commented on both passages, we will say little more here than we have already said.

[This time R. H. Ryland (]{.class_4938}[Treasury]{.class_4937}[) comes in "on the money," for he tells us the whole Psalm should be connected with the Messiah coming as a conquerer to sit on David's throne, as given in Psalm 2:6. He has the kings and nations "prostrate" at the Messiah's feet, where they will be, and this time he does not try to "beat the dealer" by pretending this happened in A.D. 33 after Acts 1. Instead, Ryland runs the "conquering hero" right into Revelation 19:13--16 when He comes with ]{.class_4938}["a vesture dipped in blood," ]{.class_4939}[and, of course, that is the truth.]{.class_4938}

[The ]{.class_4938}["I"]{.class_4939}[ in the Psalm is David praying for Israel, and the ]{.class_4938}["US"]{.class_4939}[ and ]{.class_4938}["our"]{.class_4939}[ of verses 11--13 (]{.class_4938}["we"]{.class_4939}[) is plainly Israel.]{.class_4938}

[The word ]{.class_4938}["trouble" ]{.class_4939}[occurs over fifty times in the Psalms. Most of man's trouble is "heart trouble" (Prov. 4:23). In the old days, preachers used "shock treatments" long before the shrinks started electrotherapy. These shock treatments were called "brush arbor meetings," and if you think they didn't shock, just get up on a stump on the campus of any college or university in America today and preach Matthew 23 or 2 Timothy 4. You'll learn quickly that human nature hasn't changed any since 1800.]{.class_4938}
