PSALM 109

109:1 "Hold not thy peace, O God of my praise;

2 For the mouth of the wicked and the mouth of the deceitful are opened against me: they have spoken against me with a lying tongue.

3 They compassed me about also with words of hatred; and fought against me without a cause.

4 For my love they are my adversaries: but I give myself unto prayer.

5 And they have rewarded me evil for good, and hatred for my love."

[Here is THE place to watch a liberal-minded Conservative or a liberal hearted Fundamentalist go into his "act." This will be THE opportunity for every super-pious, false-burdened "do gooder" to demonstrate how ]{.class_4938}[loving ]{.class_4937}[and ]{.class_4938}[kind ]{.class_4937}[he is, and how anxious he is to present a Saviour that "wouldn't hurt a fly."]{.class_4938}

[The Psalm is attributed to David, and it is what is called an "imprecatory" Psalm, which simply means that David is praying that God will kill and damn someone he dislikes. You don't have to be a seer to see how this will effect every saved and lost "do gooder" in America. The greatest cuss words in this century for "global citizens" are the words "SEPARATION" and "division," for both of these words are ]{.class_4938}[negative. ]{.class_4937}["Negativism" is ]{.class_4938}[the ]{.class_4937}[sin of the twentieth century in the eyes of evolutionists, homosexuals, race mixers, socialists, Catholics, Charismatics, Bible scholars, fornicators, and TV "evangelists." Here, we run into a Psalm that is 52 percent negative, and the "negativism" here goes far beyond such things as talking about ]{.class_4938}["the fear of the LORD."]{.class_4939}[ This time it extends to asking God to destroy a man's children and damn him to Hell at the White Throne Judgment. Don't you know that Stewart Custer (Bob Jones University), James Price (Tennessee Temple), James Combs (BBC), Arlin Horton (PCC), Dayton Hobbs (Santa Rosa Schools), Curtis Hutson (]{.class_4938}[Sword of the Lord]{.class_4937}[), Chuck Swindoll, and Kroll (Liberty University) aren't going to take to ]{.class_4938}[that ]{.class_4937}[like a duck takes to water?]{.class_4938}

[The first five verses can go to David's credit or to Christ's. Saul (a great type of the Son of Perdition) could be guilty of verse 2, and so would Shimei, Absalom, and Doeg the Edomite (1 Sam. 22:9). David had nothing against any of them (vss. 3--4), but a believing student can see, already, that the discourse is getting away from David's ]{.class_4938}[historical ]{.class_4937}[problems. If David is "giving himself to prayer" in love for Saul, Absalom, Doeg, Shimei, et al., it is a strange "love prayer," for the prayer that follows is nothing but one vicious diatribe of the most venomous nature imaginable. If the verses are applied to Jesus Christ (vss. 1--5), a great deal can be cleared up, for His love was absolutely manifest (Matt. 23:37--38; 1 Tim. 1:15), and His enemies ]{.class_4938}[did ]{.class_4937}[reward Him ]{.class_4938}["evil for good" ]{.class_4939}[(see Acts 10:38, 2:22), and He did "give Himself to prayer" (Matt. 26:39; Mark 1:35; Luke 5:16, 9:29, 22:32, etc.). The problem---and believe me, to a modern, apostate Fundamentalist like Panosian, Custer, Bob Jones IV, Arlin Horton, Ronald Walker, Dell, Sherman, Afman, and Hodges it is a problem---is how to make the Lord Jesus look respectable and "loving" in the eyes of unsaved humanitarians and ]{.class_4938}[backslidden Christians, ]{.class_4937}["whose God is their belly" ]{.class_4939}[(Phil. 3:19). ]{.class_4938}[That ]{.class_4937}[is the problem.]{.class_4938}

109:6 "Set thou a wicked man over him: and let Satan stand at his right hand.

7 When he shall be judged, let him be condemned: and let his prayer become sin.

8 Let his days be few; and let another take his office.

9 Let his children be fatherless, and his wife a widow.

10 Let his children be continually vagabonds, and beg: let them seek their bread also out of their desolate places.

11 Let the extortioner catch all that he hath; and let the strangers spoil his labour.

12 Let there be none to extend mercy unto him: neither let there be any to favour his fatherless children.

13 Let his posterity be cut off, and in the generation following let their name be blotted out.

14 Let the iniquity of his fathers be remembered with the LORD; and let not the sin of his mother be blotted out.

15 Let them be before the LORD continually, that he may cut off the memory of them from the earth.

16 Because that he remembered not to shew mercy, but persecuted the poor and needy man, that he might even slay the broken in heart.

17 As he loved cursing, so let it come unto him: as he delighted not in blessing, so let it be far from him.

18 As he clothed himself with cursing like as with his garment, so let it come into his bowels like water, and like oil into his bones.

19 Let it be unto him as the garment which covereth him, and for a girdle wherewith he is girded continually.

20 Let this be the reward of mine adversaries from the LORD, and of them that speak evil against my soul."

[Read it. Read the whole passage, and read it slowly. Could it pass on a "vesper" program at Bob Jones on Sunday afternoon? How about a little "Scripture" reading for today's "vespers," with all the ]{.class_4938}[parents ]{.class_4937}[and ]{.class_4938}[friends ]{.class_4937}[of the student body present!]{.class_4938}

[Here we go (with all of the measured, clipped, precise diction of]{.class_4938}[ ]{.class_4937}[professionals who have been taught in the "Speech Department" at PCC or BJU!): ]{.class_4938}["Set thou a wicked man over him\...let him be condemned\...let his prayer become sin\...let his days be few\...let his children be fatherless\...and beg\...let the extortioner catch all that he hath\...none to extend mercy unto him\...let his posterity be cut off\...their name be blotted out\...." ]{.class_4939}[Damn him (vs. 7), turn him over to the Devil (vs. 6), damn his mother with him (vs. 14), clothe him with "damns" (vss. 18--19), etc.]{.class_4938}

[How can you raise money for a new field house or a bell tower with that kind of stuff coming from your chapel platform?]{.class_4937}[ You can't. You can't serve ]{.class_4938}[God ]{.class_4937}[and ]{.class_4938}[Mammon]{.class_4937}[. No one ever has, even when they thought they were doing it (see 1 Tim. 6:5, 10 and comments in that commentary). Watch Kroll "tiptoeing through the tithers." "The primary reference of this Psalm is to be seen in the historic events of the life of David." This absolves the Lord Jesus of the theological mayhem to follow, but then again, it makes a perfect liar out of David. Is Kroll telling us that after David has "resolved not to retaliate" he suddenly ]{.class_4938}[broke ]{.class_4937}[his New Year's resolutions and "lashes out imprecations against those who slandered him"? Yes, that is exactly what he says. Feeling that what he has just said doesn't make any sense at all---and it certainly doesn't---Kroll backtracks in typical Alexandrian fashion: "Throughout the ages sages have been perplexed at this Psalm, for what is now said is so out of character for David."]{.class_4938}

[Is it "out of character" for Jesus Christ? ]{.class_4938}[He didn't say.]{.class_4937}

[Like any ]{.class_4938}[unsaved religious Liberal ]{.class_4937}[in the NCC, Kroll (with Stewart Custer at Bob Jones University) will not believe the truth when the truth is negative. So here, Kroll says: "It is difficult to see how the words of this Psalm could be placed in the mouth of our ]{.class_4938}[Lord Jesus."]{.class_4937}

[Well, Kroll, ole' buddy, that WOULD be difficult for ]{.class_4938}[your ]{.class_4937}["Lord Jesus," but for the Lord Jesus who spoke Matthew 23:3--35; Romans 11:9--10; Deuteronomy 28:18--35, 32:22--25; Jeremiah 13:14, 21:4--7; Hosea 1:6, 2:4; and Isaiah 34:5, 43:28, 65:15, there wouldn't be any "difficulty." Your problem, Kroll---and every apostate Fundamentalist like you---is that your "Lord Jesus" is not the Lord Jesus of ]{.class_4938}[the Bible. ]{.class_4937}[He is ]{.class_4938}["another Jesus" ]{.class_4939}[(2 Cor. 11:4). See how these rascals get rid of the Deity of Christ while ]{.class_4938}[professing ]{.class_4937}[it? They cut His connection with Jehovah, the God of the Old Testament. Watch Kroll: "An obvious distinction must be made between the Old Testament covenant of the Law and the New Testament covenant of Grace" (Kroll, p. 1132). Is that so? Are you telling us that the prayer is legitimate ]{.class_4938}[before ]{.class_4937}[Matthew 27? Then what is Judas doing in Acts 1:20 as a ]{.class_4938}[fulfillment ]{.class_4937}[of this Psalm (vs. 8)? Didn't Kroll know that Judas was ]{.class_4938}["the son of perdition" ]{.class_4939}[(John 17:12), and would show up more than 1,900 years ]{.class_4938}[after ]{.class_4937}[the New Testament covenant had been in effect? Evidently not. "Men of the Old Testament stage of revelation did not have the example of the Saviour nor His teachings concerning ]{.class_4938}[love and justice\...." ]{.class_4937}[Izzat so?]{.class_4938}

"[Ye serpents, ye generation of vipers\...ye fools and blind\...ye blind guides\...ye are of your father the devil\...Depart from me, ye cursed, into everlasting fire\...if the blind lead the blind, both shall fall into the ditch\...how can ye escape the damnation of hell?" ]{.class_4939}[Kroll says that the prayer of Psalm 109 is "inexcusable" (p. 1132) in the light of the New Testament and was simply "overlooked" in the Old Testament.]{.class_4938}

Thank you, Harry Emerson Fosdick and Norman Vincent Peale! Now we know why Kroll hasn't led ten people to Christ in the last twenty years.

[Now, for a "historic Baptist position" from the Metropolitan Pulpit in London: "These \[words\] are not consistent with the silent Lamb of God who opened not His mouth when led to the slaughter. It may seem very pious to put such words in His mouth; we hope it is our piety which prevents us from doing so." Wrong again, Charlie; it was your ]{.class_4938}[unbelief ]{.class_4937}[that prevented you from "doing so," while putting on a false piety to impress your readers. Joe Hammond (one of Spurgeon's commentators) goes into an Immelmann loop to get through the Psalm. He says that it actually is a record of the prayers of David's enemies against him (]{.class_4938}[Treasury]{.class_4937}[, p. 445)! Chrysostom, who had the passage right ("a prophecy in the form of a curse") is contemptuously dismissed by Spurgeon's good friend Mr. Perowne, who says that "the strain which such a view compels us to put on much of the language, ought to have led long ago to its abandonment." Delitzsch is just as faithless and fraudulent: "These imprecations are not appropriate in the mouth of the suffering Saviour." Donald Fraser (1873) assures us that the Old Testament was a "stern dispensation," unlike ours (the Crusades, Titus in A.D. 70, the Gothic invasions, the War of Roses, the Hundred Years War, the Thirty Years War, the French and American Revolutions, the English and American Civil Wars, the Napoleonic campaigns, World War I, World War II, the Franco Prussian War, the Spanish American War, the War of 1812, the Inquisition, the Holocaust, Vietnam, Korea, etc.). "The tone of the dispensation has changed" so no one now desires to ]{.class_4938}["tread down their enemies"]{.class_4939}[ (Ps. 68) and ]{.class_4938}["wash his feet in the blood of the wicked"]{.class_4939}[ (Ps. 68:23).]{.class_4938}

Well, old Donny Fraser and his buddies are in for a surprise (Rev. 14:20), aren't they?

[F. H. Dunwell is distracted out of his wits (if he had any) when he finds ]{.class_4938}["children" ]{.class_4939}[in verse 12. He hastens to tell us that these are not really "children." They are those who "resemble" the wicked man or those who "partake" with him. Anything but believe the BOOK. What happened to Dunwell's mind when he read Matthew 27:25? What would this poor, Bible-correcting, Bible-perverting dunce have done between 1939 and 1945 when the literal "children" were lined up by the thousands to go into the gas chambers? Do you know what these spiritual, super pious apostates are actually saying? They are saying that ]{.class_4938}[the words ]{.class_4937}[that God spoke in Deuteronomy 32 (and especially Deut. 28:63) are not to be taken ]{.class_4938}[seriously ]{.class_4937}[because ]{.class_4938}[Jesus Christ is NOT the God of the Old Testament.]{.class_4937}

That is the message of modern "Fundamentalism," if it is the high class, "moneyed" Fundamentalism that majors in plush campuses and "scholarship."

Let us now turn to Psalm 109 with a believing heart, instead of the heart of a "scholarly" Conservative.

[Most of the verses are very plain. The condition of the petitioner is that he is poor and needy, his heart is wounded, his knees are weak, he is skinny from fasting (vs. 24), he is a reproach to his neighbors (see Ps. 31:11), and people are shaking their head at him (see Ps. 22:7). The petitioner is the ]{.class_4938}[Lord Jesus Christ, ]{.class_4937}[according to material in verses 3, 8, and 25. A ]{.class_4938}["shadow" ]{.class_4939}[declines quickly in the evening (sundial) and gets fainter and fainter till there is no shadow (vs. 23). To move, the locust almost has to move with the wind, and when it is a strong wind (see Exod. 10:4, 13) he always moves with it. A sudden gust of air will toss two thousand locusts up, down, or sidewise, as one unit. The prayer of verse 26, from Christ's standpoint, is a prayer like that of Hebrews 5:7, about which we have already made comment (see Ps. 40:17, 6:4--6, 7:1--5, and especially Ps. 20:4--6).]{.class_4938}

[Having removed the Lord Jesus from the place of prayer, the commentators now find themselves "between a rock and a hard place." Verse 25 fits Jesus Christ to the letter, so ]{.class_4938}[now ]{.class_4937}[none of the pious "spiritualists" who shoved Him out of verses 6--20 can make any application. The commentators in Spurgeon (p. 457) suddenly decide to skip four verses (vss. 24--27). Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown are too honest to ignore Psalm 22:6--7 but are too cowardly to face it, so they write the bare reference with ]{.class_4938}[no comment ]{.class_4937}[as to its connection with Jesus Christ. Charles H. Spurgeon doesn't dare talk Scripture. Instead he gives us this: "They made him the theme of ridicule \[an anonymous "him" with no reference to who "him" is\], the butt of their ribald jests; his emaciation by fasting \[the "his" again is anonymous\] made him a tempting subject\...they resorted to gestures \[an anonymous, unidentified "they"\] which were meant to show both their derision \[not one word on whom "their" refers to\]\...many a man who could have answered a malicious speech\...has felt keenly a sneer, a putting out of the tongue, or some other sign of contempt\...."]{.class_4938}

[Spurgeon confines the entire verse to some troubles ]{.class_4938}[somebody ]{.class_4937}[can have from ]{.class_4938}[somebody, ]{.class_4937}[at some time. This is the fruit of trying to play the pious "spiritual giant." Spurgeon doesn't know about what he is talking, so he just generalizes the verse and applies it ]{.class_4938}[doctrinally ]{.class_4937}[to nothing. He was gullible enough to believe John Augustus Dathe (1731--1791), who talked him out of believing the prayer warrior in the passage was Jesus Christ. Dathe, in a mighty blast of Alexandrian buffoonery, tells us the reason that any Bible believer made the mistake of thinking Christ was praying the imprecations was because of the influence of Syriac Christians on the ]{.class_4938}[King James' ]{.class_4937}[translators. Again, he tells that the ]{.class_4938}[AV ]{.class_4937}[should have followed the LXX and the Hebrew more carefully in translating the "singulars" in the passage (]{.class_4938}[Treasury]{.class_4937}[, p. 447). Spurgeon believed ]{.class_4938}[him ]{.class_4937}[instead of the ]{.class_4938}[AV ]{.class_4937}[text of the Holy Bible. ]{.class_4938}["All have sinned, and come short of the glory of God\...the fear of man bringeth a snare\...they loved the praise of men more than the praise of God."]{.class_4939}

"[When they arise" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 28), they will be ashamed; both when they rise up in the Tribulation and when they rise up at the Final Judgment (Dan. 12:2). Christ will ]{.class_4938}["rejoice"]{.class_4939}[ (vs. 28) because Isaiah said He would (Isa. 62:5). See also Psalm 13:5, 58:10, and 63:7, 11.]{.class_4938}

"[Set thou a wicked man over him: and let Satan stand at his right hand" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 6). The prayer is answered. Satan will stand at the right hand of the Antichrist to sustain him, uphold him, and empower him (Rev. 13:4--7). Note again the familiar use of the plural (]{.class_4938}["they"]{.class_4939}[ in vss. 2--5) for ]{.class_4938}["him."]{.class_4939}[ Satan incarnate will be a trinity, and he will appear as a ]{.class_4938}["man"]{.class_4939}[ (see Isa. 14:16). This is a prayer that the Son of Perdition (and the devil) will be the helpers for Judas Iscariot---the one whom Christ "in His sufferings" (to quote the misleading location of the theologians) called ]{.class_4938}["friend" ]{.class_4939}[(Matt. 26:50). The prophecy goes way beyond the first advent of Judas to the second advent of Judas (see 2 Thess. 2:1--8), and this is what pins the apostates to the mat. They never located ]{.class_4938}["Apollyon" ]{.class_4939}[(Rev. 9:11), although their Greek lexicons---to which they referred ]{.class_4938}[twice per verse ]{.class_4937}[when correcting the King ]{.class_4938}[James Bible]{.class_4937}[---told them that it meant ]{.class_4938}["perdition" ]{.class_4939}[(Rev. 17:8), as in ]{.class_4938}["the son of perdition."]{.class_4939}

[The petitioner is Jesus Christ, and the prayer is prayed ]{.class_4938}[after ]{.class_4937}[His sufferings, as well as during His sufferings; so all the floss candy schmaltz about the "silent Lamb who opened not His mouth when led to the slaughter" was just sugar coating for a strychnine pill. Jesus Christ was saying things while He was being slaughtered (Isa. 50:7--9) that no man on this earth ever heard; not even the ones whipping Him and spitting on Him. I'll tell you who heard Isaiah 50:7--9; Satan heard Him (see Col. 2:15). Judas, as a man, is found in verse 8---or at least Simon Peter makes a primary application in Acts 1:20---but the rest of the imprecations go far beyond that. You aren't told that Judas ever had any children, or that he even was married. ]{.class_4938}["In the generation following," ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 13), if applied to Judas, would be Acts 2--28, and you couldn't find a case of Judas ]{.class_4938}["cursing" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 18) in Matthew, Mark, Luke, or John if you looked till your eyeballs fell out. It is the Son of Perdition in Revelation 13:6 who curses, and it will be he who persecutes ]{.class_4938}["the poor and needy man" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 16, see comments on Psa. 9:18 and especially 12:5 and 35:10. Psalm 37:14 and 72:4 are the same tune in the same key). The historical Judas of the Gospels is not the only subject of these passages. If he were, the prayer would have been much simpler. It would have been, "Let him commit suicide by hanging himself, " for that is what Judas did. No "extortioner" had time to take anything he had. We may say the Pharisees refused to show him mercy in Matthew 27:4--7, and we may say that after his suicide the ]{.class_4938}["strangers" ]{.class_4939}[spoiled his ]{.class_4938}["labour" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 11). But this smacks of ingenuity and inventiveness.]{.class_4938}

[Judas is not ]{.class_4938}["judged" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 7) in Matthew, Mark, Luke, or John. He will be judged at the White Throne Judgment with Satan at his right hand (Dan. 7:9--11; Rev. 20:11--14), and he will be condemned with Satan (Rev. 20:10). ]{.class_4938}["Therefore your sin remaineth, ]{.class_4939}[" Jesus says to the Pharisees (John 9:41), hence ]{.class_4938}["let not the sin of his mother be blotted out. Let them be before the LORD continually" ]{.class_4939}[(vss. 14--15). The only place sin remains before the Lord ]{.class_4938}["continually" ]{.class_4939}[is in the LAKE OF FIRE (Isa. 66:24). ]{.class_4938}["Let his children\...let his children\...his fatherless children" ]{.class_4939}[(vss. 9--10, 12) hits the apostate Conservatives and Fundamentalists like a fifteen pound log splitter. Suddenly everyone of them forgets all about the children drowning in ]{.class_4938}[the flood ]{.class_4937}[(Gen. 7), the children burning up in Sodom with the queers (Gen. 19:23--25), the children of Achan ]{.class_4938}[stoned ]{.class_4937}[and burned with him (Josh. 7:24), God allowing all the children in Bethlehem under two years old to be ]{.class_4938}[murdered ]{.class_4937}[(Matt. 2), and God commanding Saul to kill children under a year old (1 Sam. 15:3). Isn't it strange how gentle, delicate, pious, and "kind" these destructive critics get when the FEAR of God, the WRATH of God, or the JUDGMENT of God shows up? Remarkable, eh?]{.class_4938}

[Much of the prayer, here, is aimed not just at Judas but at all Israel, in their rejection of Christ; and it must never be forgotten that this was their own request when they murdered Him (Acts 7:52). ]{.class_4938}["His blood be on us, and on our children" ]{.class_4939}[(Matt. 27:25). How did Kroll, Yates, Dummelow, Bullinger, Andrew Bonar, Joseph Hammond, J. J. Perowne, Franz Delitzsch, John Calvin, Donald Fraser, F. G. Hibbard, and F. H. Dunwell read Numbers 35:33 in belief, without seeing that ]{.class_4938}["the land cannot be cleansed of the blood that is shed therein, but by the blood of him that shed it"]{.class_4939}[? Answer: ]{.class_4938}[they never believed one line in the Book if they could not agree with it or figure it out.]{.class_4937}

[Cain, the first outstanding type of the Son of Perdition (see 1 John 3:12 and 2 Cor. 11:2--3), is sentenced to be ]{.class_4938}["a vagabond"]{.class_4939}[ (Gen. 4:12). That is exactly what shows up here, at verse 10. Is this prayer much worse than the one recorded in Romans 11:8--9 under "the covenant of Grace" (to cite the "Holy Joes")? David---quoted nearly thirty years after the Resurrection of the "suffering Saviour"---is praying for the damnation of every Christ rejector in his own nation. No wonder Jerry Falwell and his staff have such a time with the Psalm!]{.class_4938}

Grace is not for those who reject grace.

Mercy is not for those who reject mercy.

Forgiveness doesn't apply to any man who doesn't need, or want, forgiveness.

[If a sinner doesn't need mercy, why ask for it? If God is merciful to everyone, regardless of their attitude towards His Righteousness, what is the point in praying ]{.class_4938}["God be merciful to me a sinner" ]{.class_4939}[(Luke 18:13)? Why, there wouldn't be any. The man described in this Psalm is not a vicious, depraved, degenerate "hell cat" who sees his need of salvation and cries out to God for it (as we just saw in Ps. 109:6, 13, 19, and 28). No, this man is a proud, arrogant, cruel blasphemer who has no intention of seeking mercy or grace, or asking for either. This is the one whose heart is as hard as a ]{.class_4938}["millstone" ]{.class_4939}[(Job 41:24), and who is ]{.class_4938}["made without fear" ]{.class_4939}[(Job 41:33). Note the context is ]{.class_4938}["leviathan" ]{.class_4939}[(Job 41:1, whom Kroll and his buddies shrunk down to a "crocodile" with the approval of the translators of the ]{.class_4938}[ASV, NASV, RV, RSV, NRSV, ]{.class_4937}[and ]{.class_4938}[NIV]{.class_4937}[).]{.class_4938}

The man in the spotlight of Psalm 109:

1\. Loved money (vss. 8--9).

2\. Loved to steal (John 12:6).

3\. Rejected His own Messiah (vs. 2).

4\. Showed no mercy to poor people (vs. 16).

5\. Killed people whose hearts were already broken (vs. 16).

6\. Loved cursing (vs. 18).

7\. Swore falsely on a covenant and broke it (Dan. 9:27).

8\. Made a covenant with death and hell (Isa. 28:15; Rev. 6:8).

9\. Committed sacrilege by letting people think he was God (2 Thess. 2:2--6), and then killed anyone who wouldn't worship him.

Now tell me that Psalm 109 couldn't have been prayed by Jesus Christ! Go on, tell me! The man in question is a combination Pope John Paul II Adolph Hitler Torquemada Pope Leo IX Jim Jones Eddie Murphy Saddam Hussein Judge Jeffries-Adolph Eichmann, and Vito Genovese. But that isn't the worst of it. The worst of it is, he has never had any intention of getting right in six thousand years of recorded history.

The imprecations in this prayer are answered if the recipient winds up in hell, and this is where Judas and Satan will wind up. Read Lamentations carefully in line with this, and notice too Job 30:18 with Psalm 109:18--19. Job 30:30 describes the condition exactly; also the last four verses in Ezekiel 20.

[Paul tells the New Testament Christian "under the covenant of grace" etc., to take a Christian and ]{.class_4938}["deliver such an one unto Satan for the destruction of the flesh" ]{.class_4939}[(1 Cor. 5:5). What does this mean? Just kick him out of the church? Is that ]{.class_4938}[all ]{.class_4937}[it means? ]{.class_4938}["Therefore put away from among yourselves that wicked person" ]{.class_4939}[(1 Cor. 5:13). It is true that the man thus treated is later restored (2 Cor. 2:6--7), but he is restored after being ]{.class_4938}["swallowed up with overmuch sorrow." ]{.class_4939}[This is ]{.class_4938}[not ]{.class_4937}[the case here (Ps. 109) at all. Judas' sorrow was ]{.class_4938}["the sorrow of the world" ]{.class_4939}[(2 Cor. 7:10) which ]{.class_4938}["worketh death." ]{.class_4939}[He does not go to Calvary to confess or to get forgiveness, nor does he plead to God for mercy. He confesses as Pharaoh confessed (cf. Exod. 9:27; Matt. 27:4) and as Balaam confessed (Num. 22:34). These men are connected with ]{.class_4938}[Satan ]{.class_4937}[(Ezek. 29:3; Jude 11; John 17:12).]{.class_4938}

Christ couldn't have prayed the prayer, did they say?

[They covered up for the Devil AGAIN. It is absolutely ]{.class_4938}[habitual. ]{.class_4937}[It is an "historic position."]{.class_4938}

[Have any of you asked God to bless Adolph Hitler's relatives? He had some living as late as 1970. Did any of you pray for Josef Goebbels' children? He killed all of them. Any loss to you? Would you really want Rock Hudson's estate to "prosper" after his decease, so some more perverts could spread AIDS? If a man loves to steal money, persecute poor people, kill people who don't worship him, and would sell Jesus Christ out for \$47 in American money (1970), would you want God to answer his prayers (vs. 7)? If the universal law of God and nature decrees that a man reaps what he sows, what is wrong with verse 17 in either Testament? If he was in jail for murder (like Charles Manson) and praying to escape from the "slammer," would you pray with him? What is wrong with an extortioner taking all that a man has if that man is a THIEF (look at John 12:6)? What makes you think the children of such a man would be unlike him? Joseph was afraid to go back into Judea because Herod's son was on the throne (Matt. 2:22). Was this "prejudice" or rather precaution? The reward of the Lord's adversaries---not David's (vs. 20)---is to be verses 6--19. David was no more speaking for himself in these passages than he was in Psalm 22:16, 110:1, or 16:10; ]{.class_4938}[nor is there one scriptural reason for looking at Psalm 109 in any other fashion. ]{.class_4937}[The reason the commentators have taken the "soft line" is because they have sold Jesus Christ out and deserted Him when He spoke in a manner in which THEY would not speak. In short, it got too hot, so they got out of the kitchen. Their comments do not betoken piety, fidelity, rationalism, sound exegesis, or faithfulness to Scripture. They betoken cowardice, infidelity, lack of understanding, and ]{.class_4938}[betrayal of a sacred trust. ]{.class_4937}[They maintained their holy and sanctified reputations at the Lord's expense; that is the long and short of it.]{.class_4938}

109:21 "But do thou for me, O GOD the Lord, for thy name's sake: because thy mercy is good, deliver thou me.

22 For I am poor and needy, and my heart is wounded within me.

23 I am gone like the shadow when it declineth: I am tossed up and down as the locust.

24 My knees are weak through fasting; and my flesh faileth of fatness.

25 I became also a reproach unto them: when they looked upon me they shaked their heads.

26 Help me, O LORD my God: O save me according to thy mercy:

27 That they may know that this is thy hand; that thou, LORD, hast done it.

28 Let them curse, but bless thou: when they arise, let them be ashamed; but let thy servant rejoice.

29 Let mine adversaries be clothed with shame, and let them cover themselves with their own confusion, as with a mantle.

30 I will greatly praise the LORD with my mouth; yea, I will praise him among the multitude.

31 For he shall stand at the right hand of the poor, to save him from those that condemn his soul."

[Our Lord's adversaries will eventually be clothed not only in filthy rags (Isa. 64:6) but in ]{.class_4938}[cursing]{.class_4937}[ (vs. 18),]{.class_4938}[ shame, ]{.class_4937}[and ]{.class_4938}[confusion ]{.class_4937}[(vs. 29). See Daniel 12:2.]{.class_4938}

[Note the close similarity between ]{.class_4938}["I will praise him among the multitude" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 30) and ]{.class_4938}["In the midst of the congregation will I praise thee" ]{.class_4939}[(Ps. 22:22). This striking corollary is deadly to Spurgeon and his crew, for every man jack of them had placed Psalm 22:22 on ]{.class_4938}[Jesus Christ. ]{.class_4937}[The context of Psalm 22:22 was not just the "suffering Saviour"; it was Jesus Christ actually hanging on the cross. John Calvin crawfishes out at fifty miles an hour. He writes eleven long lines on verse 30, and not only refuses to mention the name of Jesus Christ, but does not even give the ]{.class_4938}[cross reference. ]{.class_4937}[Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown give the cross reference (Ps. 22:22) but can't comment, and Spurgeon does the same thing, except he only runs ten long lines.]{.class_4938}

[The final clause of the Psalm can be David's, certainly, for it is a comment on God as the advocate of the poor and needy instead of Satan. Satan is on the right hand of the ]{.class_4938}["wicked" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 6), and God is on the right hand of the ]{.class_4938}["poor" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 31). Note again the Tribulation context of the ]{.class_4938}["poor." ]{.class_4939}[In the case of Joshua in Zechariah 3:1--3, Satan showed up hoping to "take the prisoner, " as he certainly took Judas in Psalm 109:6. However, in Zechariah the "attorney for the defense" shows up and overrules the prosecuting attorney. This is the ]{.class_4938}["daysman" ]{.class_4939}[of Job 9:33 and the]{.class_4938}[ "advocate"]{.class_4939}[ 1 John 2:1. The one who would condemn our souls (vs. 31) is Satan, and it is for our benefit that the Holy Spirit recorded Romans 8:34 in the New Testament. When Balak is called to curse the chosen people, God turns the curse into a blessing (Deut. 23:5) by declaring that He had]{.class_4938}[ "not beheld iniquity in Jacob, neither hath he seen perverseness in Israel" ]{.class_4939}[(Num. 23:21). When you consider it, this is one of the most remarkable statements in the entire canon of Scripture. God made that statement after killing thousands of Israelites for lusting, complaining, doubting, and murmuring; and on one occasion He threatened to wipe out the entire nation (see Exod. 32:9--10). But when faced with the Adversary (see Deut. 32:27 and Prov. 27:11), the Lord says His people are ]{.class_4938}[sinless.]{.class_4937}[ You talk about "grace"! ]{.class_4938}["As he is, so are we in this world" ]{.class_4939}[(1 John 4:17).]{.class_4938}

Verse 27 is plain. Anyone who ever heard the Gospel knows that Christ's suffering and death were planned by God (Acts 2:23) and carried out according to His will (Isa. 53:10); but further, that God raised Him from the dead (Rom. 1:4), "saved" Him from death (vs. 26), and "helped" Him (Phil. 2:9--11).

[There may be some application in parts of this Psalm to David's personal life and problems, but the "primary emphasis"---to cite the misguided Conservative and Fundamental scholars---is ]{.class_4938}[not ]{.class_4937}[on David's history; it is on the Messiah's humiliation, confrontation with Satan, victory over death, and the vindication of His life by the vengeance of Almighty God (see Deut. 32:21--24). The Psalm is His prayer beyond the shadow of a doubt, and it will be answered to the letter, beyond the shadow of a doubt. If 100 percent of the "good, godly men" didn't subscribe to that interpretation, it would be about as significant as a "talk show" by Ted Kennedy and Jesse Jackson.]{.class_4938}

[]{#Uxd5JyNYSWCtgFM2T9UhJQ2609.xhtml}

PSALM 110

110:1 "The LORD said unto my Lord, Sit thou at my right hand, until I make thine enemies thy footstool.

2 The LORD shall send the rod of thy strength out of Zion: rule thou in the midst of thine enemies.

3 Thy people shall be willing in the day of thy power, in the beauties of holiness from the womb of the morning: thou hast the dew of thy youth.

4 The LORD hath sworn, and will not repent, Thou art a priest for ever after the order of Melchizedek.

5 The Lord at thy right hand shall strike through kings in the day of his wrath.

6 He shall judge among the heathen, he shall fill the places with the dead bodies; he shall wound the heads over many countries.

7 He shall drink of the brook in the way: therefore shall he lift up the head."

[This Psalm receives "rave notices" from all the critics---and they certainly ]{.class_4938}[are ]{.class_4937}[critics of the Holy Bible. The reason for this is that ]{.class_4938}[two verses ]{.class_4937}[from it are quoted in the New Testament. In the childish minds of people like the critics, when they can recognize and locate a thing (and thereby be sure in their footing), that particular thing has to be something "super." Psalm 110 has no more material in it that Psalm 2 or Psalm 45, and not ]{.class_4938}[as much in it ]{.class_4937}[as Psalm 68 or Psalm 89, but the Scholar's Union can handle it, so they elevate it to a unique position. Spurgeon says, "he that reads with understanding will see little enough of David, here, except as writer. He is not the subject of it even in the smallest degree, but Christ is all." Isn't that remarkable after throwing Jesus Christ out of ]{.class_4938}[nineteen verses in the previous Psalm? ]{.class_4937}[There is "little enough of David" in Psalm 109, and it is so "little enough" that the expositors used "some man" and "a man" and "like us" and "we who are" to explain what went on in verses 1--5 and verses 21--31.]{.class_4938}

"[The Psalm has been well designated the crown of all the Psalms" (Edersheim, 1873). Not necessarily; not in view of Psalm 2, 22, 24, and 51. "The ancients term this Psalm 'the sum of our faith,' 'the treasure of holy writ'" (John Prideaux, 1636). "This Psalm is one of the fullest and most compendious prophecies of the person and offices of Christ in the whole Old Testament\...I call\...it Symbolum Davidicum, the prophet's creed." ]{.class_4938}[Why could that be the Davidic creed when no "son" is mentioned and the "Son" is mentioned in Psalm 2? ]{.class_4937}[Edward Reynolds (1599--1676)---who wrote what we just quoted above---makes this statement because in the Psalm he says be has found: the doctrine of the Trinity, the Incarnation of Christ, the Sufferings of Christ, His completed work, His Ascension and Intercession, THE HOLY CATHOLIC CHURCH gathered together "by the scepter of His kingdom," the Last Judgment, the Resurrection of the body, and "life everlasting."]{.class_4938}

Ed read the Catholic "Apostles' Creed" of the Anglican church back into the Psalm.

[The Trinity is missing the]{.class_4938}[ Holy Spirit ]{.class_4937}[in verse 1. The Incarnation is mentioned ]{.class_4938}[nowhere ]{.class_4937}[in the Psalm, but has to be inferred by reading the New Testament. The sufferings of Christ are ]{.class_4938}[not ]{.class_4937}[in evidence enough in the Psalm to preach a ten minute message. His "completed" work omits ANY and ALL references to His finished blood atonement on the cross for sinners (which is in Ps. 22). There isn't a "holy Catholic church" within 24,000 miles of the Psalm in either direction. There is no "Last Judgment" mentioned ]{.class_4938}[anywhere ]{.class_4937}[in the Psalm (Reynolds was postmillennial). And there is no physical resurrection mentioned ]{.class_4938}[anywhere ]{.class_4937}[in the Psalm, nor is "everlasting life" for a saved sinner in evidence one time in seven verses. Reynolds was just strutting his knowledge of ]{.class_4938}[two verses ]{.class_4937}[anyone could find in the New Testament. Ditto Augustine.]{.class_4938}

"[The whole Psalm is an epitome of the Gospel" (John Trapp). Well, not the Gospel that got Trapp saved, and not the Gospel that got the Ethiopian eunuch saved, and certainly not the Gospel that got Paul saved, or Ignatius, Polycarp, Chrysostom, Martin Luther, John Knox, George Whitefield, or anyone else. "The Gospel" is defined in 1 Corinthians 15:3--4, so if John Trapp opened his trap to preach Psalm 110 as "The Gospel," God cursed him (Gal. 1:8).]{.class_4938}

[The truth of the matter is that the older expositors (1500--1800) who gloried in their flesh---their understanding of Hebrew and Greek---and gloried in man's progress (Postmillennial) could easily go into raptures about any passage they ]{.class_4938}[understood, ]{.class_4937}[and who couldn't understand Psalm 110:1, 4 after reading the New Testament? Where they could NOT understand the Book (say Ps. 19, 45, 68, 89, 74, 109, 121, 122, 124, etc.), they simply changed the text or allegorized it out of existence. You have seen this demonstrated now more than once per Psalm; that would be 109 times. I say this not to condemn someone in 1500 or 1600 who didn't have "all the light we have today on the Scripture," because today, the same crew is turning out Scriptural "light" as fast as it is turned on. "The light on the Scriptures" that God gave since 1500 and 1600 has come---as it always has come---by believing what God said as He said it, where He said it. It has NEVER come---and never will come---by messing with Hebrew and Greek, trying to prove that a formal education makes one superior (Nicolaitanism) to the "laymen" in the Body of Christ.]{.class_4938}

We will not have to strain to prove the truth of these matters. We will get a great deal more out of Psalm 110 than even the critics say it contains. Further, as we go, we will show how "advanced light," after 1800 and 1900, doesn't produce anything in the Alexandrian Cult but stupefied nonsense.

[Verse 1. The verse is quoted six times in the New Testament. But as far as that goes, Isaiah 66:24 (which is negative! Look out!) is quoted by Jesus three times in one gospel. Psalm 110:1 is not quoted twice in any Gospel. To alleviate this intolerable situation, the gentlemen with "more light on the Scripture than they had in 1500, 1600, and 1700" erase two of the quotations (Mark 9:44--48). Check it out in the ]{.class_4938}[RV, ASV, NASV, RSV, NRSV, ]{.class_4937}[and ]{.class_4938}[NIV.]{.class_4937}

[Of course, the verse is a great verse, for it is THE prooftext for the Deity of Christ (]{.class_4938}[not ]{.class_4937}[the "Trinity"). When the Lord pulls it on His "cross examiners" (Matt. 22:44), they cannot "give a reason for the hope that is within them," for no Jewish father would think of calling his own son "Lord." This would be unthinkable in nearly any "social milieu" (NEA cliché for fornicators, dope addicts, jungle bunnies, sex perverts, and abortionists), but in a Shemitic home it is really "spaced out." Children honor and reverence their parents in Oriental homes. When Christ asks how the "Son of David" could be the Messiah, He really puts the "qualified, recognized, accredited Fundamentalists" of His day in a bind. Why would David call his own son "LORD"? David says that his ]{.class_4938}["Lord" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 1) was his son---if "Christ" (Matt. 22:44) was David's son. There is only one answer to this question, and no Christ rejecting Pharisee would give the answer (\$\$\$). The answer is that the Son of David was God ]{.class_4938}["manifest in the flesh" ]{.class_4939}[(1 Tim. 3:16 ).]{.class_4938}

"[Sit thou at my right hand, until\...."]{.class_4939}[ Off goes Spurgeon into the "wild blue yonder." Christ is reigning now, His enemies are being defeated now, and it is "through the church" that this reigning power is operating. We should "pray for the sending out of the rod of divine strength," by getting the heathen saved (p. 461). In a mighty surge of evangelistic rubbish, completely unaware of his unbelief and near blasphemy, Spurgeon cancels ]{.class_4938}[ALL of ]{.class_4937}[the words in all ]{.class_4938}[of ]{.class_4937}[the verses in Isaiah 63, Psalm 68, Revelation 14, and Isaiah 41 and tells us that Jesus Christ will never rise to tread anyone ]{.class_4938}[down. ]{.class_4937}[He will just sit while all of us good, "godly," spiritual folks "bring in the Kingdom." Nuts. N U T S, nuts.]{.class_4938}

[Swept away with his desire to be a celebrity in the Scholar's Union, Kroll (Liberty University), says "The meaning of the language is explained in Joshua 10:24. Having completed His atoning work, Jesus Christ ]{.class_4938}['must reign, till he hath put all things under his feet']{.class_4939}[ (1 Cor. 15:25)." [1. The "full explanation" of the clause takes ]{.class_4938}[fifty chapters ]{.class_4937}[to explain (Gen. 49; Deut. 32; Judg. 5, 7; Exod. 15; Josh. 6--7, 10; Psa. 2, 68; Isa. 65--66; Joel 2; Zeph. 3; Zech. 14; Matt. 24; Rev. 6--19; and some more). In Revelation 3:21, Christ's enemies are NOT under His feet, and He has not stepped on them yet.]{.class_4938}

[2. Christ's "reign," in the sense of Psalm 110:2--3, 5--6, did ]{.class_4938}[not ]{.class_4937}[begin in ANY fashion, in ANY way, or ANY form when Christ "completed" His atoning work.]{.class_4938}

[3. The reference Kroll cited (1 Cor. 15:25) is not remotely connected with ]{.class_4938}[anything ]{.class_4937}[that happened immediately after Christ's death, burial, resurrection, and ascension. First Corinthians 15:25 is a verse that comes into effect AFTER the "first resurrection," and the whole chapter (1 Cor. 15) was on that resurrection.]{.class_4938}

[This is "Fundamental Baptist scholarship" one hundred years after Charles Haddon Spurgeon. "More light" did nothing but make the blindness more dense and more complete. [Kroll (supposedly Premillennial) is just as fouled up as a Chinese fire drill when it comes to understanding the Scripture. But the ]{.class_4938}["footstool" ]{.class_4939}[of this passage is the HEADS of people. Joshua 10:24 was the right verse for that, but Kroll spiritualized it to mean the Church Age. ]{.class_4938}["The rod of thy strength out of Zion" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 2 is Ps. 2 again, with full knowledge confirming that Jerusalem]{.class_4938}[ "is]{.class_4939}[ ]{.class_4938}[the city of the great king," ]{.class_4939}[Matt. 5:35). [The ]{.class_4938}["enemies" ]{.class_4939}[are on earth during the Millennium and are said to be so in THREE places in Scripture; Liberty University could no more find ]{.class_4938}[one of them ]{.class_4937}[than any expositor in 1500, 1600, 1700, 1800, or 1900. Kroll got his screwed up mess from Spurgeon (]{.class_4938}[Treasury,]{.class_4937}[ p.]{.class_4938}[ ]{.class_4937}[461). Both of these men, yielding to their carnal natures and trying to show off their spirituality to their contemporaries, have Christ "conquering" by "love and grace" (Kroll, ]{.class_4938}[Liberty Commentary,]{.class_4937}[ p. 1135). Kroll knows he is compromising because he quotes Revelation 19:15 as the ]{.class_4938}[end ]{.class_4937}[of the present "reign." Wrong; it is the ]{.class_4938}[beginning ]{.class_4937}[of His reign. When He said, ]{.class_4938}["All power is given unto me in heaven and in earth" ]{.class_4939}[(Matt. 28:18), He did not mean "I am now the Great King, sitting down on the Throne of David at Jerusalem, to reign in the midst of my enemies from Mt. Zion." That is what is going to take place at the Second Advent. The problem was ]{.class_4938}[unbelief, ]{.class_4937}[as usual. Kroll had a false conception of the Millennium and the Church Age. He forgot Isaiah 26:10, Zechariah 14:16--19, and Revelation 20:7--8. [The context of the passage, in Isaiah 26, was the willingness of the Jewish people to worship the King in the day of His power (vs. 3). Look at Isaiah 26:5--6. God's people Israel (]{.class_4938}["thy people" ]{.class_4939}[in Ps. 110:3) say: ]{.class_4938}["In the way of thy judgments, O LORD, have we waited for thee; the desire of our soul is to thy name\...With my soul have I desired thee in the night\...with my spirit within me will I seek thee early: for\...thy judgments are in the earth" ]{.class_4939}[(Isa. 26:8--9). Imagine a ]{.class_4938}[premillennial ]{.class_4937}[Baptist, at a Baptist university in America, discarding that God given key to Psalm 110:3 in favor of a postmillennial conjecture by a nineteenth-century Bible corrector! Imagine that, with ]{.class_4938}["the morning" ]{.class_4939}[given in verse 3, and the morning ]{.class_4938}[identified ]{.class_4937}[as the end of the Tribulation and the beginning of the Millennium: Malachi 4:1--5; Matthew 13:43; Judges 5:31; 2 Samuel 23:4; Exodus 19:16; Job 11:17; Psalm 49:14; Isaiah 21:12, 58:8; and Hosea 6:3. Impossible, absolutely ]{.class_4938}[impossible. ]{.class_4937}[But with the Scholar's Union ]{.class_4938}["all things are possible."]{.class_4939}

[Here is a Bible correcting, Bible rejecting Fundamentalist (who believes in the "plenary, verbal inspiration of the infallible, inspired, original autographs") who thinks that the dew falling on the grass "fresh every morning" is being fulfilled in this age by "the message of God's redeeming grace" (Kroll, p. 1136). ]{.class_4938}[He is just as nutty as a fruitcake. ]{.class_4937}[If he had gotten the locks of his head wet ]{.class_4938}["with the drops of the night" ]{.class_4939}[(Song of Sol. 5:2) he should have seen Isaiah 18:4 and 26:19 where the ]{.class_4938}["dew"]{.class_4939}[ is the end of the Tribulation. Hosea 14:5 has the ]{.class_4938}["dew" ]{.class_4939}[in ]{.class_4938}["the morning," ]{.class_4939}[and so does Micah (Micah 5:7), and so does Zechariah (Zech. 8:12). [Well yes, if the name of your surgeon is "Mack the Knife" or "Jack the Ripper." The truth is you didn't even get ]{.class_4938}[into ]{.class_4937}[the Psalm with three tries (vss. 1--3). Like Hermann Witsius, back in 1700, you converted the chosen people to Church Age Christians (]{.class_4938}[Treasury]{.class_4937}[,]{.class_4938}[, ]{.class_4937}[p. 469). Like Edward Reynolds, you thought that ]{.class_4938}["thy people"]{.class_4939}[ were Christians who "set up" the "ensign of the gospel." And like Joshua Arnd, back in 1680, you thought that Christ had His enemies under His feet right then, and has had them under His feet for the last nineteen centuries. In short, ]{.class_4938}[you didn't learn one true thing about the Psalm in three hundred years]{.class_4937}[ (1680--1980).]{.class_4938}

[Verse 3 says that in the day of Christ's ]{.class_4938}["power"]{.class_4939}[---when He lands on earth as King of Kings and Lord of Lords---the Jews will be willing to accept Him as Pilate originally "wrote Him up": ]{.class_4938}["THIS IS JESUS, THE KING OF THE JEWS." ]{.class_4939}[Verse 3 says that the Jew will be willing "in the beauties of holiness" because there will be a Holy King (1) on a holy hill (2) with holy standards (3) demanding holiness. (Read Matt. 5--7; Isa. 2; and Zech. 14:20--21 and tell me that Kroll, Yates, Motyer, Clarke, Lange, Delitzsch, Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown had a brain in their head.) It was ]{.class_4938}["beauties"]{.class_4939}[ (plural).]{.class_4938}

[It will be ]{.class_4938}["the womb of the morning" ]{.class_4939}[when the Lord returns, because a birth is connected with that morning in the same verse with ]{.class_4938}["thy people"]{.class_4939}[ (Isa. 26:17--19). All of the commentators missed all the references and all the applications; ]{.class_4938}[the apostasy was one hundred percent. ]{.class_4937}[Richard Dixon speaks for the whole crew of Alexandrian Laodiceans when he says that the ]{.class_4938}["dew"]{.class_4939}[ of verse 3 is a reference to "gospel preachers." John Calvin says the ]{.class_4938}["dew"]{.class_4939}[ are Christians in this age. One dingbat per second.]{.class_4938}

Caleb has the "dew of his youth" when he is eighty five years old (see Josh. 14:10--11).]{.class_4938}

[Five blunders ]{.class_4937}[(in four verses) in a Psalm that is quoted six times in the New Testament. "All hail the power of Fundamental scholarship, let the ]{.class_4938}[AV ]{.class_4937}[prostrate fall! Bring forth the original autographs and crown scholars lord of ALL!" ]{.class_4938}["Professing themselves to be wise, they became fools" ]{.class_4939}[(Rom. 1:22).]{.class_4938}

Of course we can make spiritual applications. As we have said before: holiness is "beautiful" because it pleases God, it enables God to manifest Himself, it maintains fellowship with God, it shows what a man is capable of going through with God and through God. But there is not much "devotional" about verses 1 and 2. These are Second Advent doctrinal truths.

[Verse 4 is explained as far as it can be explained in Hebrews (which see in that commentary). David has a right to talk about Melchizedek because David steps into the priest's office twice without suffering for it (1 Sam. 21:5--6; 2 Sam. 24:24--25). Saul was sacked the first time he made a move in that direction (1 Sam. 13:9--14). As David is from the tribe of Judah, Melchizedek was "king" of the capital of Judah---Jerusalem (see ]{.class_4938}[The Bible Believer's Commentary on Hebrews: ]{.class_4937}[Heb. 7:1--4). This "priest king" is found in three men in the Old Testament: Noah, Moses, and David. Don't ever doubt that Moses was a king (Deut. 33:5) or that Noah was a king. Noah was king over the largest kingdom any human being ever ruled, or ever will rule, until the Second Advent. The prophet king is found in Joseph, Saul, and Joshua. The prophet priest is found in Samuel, Aaron, and Ezra; but the complete combination (prophet-priest-king) is only found in Noah, Moses, and David. They are rulers, they prophesy, and they offer up sacrifices acceptable to God (see 2 Sam. 24:25). Melchizedek is also a prophet priest king. He is a type of Jesus Christ, the Prophet (4 B. C.--A.D. 33), the Priest (A.D. 33--2002plus), and the King (2002plus). The bungling commentators (on the three preceding verses) confused His offices and got Him on the throne while He was sitting on the right hand of the throne as a priest. Typical; absolutely standard.]{.class_4938}

[Verse 5 is a reference to Joel 2, Revelation 19, etc. Verse 6 is a literal judgment of literal heathen (as in Joel 3; Matt. 25; and Ps. 5, 15, 19). There are at least ten kings that are identified (see Rev. 17:12). These are the ]{.class_4938}["kings" ]{.class_4939}[of Judges 5:19, which see. Verse 6 is clear; there will be enough dead bodies on this earth at the Advent to fill 20,000 football stadiums. Two hundred million bodies hit the dirt at Armageddon, but before that, one billion people were killed in World War III, and another billion were killed by demonic monsters (Rev. 6:7--8). This would still exclude those killed in the earthquakes, tidal waves, hail storms, and fire storms that take place in the Great Tribulation. ]{.class_4938}["He shall fill the places with the dead bodies" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 6). The wounded ]{.class_4938}["heads" ]{.class_4939}[we have discussed before (see Ps. 7:16 with Gen. 3:15; Rom. 16:20; and Hab. 3:13).]{.class_4938}

[Verse 7 is the hardest verse in the Psalm to locate ]{.class_4938}[doctrinally.]{.class_4937}[ None of the commentators make any attempt to locate it, and personally, I cannot locate it. But the ]{.class_4938}["dead bodies" ]{.class_4939}[of verse 6, strangely enough, are connected with ]{.class_4938}["the brook of Kidron" ]{.class_4939}[in Jeremiah 31:40. It is over this same ]{.class_4938}["brook"]{.class_4939}[ that Jesus went on His way to His bloody agony in the Garden of Gethsemane (John 18:1). In view of the fact that the site of the judgment of the nations is ]{.class_4938}["the valley of Jehoshaphat" ]{.class_4939}[(Joel 3:2), it would look like ]{.class_4938}["in the way" ]{.class_4939}[meant that the Lord Jesus will stop there on the]{.class_4938}[ "way"]{.class_4939}[ to judging His enemies (Matt. 25:32), and His ]{.class_4938}["sheep."]{.class_4939}[ It is certain that He crosses this brook at the Advent, for He proceeds from the Mount of Olives to Jerusalem via the "Eastern Gate."]{.class_4938}

[The spiritual application is much easier. He will ]{.class_4938}["lift up the head" ]{.class_4939}[in the sense of being refreshed and taking courage (Judg. 15:18--19) after combat, or He will rise up eagerly to combat as Gideon's "three hundred" who drank of the brook, without taking their eyes off the landscape around them (Judg. 7:6).]{.class_4938}

[There is an interesting footnote to all of this, because David, the second greatest type of Christ in the Bible, stopped at a ]{.class_4938}["brook" ]{.class_4939}[(1 Sam. 17:40) just before "rocking" Goliath to sleep. Goliath is a perfect type of the Antichrist, who gets hit in the same place (vs. 6).]{.class_4938}

PSALM 111

111:1 "Praise ye the LORD. I will praise the LORD with my whole heart, in the assembly of the upright, and in the congregation.

2 The works of the LORD are great, sought out of all them that have pleasure therein.

3 His work is honourable and glorious: and his righteousness endureth for ever.

4 He hath made his wonderful works to be remembered: the LORD is gracious and full of compassion.

5 He hath given meat unto them that fear him: he will ever be mindful of his covenant.

6 He hath shewed his people the power of his works, that he may give them the heritage of the heathen.

7 The works of his hands are verity and judgment; all his commandments are sure.

8 They stand fast for ever and ever, and are done in truth and uprightness.

9 He sent redemption unto his people: he hath commanded his covenant for ever: holy and reverend is his name.

10 The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom: a good understanding have all they that do his commandments: his praise endureth for ever."

[This Psalm, and the following one, are what are known as "Alphabet Psalms." Each Psalm has twenty two phrases matching the twenty two letters of the Hebrew alphabet (see Psa. 119); each phrase beginning with the appropriate letter. Psalm 111, 112, and 113 form a "trilogy," each beginning with ]{.class_4938}["Praise ye the Lord." ]{.class_4939}[By stretching things a little, one might say that the FATHER is manifest in Psalm 111 (vss. 5--7), the SON is the subject of Psalm 112, and the work of the HOLY SPIRIT is manifest in Psalm 113 (vss. 5--9), although this last similitude is not very good.]{.class_4938}

There is nothing difficult anywhere in the Psalm. Verses 4--6, and 9 deal with the restoration of Israel at the Tribulation. Spiritual application can be made to just about any of the verses, and verse 10 is one of the key verses in Scripture. You can well guess what the Alexandrians in the Scholar's Union are going to do with it when they get there!

[Verse 1, ]{.class_4938}[doctrinally]{.class_4937}[, is Jesus Christ speaking to the Father (as in Ps. 109:30). God's ]{.class_4938}["works"]{.class_4939}[ in the Psalm are described in nine different ways. They are:]{.class_4938}

1\. Great (vs. 2).

2\. To be sought out (vs. 2).

3\. Wonderful (vs. 4).

5\. Honorable (vs. 3).

6\. Glorious (vs. 3).

7\. True (vs. 7).

8\. Judgmental (vs. 7).

9\. Powerful (vs. 6).

[Note that ]{.class_4938}["lovers of pleasure" ]{.class_4939}[(2 Tim. 3:4) are acceptable as long as the ]{.class_4938}["pleasure"]{.class_4939}[ is found in the works of God instead of ]{.class_4938}["sin for a season" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 2). Our righteousness---in contrast to God's righteousness (vs. 3)---endures only till we die. God ]{.class_4938}["hath made his wonderful works to be remembered" ]{.class_4939}[in that He has recorded them in a Book, He often calls them to mind, He fulfills prophecy regularly, and He calls certain men to repeat a recitation of His works in every generation.]{.class_4938}

Verse 2 is illustrated graphically by the Queen of Sheba.

Verse 3 is demonstrated by Solomon's temple.

Verse 4 is demonstrated in the preaching of Samuel (1 Sam. 12:7--12) and Hosea (Hos. 1--2).

Verse 5 was demonstrated with the manna and quail in the wilderness.

Verse 6 was demonstrated at the Jordan when Joshua took charge of the hosts of Israel (Josh. 3:14--4:5).

[Verse 7 is expounded on to the tune of 176 verses in Psalm 119. Verse 8 is a comment on the same thing (see Ps. 119:89, 160). Not one ]{.class_4938}["jot" ]{.class_4939}[or ]{.class_4938}["tittle" ]{.class_4939}[shall fail from His]{.class_4938}[ "law." "The scripture cannot be broken" ]{.class_4939}[(John 10:35), even though every apostate Fundamentalist in the last one hundred years has been working at it overtime.]{.class_4938}

[Spiritually speaking, ]{.class_4937}[God sent His Son to Israel (Acts 3:26) in verse 9. The two adjectives ]{.class_4938}["holy and reverend" ]{.class_4939}[(vs. 9) are ]{.class_4938}[adjectives]{.class_4937}[---not titles, as wrongly supposed by some of the ultra-separated brethren. They are used as we find other adjectives used: ]{.class_4938}["God is good," "God is love," "great is the Lord," ]{.class_4939}[etc. Personally, I do not like the word used as a ]{.class_4938}[title, ]{.class_4937}[but I am not a fanatic about it. No one has ever called me "Reverend" unless it was some Yankee who did not know me personally. I get called everything ]{.class_4938}[but ]{.class_4937}["Reverend."]{.class_4938}

[I collect (and sometimes print in the ]{.class_4938}[Bible Believers' Bulletin]{.class_4937}[)]{.class_4938}[ ]{.class_4937}[what people call me, and they call me "everything but white" most of the time. Little children call me "Peterupman" or "Peterupkin." (The surname is joined to the first name.) My cleaners have called me "Mr. Rubberman," "Mr. Roughman," and "Mr. Ruckerman." I am "Der Herr Doktor" to the Deutsche, and "Brother Ruckman" to my congregation. I am "Dr. Pete" to my closest associates and "the good Doctor" to saved Lutherans who know something about Martin Luther. I am "Pete" to rude Yankees who have no sense of courtesy or social graces, so I usually answer "in kind" when the Alexandrian Cult starts showing its contempt for my ministry. If they can call a man with an earned doctorate "Pete," when they have never been in his house, never met his family, never sat in his classes, and never attended his church, he has a perfect right to refer to them as "son" or "buster"---which I often do. The educated asses at BJU would rather cut out their tongues than say "Doctor Ruckman," when discussing my nine thousand pages of written material. With them (as their brother dunces at BBC, TTU, and PCC), it is "Pete," "Pete Ruckman," or "Ruckman." We assume that when they took this stance they were familiar with Galatians 6:7--8, so they shouldn't have been upset with "Bobby Wobby" (Bob Jones III), "Stu Baby" (Stewart Custer), "Hot Dog Hymers" (Robert Hymers), and "Curt Baby" (Curtis Hutson). If a man can't take it, he better hadn't try to dish it out.]{.class_4938}

[Verse 10 shows us that no man who begins his education with a positive outlook can ever really learn anything that is SO (2 Tim. 3:7). No evolutionist is capable of real understanding. ]{.class_4938}["Wisdom" ]{.class_4939}[is simply knowing when to be afraid and when not to be afraid, and it has never been anything else since Genesis 1:1. The man who is afraid when he should not be afraid, or who is not afraid when a real danger is present, is a "FOOL," per se. That definition will lasso and roundup every fool that ever breathed. The shrinks call these things "phobias," from the Greek (phobos). The trick in the world system---when I say that, I mean the vast majority of scientists, educators, religious leaders, socialists, humanists, politicians, and philosophers in any age---is simple: teach you to fear what God told you ]{.class_4938}[not ]{.class_4937}[to be afraid of (2 Kings 17:7, 25), and teach you not to fear what God told you to ]{.class_4938}[be afraid of ]{.class_4937}[(Exod. 20:20). That sums up "progress" and "civilization" from Adam to now. The principle is given twice in the Scripture: in Proverbs 29:27 and in Luke 16:15. It is illustrated in the second book in the Bible where no man for the next four thousand years could miss it: ]{.class_4938}["he that feared the word of the Lord ]{.class_4939}[\[and I don't mean "reverential trust" or anything like it\] ]{.class_4938}[made his servants and his cattle flee\...And he that regardeth not the word of the Lord left his servants and his cattle in the field" ]{.class_4939}[(Exod. 9:20--21).]{.class_4938}

That is the history of mankind in thirty four words (see 28:24).

[God told Abraham to ]{.class_4938}["fear not" ]{.class_4939}[(Gen. 15:1), but he was worried about ]{.class_4938}["the fear of God" ]{.class_4939}[not being in a place (Gen. 20:11). Abraham's God was called ]{.class_4938}["the fear of Isaac" ]{.class_4939}[(Gen. 31:42). God said that Abraham FEARED Him (Gen. 22:12). The idea of "reverential trust" is so absent from Exodus 9:30, 15:16, 20:20, and 23:27 that no one could find it with a laser beam and a strobe light. You fear ]{.class_4938}[God ]{.class_4937}[or you fear ]{.class_4938}[man ]{.class_4937}[(Prov. 29:25), and Jesus Christ told you which to fear. You believe Him or you don't. You are a fool if you don't, and you are a ]{.class_4938}[wise man ]{.class_4937}[if you do (Matt. 7:24).]{.class_4938}

"[Learning skills" are not ]{.class_4938}["the beginning of wisdom."]{.class_4939}

"[Discovering meaningful relations" is not ]{.class_4938}["the beginning of wisdom."]{.class_4939}

"[Sharing an enriched curriculum" is not ]{.class_4938}["the beginning of wisdom."]{.class_4939}

"[Finding one's self" is not ]{.class_4938}["the beginning of wisdom."]{.class_4939}

"[Learning how to share and cope with problems" is not ]{.class_4938}["the beginning of wisdom."]{.class_4939}

"[Tolerating the viewpoints of others" is not ]{.class_4938}["the beginning of wisdom."]{.class_4939}

"[Learning how to adapt and adjust to the lifestyles of others" is not ]{.class_4938}["the beginning of wisdom."]{.class_4939}

[Adopting a "global view," as a "world citizen," is not ]{.class_4938}["the beginning of wisdom."]{.class_4939}

[Learning how to "communicate" and "reach out, " so as to "touch others," is not ]{.class_4938}["the beginning of wisdom."]{.class_4939}

[Nothing ]{.class_4937}[the NEA or the world teaches (or has ever taught) is ]{.class_4938}["the beginning of wisdom."]{.class_4939}[ The "hidden wisdom" of the Theosophists, Rosicrucians, and Tibetans is just more blind stumbling in the darkness.]{.class_4938}

"[All the treasures of wisdom and knowledge" ]{.class_4939}[were deposited by God Almighty into ]{.class_4938}[Jesus Christ]{.class_4937}[ (Col. 2:3).]{.class_4938}

"[The fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom."]{.class_4939}

[There are some things worse than war or death, and there are some things worse than sickness, blindness, or cancer. There is a fate more horrible and more lasting than poverty, starvation, disease, or torture; and those who do not fear God know ]{.class_4938}[nothing ]{.class_4937}[about such matters. They all fear the wrong things.]{.class_4938}

It is now time on the "Tonight Show" for a little slapstick comedy: The Alexandrian Cult.

[I. ]{.class_4938}[The Champions in Lynchburg, Virginia: ]{.class_4937}["Fear is NOT to be understood in this passage as referring to the first or elementary principle of piety\...is comprehensive of all true godliness or the worship of God" (citing Calvin). Sorry, old chap, it is the bedrock, tap root, first, basic, elemental principle for human life on this planet.]{.class_4938}

[II. ]{.class_4938}["The fear of the Lord" ]{.class_4939}[is childlike reverential fear\...this fear calls forth love and delight in His commandments" (Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown). It also causes trembling (Phil. 2:12), quaking (Heb. 12:21), panic (Jonah 3:5--8), and terror (Exod. 20:1--20).]{.class_4938}

[III]{.class_4938}[. Spurgeon: ]{.class_4937}["Holy reverence of God leads us to praise Him\...it is a wise act on the part of the creature towards his Creator."]{.class_4938}

[IV. ]{.class_4938}[Samuel Davies: ]{.class_4937}["That pious passion or filial reverence of our adorable Father who is in heaven\...the fear of the Lord implies all the graces and all the virtues of Christianity."]{.class_4938}

V. "The fear of God\...it is surely wisdom to LOVE that which is not loveable and to occupy our hearts with that which is most worthy of attachment and most capable of satisfying us."

Ain't that a beaut? That is Spurgeon citing Daniel De Sluperville (1700).

[VI. When Fausset hit ]{.class_4938}["fear" ]{.class_4939}[and ]{.class_4938}["trembling" ]{.class_4939}[in Psalm 2:11, he left the word ]{.class_4938}["fear" ]{.class_4939}[out of the text and wrote, "serve the Lord with ]{.class_4938}[rejoicing."]{.class_4937}

[VII. ]{.class_4938}[Dummelow ]{.class_4937}[says that "religion" is the fear of the Lord. "The meaning is that RELIGION is the foundation of all wisdom." "The Voice of the Mule" (Byron Nelson, ]{.class_4938}[After Its Kind]{.class_4937}[).]{.class_4938}

(See comments under Psalm 107:11).

[The wisdom of this world is foolishness with God, for it begins on the ]{.class_4938}[wrong premise ]{.class_4937}[(1 Cor. 1--3). The wisdom of Plato, Socrates, Aristotle, Marx, Darwin, Einstein, Glasser, Bertrand Russell, Tillich, Niebhur, Gandhi, Martin L. King Jr., Hegel, Spinoza, Sartre, Descartes, Kant, Schopenhauer, and Freud is a JOKE. Don't ever take one of them seriously. According to Psalm 111:10, they were fools from the cradle to the grave, and so were the fools who followed them. (See lengthy documentation in ]{.class_4938}[The Christian's Handbook of Science and Philosophy, ]{.class_4937}[1989, and ]{.class_4938}[The Anti Intellectual Manifesto, ]{.class_4937}[1992.)]{.class_4938}
